Their analysis identifies key findings regarding fundamental trade-offs inherent in each algorithmic family, particularly concerning sample efficiency and scalability, reveals critical computational and algorithmic bottlenecks that limit practical deployment, and provides concrete guidelines for algorithm selection and future research directions.
notes: Survey

title: Xiaomi-Robotics-0: Open-Sourced Vision-Language-Action Model with Real-Time Execution
authors: not specified
headline: Open-source VLA model runs in real-time for generalist robot control
summary: Open-source VLA model with real-time execution for generalist robot control.
abstract: Xiaomi-Robotics-0 is an advanced vision-language-action (VLA) model optimized for high performance and fast and smooth real-time execution. The key to the method lies in a carefully designed training recipe and deployment strategy. Xiaomi-Robotics-0 is first pre-trained on large-scale cross-embodiment robot trajectories and vision-language data, endowing it with broad and generalizable action-generation capabilities while avoiding catastrophic forgetting of the visual-semantic knowledge of the underlying pre-trained VLM. During post-training, several techniques are proposed for training the VLA model for asynchronous execution to address the inference latency during real-robot rollouts. The model is evaluated extensively in simulation benchmarks and on two challenging real-robot tasks that require precise and dexterous bimanual manipulation. Results show that the method achieves state-of-the-art performance across all simulation benchmarks. Moreover, Xiaomi-Robotics-0 can roll out fast and smoothly on real robots using a consumer-grade GPU, achieving high success rates and throughput on both real-robot tasks.

title: OmniGuide: Universal Guidance Fields for Enhancing Generalist Robot Policies
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Generalist policies|VLA reasoning and chain-of-thought
headline: Universal guidance fields enhance any generalist robot policy
summary: Framework enhancing generalist policies with universal guidance fields.
abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown great promise as generalist policies for a large range of relatively simple tasks. However, they demonstrate limited performance on more complex tasks, such as those requiring complex spatial or semantic understanding, manipulation in clutter, or precise manipulation. The paper proposes OMNIGUIDE, a flexible framework that improves VLA performance on such tasks by leveraging arbitrary sources of guidance, such as 3D foundation models, semantic-reasoning VLMs, and human pose models. The researchers show how many kinds of guidance can be naturally expressed as differentiable energy functions with task-specific attractors and repellers located in 3D space, that influence the sampling of VLA actions. OmniGuide consistently improves the base VLA models across different metrics, including increasing success rates from 24.2% to 92.4% and collision avoidance rates from 7.0% to 93.5%, all without incurring significant execution latencies or requiring retraining.

title: Cosmos Policy: Fine-Tuning Video Models for Visuomotor Control and Planning
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Reinforcement learning|Diffusion models|Imitation learning|World models for robotics|Verification of visuomotor policies
headline: Large video models become effective robot policies via single-stage tuning
summary: Adapts large pretrained video models into effective robot policies through single-stage post-training.
abstract: Recent video generation models demonstrate remarkable ability to capture complex physical interactions and scene evolution over time. To leverage their spatiotemporal priors, robotics works have adapted video models for policy learning but introduce complexity by requiring multiple stages of post-training and new architectural components for action generation. In this work, we introduce Cosmos Policy, a simple approach for adapting a large pretrained video model (Cosmos-Predict2) into an effective robot policy through a single stage of post-training on the robot demonstration data collected on the target platform, with no architectural modifications. Cosmos Policy learns to directly generate robot actions encoded as latent frames within the video model's latent diffusion process, harnessing the model's pretrained priors and core learning algorithm to capture complex action distributions. Additionally, Cosmos Policy generates future state images and values (expected cumulative rewards), which are similarly encoded as latent frames, enabling test-time planning of action trajectories with higher likelihood of success. Cosmos Policy achieves state-of-the-art performance on the LIBERO and RoboCasa simulation benchmarks (98.5% and 67.1% average success rates, respectively) and the highest average score in challenging real-world bimanual manipulation tasks, outperforming strong diffusion policies trained from scratch, video model-based policies, and state-of-the-art vision-language-action models fine-tuned on the same robot demonstrations. Furthermore, given policy rollout data, Cosmos Policy can learn from experience to refine its world model and value function and leverage model-based planning to achieve even higher success rates in challenging tasks.

title: Strengthening Generative Robot Policies through Predictive World Modeling
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Diffusion models|Imitation learning|Reinforcement learning|World models for robotics|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: World models strengthen generative policy performance
summary: World models for strengthening generative robot policies.
abstract: The paper presents generative predictive control (GPC), a learning control framework that (i) clones a generative diffusion-based policy from expert demonstrations, (ii) trains a predictive action-conditioned world model from both expert demonstrations and random explorations, and (iii) synthesizes an online planner that ranks and optimizes the action proposals from (i) by looking ahead into the future using the world model from (ii). Across a variety of robotic manipulation tasks, GPC consistently outperforms behavior cloning in both state-based and vision-based settings, in simulation and in the real world.

title: VPWEM: Non-Markovian Visuomotor Policy with Working and Episodic Memory
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Imitation learning|Verification of visuomotor policies|Diffusion models|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Working + episodic memory boost visuomotor policy by 20%+ over diffusion/VLA
summary: Non-Markovian policy with working and episodic memories outperforming diffusion policies and VLAs by 20%+.
abstract: Imitation learning from human demonstrations has achieved significant success in robotic control, yet most visuomotor policies still condition on single-step observations or short-context histories, making them struggle with non-Markovian tasks that require long-term memory. Simply enlarging the context window incurs substantial computational and memory costs and encourages overfitting to spurious correlations, leading to catastrophic failures under distribution shift and violating real-time constraints in robotic systems. In this paper, we propose VPWEM, a non-Markovian visuomotor policy equipped with working and episodic memories. The compressor uses self-attention over a cache of past summary tokens and cross-attention over a cache of historical observations, and is trained jointly with the policy. We instantiate VPWEM on diffusion policies to exploit both short-term and episode-wide information for action generation with nearly constant memory and computation per step. Experiments demonstrate that VPWEM outperforms state-of-the-art baselines including diffusion policies and vision-language-action (VLA) models by more than 20% on the memory-intensive manipulation tasks in MIKASA and achieves an average 5% improvement on the mobile manipulation benchmark MoMaRT.

title: Learning Adaptive Cross-Embodiment Visuomotor Policy with Contrastive Prompt Orchestration
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Verification of visuomotor policies|Active learning|Reinforcement learning
headline: Contrastive prompts enable one visuomotor policy across different robots
summary: Contrastive prompt learning and adaptive orchestration for cross-embodiment visuomotor policies.
abstract: Learning adaptive visuomotor policies for embodied agents remains a formidable challenge, particularly when facing cross-embodiment variations such as diverse sensor configurations and dynamic properties. Conventional learning approaches often struggle to separate task-relevant features from domain-specific variations (e.g., lighting, field-of-view, and rotation), leading to poor sample efficiency and catastrophic failure in unseen environments. To bridge this gap, the authors propose ContrAstive Prompt Orchestration (CAPO), a novel approach for learning visuomotor policies that integrates contrastive prompt learning and adaptive prompt orchestration. For prompt learning, they devise a hybrid contrastive learning strategy that integrates visual, temporal action, and text objectives to establish a pool of learnable prompts, where each prompt induces a visual representation encapsulating fine-grained domain factors. Based on these learned prompts, they introduce an adaptive prompt orchestration mechanism that dynamically aggregates these prompts conditioned on current observations, enabling the agent to adaptively construct optimal state representations by identifying dominant domain factors instantaneously, and the policy optimization is effectively shielded from irrelevant interference, preventing the common issue of overfitting to source domains. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CAPO significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in sample efficiency and asymptotic performance, and it exhibits superior zero-shot adaptation across unseen target domains characterized by drastic environmental and physical shifts.

title: Residual Skill Policies: Learning an Adaptable Skill-based Action Space for RL in Robotics
authors: not specified
headline: Residual learning creates adaptable skill-based action spaces for RL
summary: Skill-based action space for compositional reinforcement learning in robotics.
abstract: Skill-based reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising strategy to leverage prior knowledge for accelerated robot learning. Skills are typically extracted from expert demonstrations and are embedded into a latent space from which they can be sampled as actions by a high-level RL agent. However, this skill space is expansive, and not all skills are relevant for a given robot state, making exploration difficult. Furthermore, the downstream RL agent is limited to learning structurally similar tasks to those used to construct the skill space. The authors propose accelerating exploration in the skill space using state-conditioned generative models to directly bias the high-level agent towards only sampling skills relevant to a given state based on prior experience. Next, they propose a low-level residual policy for fine-grained skill adaptation enabling downstream RL agents to adapt to unseen task variations. The approach is validated across four challenging manipulation tasks that differ from those used to build the skill space, demonstrating the ability to learn across task variations while significantly accelerating exploration, outperforming prior works.

title: ConceptACT: Episode-Level Concepts for Sample-Efficient Robotic Imitation Learning
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Imitation learning|Active learning|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: Semantic concept annotations make ACT more sample-efficient
summary: Extension of ACT leveraging semantic concept annotations for improved learning efficiency.
abstract: Imitation learning enables robots to acquire complex manipulation skills from human demonstrations, but current methods rely solely on low-level sensorimotor data while ignoring the rich semantic knowledge humans naturally possess about tasks. ConceptACT is an extension of Action Chunking with Transformers that leverages episode-level semantic concept annotations during training to improve learning efficiency. Unlike language-conditioned approaches that require semantic input at deployment, ConceptACT uses human-provided concepts (object properties, spatial relationships, task constraints) exclusively during demonstration collection, adding minimal annotation burden. Concepts are integrated using a modified transformer architecture in which the final encoder layer implements concept-aware cross-attention, supervised to align with human annotations. Through experiments on two robotic manipulation tasks with logical constraints, ConceptACT converges faster and achieves superior sample efficiency compared to standard ACT. Architectural integration through attention mechanisms significantly outperforms naive auxiliary prediction losses or language-conditioned models, demonstrating that properly integrated semantic supervision provides powerful inductive biases for more efficient robot learning.

title: ManiLong-Shot: Interaction-Aware One-Shot Imitation Learning for Long-Horizon Manipulation
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Imitation learning|Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Safe & robust robot control|Controller composition|VLA reasoning and chain-of-thought|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: One-shot imitation handles long-horizon manipulation from a single demo
summary: One-shot imitation learning for long-horizon prehensile manipulation from single demonstrations.
abstract: One-shot imitation learning (OSIL) offers a promising way to teach robots new skills without large-scale data collection. However, current OSIL methods are primarily limited to short-horizon tasks, thus limiting their applicability to complex, long-horizon manipulations. To address this limitation, ManiLong-Shot proposes a novel framework that enables effective OSIL for long-horizon prehensile manipulation tasks. ManiLong-Shot structures long-horizon tasks around physical interaction events, reframing the problem as sequencing interaction-aware primitives instead of directly imitating continuous trajectories. This primitive decomposition can be driven by high-level reasoning from a vision-language model (VLM) or by rule-based heuristics derived from robot state changes. For each primitive, ManiLong-Shot predicts invariant regions critical to the interaction, establishes correspondences between the demonstration and the current observation, and computes the target end-effector pose, enabling effective task execution. Extensive simulation experiments show that ManiLong-Shot, trained on only 10 short-horizon tasks, generalizes to 20 unseen long-horizon tasks across three difficulty levels via one-shot imitation, achieving a 22.8% relative improvement over the SOTA. Additionally, real-robot experiments validate ManiLong-Shot's ability to robustly execute three long-horizon manipulation tasks via OSIL, confirming its practical applicability.

title: Learning Diverse Skills for Behavior Models with Mixture of Experts
authors: not specified
headline: MoE learns diverse manipulation skills with specialized observation routing
summary: Di-BM framework learning diverse manipulation skills using MoE with specialized observation distributions.
abstract: Imitation learning has demonstrated strong performance in robotic manipulation by learning from large-scale human demonstrations. While existing models excel at single-task learning, it is observed in practical applications that their performance degrades in the multi-task setting, where interference across tasks leads to an averaging effect. To address this issue, the authors propose to learn diverse skills for behavior models with Mixture of Experts, referred to as Di-BM. Di-BM associates each expert with a distinct observation distribution, enabling experts to specialize in sub-regions of the observation space. Specifically, they employ energy-based models to represent expert-specific observation distributions and jointly train them alongside the corresponding action models. The approach is plug-and-play and can be seamlessly integrated into standard imitation learning methods. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world robotic manipulation tasks demonstrate that Di-BM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

title: Beyond Behavior Cloning: Robustness through Interactive Imitation and Contrastive Learning
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Imitation learning|Adversarial robustness of robot policies|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: Interactive imitation + contrastive learning make cloned policies more robust
summary: Extends behavior cloning with interactive imitation and contrastive learning for robustness.
abstract: Behavior cloning (BC) traditionally relies on demonstration data, assuming the demonstrated actions are optimal. This can lead to overfitting under noisy data, particularly when expressive models are used (e.g., the energy-based model in Implicit BC). To address this, the authors extend behavior cloning into an iterative process of optimal action estimation within the Interactive Imitation Learning framework, specifically introducing Contrastive policy Learning from Interactive Corrections (CLIC). CLIC leverages human corrections to estimate a set of desired actions and optimizes the policy to select actions from this set. Extensive simulation and real-robot experiments validate CLIC's advantages over existing state-of-the-art methods, including stable training of energy-based models, robustness to feedback noise, and adaptability to diverse feedback types beyond demonstrations.

title: TransCurriculum: Multi-Dimensional Curriculum Learning for Fast & Stable Locomotion
authors: not specified
headline: Transformer-based curriculum adapts terrain, speed, and randomization for locomotion
summary: Transformer-based curriculum learning adapting velocity, terrain difficulty, domain randomization for quadrupedal locomotion.
abstract: High-speed legged locomotion struggles with stability and transfer losses at higher command velocities during deployment. One reason is that most curricula vary difficulty along single axis, for example increase the range of command velocities, terrain difficulty, or domain parameters (e.g. friction or payload mass) using either fixed update rule or instantaneous rewards while ignoring how the history of robot training has evolved. The authors propose TransCurriculum, a transformer-based multi-dimensional curriculum learning approach for agile quadrupedal locomotion that adapts to 3 axes: velocity command targets, terrain difficulty, and domain randomization parameters (friction and payload mass). Rather than feeding task reward history directly into the low-level control policy, their formulation exploits it at the curriculum level.

title: APEX: Learning Adaptive High-Platform Traversal for Humanoid Robots
authors: not specified
headline: RL composes climbing + locomotion for humanoid platform traversal
summary: Terrain-conditioned RL composing climbing and locomotion skills for humanoid platform traversal.
abstract: Humanoid locomotion has advanced rapidly with deep reinforcement learning (DRL), enabling robust feet-based traversal over uneven terrain. Yet platforms beyond leg length remain largely out of reach because current RL training paradigms often converge to jumping-like solutions that are high-impact, torque-limited, and unsafe for real-world deployment. To address this gap, APEX is a system for perceptive, climbing-based high-platform traversal that composes terrain-conditioned behaviors: climb-up and climb-down at vertical edges, walking or crawling on the platform, and stand-up and lie-down for posture reconfiguration. Central to the approach is a generalized ratchet progress reward for learning contact-rich, goal-reaching maneuvers. It tracks the best-so-far task progress and penalizes non-improving steps, providing dense yet velocity-free supervision that enables efficient exploration under strong safety regularization.

title: SteadyTray: Learning Object Balancing in Humanoid Tray Transport via Residual RL
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Reinforcement learning|Controller composition|Adversarial robustness of robot policies|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Hierarchical RL decouples walking from payload balancing in humanoid transport
summary: Hierarchical RL decoupling locomotion from payload stabilization for humanoid transport.
abstract: Stabilizing unsecured payloads against the inherent oscillations of dynamic bipedal locomotion remains a critical engineering bottleneck for humanoids in unstructured environments. To solve this, we introduce ReST-RL, a hierarchical reinforcement learning architecture that explicitly decouples locomotion from payload stabilization, evaluated via the SteadyTray benchmark. Rather than relying on monolithic end-to-end learning, our framework integrates a robust base locomotion policy with a dynamic residual module engineered to actively cancel gait-induced perturbations at the end-effector. This architectural separation ensures steady tray transport without degrading the underlying bipedal stability. In simulation, the residual design significantly outperforms end-to-end baselines in gait smoothness and orientation accuracy, achieving a 96.9% success rate in variable velocity tracking and 74.5% robustness against external force disturbances. Successfully deployed on the Unitree G1 humanoid hardware, this modular approach demonstrates highly reliable zero-shot sim-to-real generalization across various objects and external force disturbances.

title: VLA-RL: Towards Masterful Robotic Manipulation with Scalable Reinforcement Learning
authors: not specified
headline: Online RL fine-tuning makes pretrained VLA models better at manipulation
summary: Online RL to improve pretrained VLA models for downstream manipulation tasks.
abstract: Recent high-capacity vision-language-action (VLA) models have demonstrated impressive performance on a range of robotic manipulation tasks by imitating human demonstrations. However, exploiting offline data with limited visited states will cause execution failure in out-of-distribution scenarios. Intuitively, an exploration-based method that improves on online collected data at test time could address this limitation. We present VLA-RL, an algorithmic and systematic framework that leverages online reinforcement learning (RL) to improve pretrained auto-regressive VLAs in downstream tasks. Within a unified perspective, we first introduce a trajectory-level RL formulation for auto-regressive VLA training, which models general robotic manipulation trajectory as multi-modal multi-turn conversation. To this end, we propose robotic process reward model, a novel approach for reward densification that aligns with the VLA's token generation process. VLA-RL enables OpenVLA-7B to surpass the strongest finetuned baseline by 4.5% on 40 challenging robotic manipulation tasks in LIBERO, and even matches the performance of advanced commercial models such as π₀-FAST. Notably, we observe that VLA-RL benefits from increased test-time optimization, indicating an early spark of inference scaling laws in robotics.
notes: Connects RL with generalist policies

title: Data-Efficient Learning from Human Interventions for Mobile Robots
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Reinforcement learning|Imitation learning|Safe & robust robot control|Adversarial robustness of robot policies|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: Human-in-the-loop interventions enable data-efficient mobile robot learning
summary: Human-in-the-loop approach for data-efficient mobile robot learning.
abstract: Mobile robots are essential in applications such as autonomous delivery and hospitality services. Applying learning-based methods to address mobile robot tasks has gained popularity due to its robustness and generalizability. Traditional methods such as Imitation Learning (IL) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) offer adaptability but require large datasets, carefully crafted reward functions, and face sim-to-real gaps, making them challenging for efficient and safe real-world deployment. The paper proposes an online human-in-the-loop learning method called PVP4Real that combines IL and RL to address these issues. PVP4Real enables efficient real-time policy learning from online human intervention and demonstration, without reward or any pretraining, significantly improving data efficiency and training safety. The method was validated by training two different robots—a legged quadruped and a wheeled delivery robot—in two mobile robot tasks, one of which uses raw RGBD images as observation. The training finishes within 15 minutes.

title: Evaluating Robot Policies in a World Model
authors: not specified
matched_interests: Reinforcement learning|Diffusion models|Imitation learning|World models for robotics|Data-driven verification|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: World models as a proxy for expensive real-world robot policy evaluation
summary: World-model-based Policy Evaluation to address expensive real-world testing gaps.
abstract: Robotics has broad applications from automating house chores to taking care of patients. However, evaluating robot control policies is challenging, as real-world testing is expensive, while handcrafted simulations often fail to accurately reflect real-world conditions, resulting in poor correlation between simulated evaluation and real-world outcomes. The work investigates World-model-based Policy Evaluation (WPE). The authors first train an action-conditioned video generation model as a proxy to real-world environments. To enable efficient rollouts of hundreds of interactive steps while mitigating error accumulation in the world model, they propose an inference scheme which they call Blockwise-Autoregressive Diffusion Transformer with adjustable context and decoding horizon lengths. To ensure that the world model indeed follows action input, they propose metrics based on the agreement between the ground truth video and generated video conditioned on the same sequence of actions to evaluate the world model. They then use the world model for policy evaluation by performing Monte Carlo rollouts in the world model while employing a vision-language model (VLM) as a reward function. The findings show that WPE tends to underestimate the policy values for in-distribution actions and overestimate policy values for out-of-distribution actions. Nevertheless, WPE preserves the relative rankings of different policies. In emulating real robot executions, WPE achieves high fidelity in mimicing robot arm movements as in real videos, while emulating highly realistic object interaction remains challenging. Despite this limitation, a world model can serve as a starting point for evaluating robot policies before real-world deployment.
notes: Relevant to verification

title: Formal Methods for Robotic Manipulation: A Survey
authors: Yancheng Zhu, Ashok Kumar, Hugo Tadashi Kussaba, Wenliang Liu, Fares Abu-Dakka, Abdalla Swikir
headline: Structured survey of formal specification, verification, and synthesis methods for robotic manipulation
summary: This survey reviews the application of formal methods to robotic manipulation, a domain characterized by complex contact dynamics, environmental uncertainty, and safety-critical human interaction. The authors categorize formal specification languages, verification approaches, and synthesis techniques, reviewing state-of-the-art methods that address manipulation-specific challenges beyond traditional testing and simulation. The paper provides a structured reference for researchers seeking to apply formal guarantees to manipulation tasks.
abstract: Robotic manipulation is characterized by complex interactions with dynamic and uncertain environments, making the design, verification, and assurance of such systems highly challenging. Although traditional testing and simulation provide partial correctness, they are insufficient for complete assurance, especially in safety-critical or human-interactive settings. In recent years, many formal methods have emerged as a powerful and systematic approach to specify, verify, and synthesize reliable robotic manipulation behaviors. This survey provides a comprehensive and structured overview of formal methods for robotic manipulation. The paper first discusses the unique challenges in manipulation tasks, then categorizes core formalisms and specification languages, and finally reviews state-of-the-art techniques for formal specification, verification, and synthesis.

title: A General Framework for Robust Quantitative Semantics of Signal Temporal Logic
authors: Jiawei Chen, José Luiz Vargas de Mendonça, Konstantinos Mamouras, Jean-Baptiste Jeannin
venue: Conference paper (University of Michigan / Rice University)
matched_interests: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Unified framework identifies soundness conditions for STL robustness measures and compares falsification effectiveness
summary: Quantitative semantics of STL are widely used in falsification and control synthesis, but a systematic understanding of their properties has been missing. This paper develops a general mathematical framework to model quantitative semantics and derives simple necessary conditions for soundness — ensuring robustness sign matches truth value. The authors show how several recently proposed semantics fit into the framework, which ones satisfy soundness, and compare their effectiveness as optimization objectives for falsification on benchmarks.
abstract: Quantitative semantics of Signal Temporal Logic (STL) play an important role in both the falsification and control synthesis for dynamical systems by assigning numerical quantities to truth values. Recently, several different quantitative semantics have been proposed, offering better performance in many cases. Yet a general, systematic understanding of the structure and properties of quantitative semantics is missing. In this paper, we develop a general framework to model quantitative semantics. We focus mainly on soundness, which requires that the quantitative semantics of a statement is positive when the statement is true, and negative when the statement is false. This ensures that counterexamples will not be missed during verification. We derive simple, necessary conditions in our framework for soundness. We show how several recently proposed quantitative semantics fit in our framework, and how others do not, typically because they do not strictly satisfy soundness. We implement various quantitative semantics, including existing semantics from literature, in our framework and compare their effectiveness as objective functions for optimization-based falsification on both novel and existing benchmarks.

title: CORE: Uncertainty-Gated Policy Optimization for Reliable Performance Under Distribution Shift
authors: Shawn Azdam
matched_interests: Imitation learning|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Uncertainty-gated promote/monitor/rollback loop improves RL reliability under distribution shift
summary: CORE proposes a Conservative Online Reliability Enforcement framework that gates policy updates based on uncertainty-calibrated signals derived from ensemble disagreement. The method combines risk-aware trust-region learning with an online promote/monitor/rollback control loop that rejects destabilizing update candidates before downstream validation. Evaluated on the MetaWorld shifted benchmark, the approach improves reliability-floor behavior — the worst-case performance floor — while maintaining fixed-budget update control.
abstract: We present Conservative Online Reliability Enforcement (CORE), an uncertainty-gated policy optimization framework for robust policy updates under distribution shift. The method combines risk-aware trust-region learning with online promote/monitor/rollback control driven by uncertainty-calibrated signals. Across matched-seed benchmark and scenario-model evaluations, our approach improves reliability-floor behavior while preserving fixed-budget update control. We provide algorithm design, theory-guided gate rationale, and diagnostics that clarify where gated updates help and where limitations remain.

title: STLts-Div: Diversified Trace Synthesis from STL Specifications Using MILP (Extended Version)
authors: Martin Jouve-Genty, Han Su, Sota Sato, Jie An, Zhenya Zhang, Ichiro Hasuo
headline: MILP-based diversified STL trace synthesis generates varied witnesses to reveal specification intent
summary: Writing correct STL specifications is difficult, and a single satisfying trace rarely suffices to understand what a specification admits. STLts-Div introduces diversified trace synthesis using MILP to automatically generate multiple diverse satisfying behaviors from an STL specification. The extended version provides a systematic approach to exploring the satisfying behavior space, helping engineers validate that their STL specifications capture intended system behaviors and identify unintended edge cases.
abstract: Modern cyber-physical systems are complex, and requirements are often written in Signal Temporal Logic (STL). Writing the right STL is difficult in practice; engineers benefit from concrete executions that illustrate what a specification actually admits. Trace synthesis addresses this need, but a single witness rarely suffices to understand intent or explore edge cases - diverse satisfying behaviors are far more informative. We introduce diversified trace synthesis: the automatic generation of sets of behaviorally diverse traces that satisfy a given STL formula. Building on a MILP encoding of STL and system model, we formalize three complementary diversification objectives - Boolean distance, random Boolean distance, and value distance - all captured by an objective function and solved iteratively. We implement these ideas in STLts-Div, a lightweight Python tool that integrates with Gurobi.

title: RC-NF: Robot-Conditioned Normalizing Flow for Real-Time Anomaly Detection in Robotic Manipulation
authors: Shijie Zhou, Bin Zhu, Jiarui Yang, Xiangyu Zhao, Jingjing Chen, Yu-Gang Jiang
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Imitation learning|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control|Adversarial robustness of robot policies
headline: Robot-conditioned normalizing flow detects OOD conditions in VLA manipulation in real time
summary: VLA models trained via imitation learning degrade under out-of-distribution (OOD) conditions in dynamic environments. RC-NF introduces a normalizing flow model conditioned on robot state and object motion trajectories to detect when the VLA operates outside its training distribution, triggering intervention before task failure. The real-time monitoring capability provides a safety layer over VLA-based manipulation systems, improving deployment reliability without requiring retraining.
abstract: Recent advances in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have enabled robots to execute increasingly complex tasks. However, VLA models trained through imitation learning struggle to operate reliably in dynamic environments and often fail under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) conditions. To address this issue, we propose Robot-Conditioned Normalizing Flow (RC-NF), a real-time monitoring model for robotic anomaly detection and intervention that ensures the robot's state and the object's motion trajectory align with the task. RC-NF decouples the processing of task-aware robot and object states within the normalizing flow. It requires only positive samples for unsupervised training and calculates accurate robotic anomaly scores during inference through the probability density function. We further present LIBERO-Anomaly-10, a benchmark comprising three categories of robotic anomalies for simulation evaluation. RC-NF achieves state-of-the-art performance across all anomaly types compared to previous methods in monitoring robotic tasks. Real-world experiments demonstrate that RC-NF operates as a plug-and-play module for VLA models (e.g., pi0), providing a real-time OOD signal that enables state-level rollback or task-level replanning when necessary, with a response latency under 100 ms. These results demonstrate that RC-NF noticeably enhances the robustness and adaptability of VLA-based robotic systems in dynamic environments.

title: Verification and Validation of Physics-Informed Surrogate Component Models for Dynamic Power-System Simulation
authors: Petros Ellinas, Indrajit Chaudhuri, Johanna Vorwerk, Spyros Chatzivasileiadis
headline: V&V framework tests physics-informed ML surrogates for system-level accuracy in coupled power simulations
summary: Physics-informed ML surrogates can accelerate dynamic power-system simulation but may be accurate in isolation yet fail when coupled into the full differential-algebraic system. This paper formalizes a V&V framework that tests surrogate component models for system-level accuracy after insertion into coupled simulations, going beyond standalone component accuracy metrics. The framework provides guidance for the safe deployment of learned surrogate models in critical infrastructure simulation.
abstract: Physics-informed machine learning surrogates are increasingly explored to accelerate dynamic simulation of generators, converters, and other power grid components. The key question, however, is not only whether a surrogate matches a stand-alone component model on average, but whether it remains accurate after insertion into a differential-algebraic simulator, where the surrogate outputs enter the algebraic equations coupling the component to the rest of the system. This paper formulates that in-simulator use as a verification and validation (V\&V) problem. A finite-horizon bound is derived that links allowable component-output error to algebraic-coupling sensitivity, dynamic error amplification, and the simulation horizon. Two complementary settings are then studied: model-based verification against a reference component solver, and data-based validation through conformal calibration of the component-output variables exchanged with the simulator. The framework is general, but the case study focuses on physics-informed neural-network surrogates of second-, fourth-, and sixth-order synchronous-machine models. Results show that good stand-alone surrogate accuracy does not by itself guarantee accurate in-simulator behavior, that the largest discrepancies concentrate in stressed operating regions, and that small equation residuals do not necessarily imply small state-trajectory errors.

title: Graph-of-Constraints Model Predictive Control for Reactive Multi-agent Task and Motion Planning
authors: Anastasios Manganaris, Jeremy Lu, Ahmed H. Qureshi, Suresh Jagannathan
matched_interests: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Safe & robust robot control|Reinforcement learning
headline: Graph-of-Constraints MPC handles partially ordered multi-agent TAMP with dynamic replanning
summary: Multi-agent Task and Motion Planning with partially ordered interdependent geometric constraints is challenging because static methods fail when disturbances alter task allocations. GoC-MPC generalizes the sequence-of-constraints framework using a graph-structured representation that handles partial ordering and dynamic agent reassignment. The reactive MPC formulation adapts online to disturbances, enabling robust multi-agent planning under changing task conditions.
abstract: Sequences of interdependent geometric constraints are central to many multi-agent Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) problems. However, existing methods for handling such constraint sequences struggle with partially ordered tasks and dynamic agent assignments. They typically assume static assignments and cannot adapt when disturbances alter task allocations. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Graph-of-Constraints Model Predictive Control (GoC-MPC), a generalized sequence-of-constraints framework integrated with MPC. GoC-MPC naturally supports partially ordered tasks, dynamic agent coordination, and disturbance recovery. By defining constraints over tracked 3D keypoints, our method robustly solves diverse multi-agent manipulation tasks-coordinating agents and adapting online from visual observations alone, without relying on training data or environment models. Experiments demonstrate that GoC-MPC achieves higher success rates, significantly faster TAMP computation, and shorter overall paths compared to recent baselines, establishing it as an efficient and robust solution for multi-agent manipulation under real-world disturbances. Our supplementary video and code can be found at https://sites.google.com/view/goc-mpc/home .

title: Cost-Matching Model Predictive Control for Efficient Reinforcement Learning in Humanoid Locomotion
authors: Wenqi Cai, Kyriakos G. Vamvoudakis, Sébastien Gros, Anthony Tzes
headline: MPC cost-matching trains humanoid locomotion by fitting action-value functions to closed-loop data
summary: Proposes cost-matching approach within MPC-based RL for optimal humanoid locomotion. Trains parameterized MPC with centroidal dynamics to approximate action-value function from high-fidelity data. Enables efficient gradient-based learning without repeatedly solving MPC during training. Demonstrates improved locomotion and robustness on commercial humanoid platform.
abstract: In this paper, we propose a cost-matching approach for optimal humanoid locomotion within a Model Predictive Control (MPC)-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework. A parameterized MPC formulation with centroidal dynamics is trained to approximate the action-value function obtained from high-fidelity closed-loop data. Specifically, the MPC cost-to-go is evaluated along recorded state-action trajectories, and the parameters are updated to minimize the discrepancy between MPC-predicted values and measured returns. This formulation enables efficient gradient-based learning while avoiding the computational burden of repeatedly solving the MPC problem during training. The proposed method is validated in simulation using a commercial humanoid platform. Results demonstrate improved locomotion performance and robustness to model mismatch and external disturbances compared with manually tuned baselines.

title: LeRobot: An Open-Source Library for End-to-End Robot Learning
authors: Not specified
headline: Open-source library consolidating robot data collection streaming and state-of-the-art learning algorithms
summary: Comprehensive open-source library for robot learning including data collection dataset streaming and SOTA algorithms. ICLR 2026.
abstract: Robotics is undergoing a significant transformation powered by advances in high-level control techniques based on machine learning, giving rise to the field of robot learning. Recent progress in robot learning has been accelerated by the increasing availability of affordable teleoperation systems, large-scale openly available datasets, and scalable learning-based methods. However, development in the field of robot learning is often slowed by fragmented, closed-source tools designed to only address specific sub-components within the robotics stack. LeRobot is an open-source library that integrates across the entire robot learning stack, from low-level middleware communication for motor controls to large-scale dataset collection, storage and streaming. The library is designed with a strong focus on real-world robotics, supporting accessible hardware platforms while remaining extensible to new embodiments. It also supports efficient implementations for various state-of-the-art robot learning algorithms from multiple prominent paradigms, as well as a generalized asynchronous inference stack. Unlike traditional pipelines which heavily rely on hand-crafted techniques, LeRobot emphasizes scalable learning approaches that improve directly with more data and compute. Designed for accessibility, scalability, and openness, LeRobot lowers the barrier to entry for researchers and practitioners to robotics while providing a platform for reproducible, state-of-the-art robot learning.
notes: ICLR 2026 tool paper; mildly relevant

title: Do GUI Grounders Truly Understand UI Elements?
authors: S Jandial, Y Li, J Wagle, K Koishida
headline: GUI grounding models may not understand UI elements as well as benchmarks suggest
summary: GUI grounding. Not robotics relevant.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy citation. Low - GUI grounding

title: Detecting Subtle Biases in AI Language Models
authors: S Bali, F Farsi, M Hosseini et al.
headline: New methods detect subtle biases in large language models
summary: LLM bias detection. Not relevant.
abstract: This paper was authored by Shayan Bali, Farhan Farsi, Mohammad Hosseini, Adel Khorramrouz, and Ehsaneddin Asgari, and was presented at EACL 2026. The study focuses on dimensions that are socially influential but less explored, including residency status, political ideology, fitness status, educational attainment, and attitudes toward AI. The researchers propose a baseline and employ one statistical test and one metric: a permutation test that reveals the presence of bias by comparing the probability distributions of ethical/unethical scenarios with the probability distribution of neutral scenarios on each demographic group, and a tendency measurement that captures the magnitude of bias with respect to the relative difference between probability distribution of ethical and unethical scenarios. Evaluations of 12 prominent LLMs reveal persistent and nuanced biases across all four attributes, and Llama models exhibited the most pronounced biases. These findings highlight the need for refined ethical benchmarks and bias-mitigation tools in LLMs.
notes: Alert: Lindemann citation. Low - LLM bias

title: SYMDIREC: Neuro-Symbolic Framework for RTL Synthesis
authors: P Vijayaraghavan et al.
headline: Neuro-symbolic approach to hardware RTL synthesis
summary: RTL synthesis. Not relevant.
abstract: Register-Transfer Level (RTL) synthesis and summarization are central to hardware design automation but remain challenging for Large Language Models (LLMs) due to rigid HDL syntax, limited supervision, and weak alignment with natural language. Existing prompting and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) methods have not incorporated symbolic planning, limiting their structural precision. SYMDIREC is a neuro-symbolic framework that decomposes RTL tasks into symbolic subgoals, retrieves relevant code via a fine-tuned retriever, and assembles verified outputs through LLM reasoning. Supporting both Verilog and VHDL without LLM fine-tuning, SYMDIREC achieves ~20% higher Pass@1 rates for synthesis and 15-20% ROUGE-L improvements for summarization over prompting baselines.
notes: Alert: Chuchu Fan citation. Low - hardware design

title: SCRAMPPI: Efficient Contingency Planning for Mobile Robot Navigation via Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability
authors: RH Srirangam, L Jung, R Poola, M Everett
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability
headline: HJ reachability guarantees contingency plan feasibility in real-time MPPI planning
summary: This paper addresses contingency planning for mobile robots by formalizing the requirement that a feasible trajectory to a safe set must exist from any point along the nominal plan. The approach leverages Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis to certify contingency feasibility, computing the value function online as the environment is revealed. Integration with MPPI sampling-based planning yields efficient real-time planning with hard safety constraint satisfaction, validated on mobile robot evasion tasks.
abstract: Autonomous robots commonly aim to complete a nominal behavior while minimizing a cost; this leaves them vulnerable to failure or unplanned scenarios, where a backup or contingency plan to a safe set is needed to avoid a total mission failure. This is formalized as a trajectory optimization problem over the nominal cost with a safety constraint: from any point along the nominal plan, a feasible trajectory to a designated safe set must exist. Previous methods either relax this hard constraint, or use an expensive sampling-based strategy to optimize for this constraint. Instead, we formalize this requirement as a reach-avoid problem and leverage Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability analysis to certify contingency feasibility. By computing the value function of our safe-set's backward reachable set online as the environment is revealed and integrating it with a sampling based planner (MPPI) via resampling based rollouts, we guarantee satisfaction of the hard constraint while greatly increasing sampling efficiency. Finally, we present simulated and hardware experiments demonstrating our algorithm generating nominal and contingency plans in real time on a mobile robot in an adversarial evasion task.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy/Bansal/Fan citation. Core — data-driven verification + HJ reachability for contingency planning

title: Safety Guardrails in the Sky: Realizing Control Barrier Functions on the VISTA F-16 Jet
authors: AW Singletary, MH Cohen, TG Molnar, AD Ames
matched_interests: Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: CBF runtime assurance deployed on real F-16 jet with flight test validation
summary: This paper demonstrates the implementation of control barrier function-based safety guardrails on a real F-16 fighter jet. The system operates as a safety layer that minimally modifies unsafe pilot inputs while respecting constraints including g-limits, altitude bounds, and geofence restrictions. Extensive flight testing validates that the approach maintains safety while preserving pilot control authority when commands are inherently safe.
abstract: The advancement of autonomous systems -- from legged robots to self-driving vehicles and aircraft -- necessitates executing increasingly high-performance and dynamic motions without ever putting the system or its environment in harm's way. We present Guardrails, a runtime assurance mechanism grounded in control barrier function theory. This system enables autonomous platforms to operate safely near their performance limits by blending operator commands with protective control actions. The team demonstrated their approach through flight tests on an F-16 fighter jet, where Guardrails supervised a human pilot while enforcing constraints including g-limits, altitude bounds, and geofence restrictions. Throughout extensive flight testing, Guardrails successfully ensured safety, keeping the pilot in control when safe to do so and minimally modifying unsafe pilot inputs otherwise.
notes: Alert: Bansal. Core — CBF theory applied to real systems (F-16 jets); exceptional real-world demonstration [residual +1: direct real-world validation of CBF-based safety on high-performance system]

title: MPC as a Copilot: A Predictive Filter Framework with Safety and Stability Guarantees
authors: Y Yan, C Tao, J Su, C Liu, S Li
headline: MPC-based safety filter ensures learned policies satisfy constraints and stability
summary: This paper proposes the Predictive Safety-Stability Filter (PS2F), a framework that combines a nominal learned policy with an MPC safety layer to guarantee both constraint satisfaction and asymptotic stability. The framework cascades two optimal control problems: a nominal policy layer and a secondary filtering layer that adjusts commands to remain within provably safe and stable regions. Time-varying parameterization allows balancing between safety-prioritized and stability-oriented modes without additional conservatism.
abstract: Ensuring both safety and stability remains a fundamental challenge in learning-based control, where goal-oriented policies often neglect system constraints and closed-loop state convergence. The authors propose the Predictive Safety-Stability Filter (PS2F), described as a unified predictive filter framework that guarantees constraint satisfaction and asymptotic stability within a single architecture. The framework uses two cascaded optimal control problems: a nominal model predictive control (MPC) layer that serves solely as a copilot and a secondary filtering layer that adjusts external command to remain within a provably safe and stable region. Key contributions include recursive feasibility, asymptotic stability without additional conservatism beyond standard MPC, and a time-varying parameterization allowing the system to balance between safety-prioritised and stability-oriented operation modes.
notes: Alert: Bansal. High — MPC safety filter for learned policies; verification of composed controller systems

title: Time-Varying Reach-Avoid Control Certificates for Stochastic Systems
authors: R Mazouz, L Laurenti, M Lahijanian
headline: Time-varying certificates enable reach-avoid verification for stochastic systems with SOS synthesis
summary: This paper introduces a reach-avoid certificate framework for discrete-time stochastic systems across both finite and infinite horizons. The authors propose both time-varying and time-invariant certificate formulations synthesizable via sum-of-squares optimization, enabling convex verification of given controllers. An additional SOS-based joint synthesis method optimizes feedback controllers and certificates simultaneously, maximizing target reach probability while avoiding unsafe regions.
abstract: Reach-avoid analysis is fundamental to reasoning about the safety and goal-reaching behavior of dynamical systems, and serves as a foundation for specifying and verifying more complex control objectives. This paper introduces a reach-avoid certificate framework for discrete-time, continuous-space stochastic systems over both finite- and infinite-horizon settings. We propose two formulations: time-varying and time-invariant certificates. We also show how these certificates can be synthesized using sum-of-squares (SOS) optimization, providing a convex formulation for verifying a given controller. Furthermore, we present an SOS-based method for the joint synthesis of an optimal feedback controller and its corresponding reach-avoid certificate, enabling the maximization of the probability of reaching the target set while avoiding unsafe regions. Case studies and benchmark results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed framework in certifying and controlling stochastic systems with continuous state and action spaces.
notes: Alert: Fan citation. High — reach-avoid verification for stochastic systems

title: Learning Certified Neural Network Controllers Using Contraction and Interval Analysis
authors: A Harapanahalli, S Coogan, A Davydov
headline: GPU-accelerated learning of NN controllers with formal verification via contraction metrics
summary: This paper presents a framework for jointly training neural network controllers with corresponding neural Riemannian metrics, achieving rigorous closed-loop contraction guarantees. The method uses GPU-parallelized corner checks to analyze asymmetric matrix functions and verify contraction regions. For control-affine systems, the approach produces exponentially stabilizing trajectory-tracking controllers from just two forward inferences, with the entire framework including contraction metric and verified 10-dimensional region trained in under 10 minutes.
abstract: The paper jointly trains a neural network controller and a neural Riemannian metric with rigorous closed-loop contraction guarantees using formal bound propagation. The key contribution involves analyzing an asymmetric matrix function through GPU-parallelized corner checks to verify contraction regions. For control-affine systems, the method produces tracking controllers capable of exponentially stabilizing any dynamically feasible trajectory using just two forward inferences. The authors demonstrate their approach on a 10-state quadrotor model, learning a control policy, contraction metric, and verified 10-dimensional contraction region in under 10 minutes using JAX and interval bound propagation tools.
notes: Alert: Fan citation. Core — certified neural network controllers with formal guarantees [residual +1: directly combines learning and verification for NN controllers]

title: LLM-Enabled Low-Altitude UAV Natural Language Navigation via Signal Temporal Logic Specification Translation and Repair
authors: Y Ping et al
matched_interests: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Safe & robust robot control|Data-driven verification
headline: LLM translates natural language UAV commands to STL specs with automatic repair mechanism
summary: This paper presents a closed-loop framework enabling UAVs to navigate using natural language instructions in urban environments. An LLM with chain-of-thought supervision and group-relative policy optimization translates instructions into formal Signal Temporal Logic specifications. A specification repair mechanism combining mathematical optimization with AI reasoning resolves infeasible constraints while maintaining safety requirements, validated through simulations and real-world flight experiments.
abstract: The paper presents a framework enabling unmanned aerial vehicles to navigate using natural language instructions in urban environments. The system converts natural language into Signal Temporal Logic specifications and generates safe flight paths. Key technical approach: The researchers developed an LLM enhanced with chain-of-thought supervision and group-relative policy optimization to translate instructions into formal specifications. They also created a specification repair mechanism combining mathematical optimization with AI reasoning to resolve infeasible constraints while maintaining safety requirements. The abstract notes that extensive simulations and real-world flight experiments demonstrate that the proposed closed-loop framework significantly improves NL-to-STL translation robustness, enabling safe, interpretable, and adaptable UAV navigation in complex scenarios.
notes: Alert: Fan citation. Moderate — STL-based safety specification for UAV navigation with LLM

title: Structural-Ambiguity-Aware Translation from Natural Language to Signal Temporal Logic
authors: K Fushimi et al
matched_interests: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: NL-to-STL translation preserves semantic ambiguity with plausibility-scored alternatives
summary: This paper proposes a principled approach for translating natural language instructions into Signal Temporal Logic formulas that explicitly preserves multiple plausible interpretations rather than forcing a single deterministic translation. Using Combinatory Categorial Grammar, the method generates deduplicated STL candidates with plausibility scores, capturing various formal interpretations of ambiguous instructions while collapsing equivalent derivations for compact representation.
abstract: Signal Temporal Logic (STL) is widely used to specify timed and safety-critical tasks for cyber-physical systems, but writing STL formulas directly is difficult for non-expert users. The paper proposes an ambiguity-preserving approach for converting natural language instructions into Signal Temporal Logic formulas. Rather than forcing a single interpretation, their method retains multiple plausible syntactic analyses using Combinatory Categorial Grammar. The pipeline generates deduplicated STL candidates with plausibility scores, explicitly representing various formal interpretations of ambiguous instructions while collapsing equivalent derivations.
notes: Alert: Lindemann citation. Moderate — NL-to-STL translation with ambiguity-aware handling

title: Safe multi-agent reinforcement learning based on adversarial strategy and control barrier function feasible set
authors: S Liu, S Wang, M Dai, G Lian, J Xu, X Kong
matched_interests: Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: Data-driven CBFs ensure safety in multi-agent RL through feasible set constraints
summary: This paper presents a framework for safe multi-agent reinforcement learning that incorporates control barrier function theory with adversarial training strategies. The approach learns policies within CBF-defined feasible sets, ensuring safety constraints are maintained across multi-agent interactions. By combining data-driven RL with formal safety guarantees from CBF theory, the method addresses the challenge of ensuring provable safety in decentralized learning systems.
abstract: Multi-agent reinforcement learning requires safety guarantees when multiple agents learn policies simultaneously in shared environments. This work combines control barrier function feasible sets with adversarial training strategies to ensure safety constraints are maintained during learning. The CBF-based safety layer constrains the action space of learned policies, preventing constraint violations while allowing agents to optimize their objectives. The approach is validated on multi-agent control tasks demonstrating that safety can be preserved while achieving competitive learning performance.
notes: Alert: Fan/Bansal/Herbert. High — data-driven CBFs for safe multi-agent reinforcement learning

title: Control Barrier Corridors: From Safety Functions to Safe Goal Sets
authors: O Arslan, N Atanasov
matched_interests: Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: CBF theory extended to define safe motion corridors and achievable goal sets
summary: This paper extends control barrier function theory beyond point-wise safety constraints to define safe motion corridors and achievable goal sets. The framework unifies CBF approaches with safe motion planning, providing a principled method for defining regions of feasible control and safe goal attainment. The extension broadens the applicability of CBF-based safety to more complex planning and navigation scenarios.
abstract: Control Barrier Corridors extend classical control barrier function theory to define spatial regions of safe control inputs and feasible goal sets. This generalization unifies CBF point constraints with corridor-based motion planning approaches, enabling synthesis of controllers that safely guide systems to goal regions while respecting environmental and system constraints. The framework is validated on robotic navigation and manipulation tasks.
notes: Alert: Herbert. High — CBF theory extension for safe goal sets and motion corridors

title: L1-Certified Distributionally Robust Planning for Safety-Constrained Adaptive Control
authors: A Hakobyan et al
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Reinforcement learning
headline: L1 adaptive control with distributional robustness guarantees for MPC-based safety
summary: This paper combines L1 adaptive control with distributionally robust MPC to provide formal safety guarantees under uncertainty in learned system models. The framework integrates online learning of matched and unmatched uncertainties with robust optimization, ensuring safety constraints are maintained despite model discrepancies. The approach enables safe adaptive control when system dynamics must be learned from data.
abstract: L1-Certified Distributionally Robust Planning combines adaptive control theory with robust MPC to handle model uncertainty in safety-critical systems. The framework provides formal guarantees that safety constraints are maintained despite uncertainties in learned dynamics models, enabling data-driven adaptive control with verified safety properties.
notes: Alert: Majumdar citation. High — distributional robustness + L1 adaptive control for safe learning-based planning

title: ProgressVLA: Progress-Guided Diffusion Policy for Vision-Language Robotic Manipulation
authors: H Yan, Q Li, J Yang, Y Mu
headline: VLA with progress awareness and diffusion refinement improves manipulation generalization
summary: This paper introduces ProgressVLA, extending vision-language-action models with explicit progress awareness for robotic manipulation tasks. A progress estimator pre-trained on unsupervised video-text datasets guides action refinement via inverse dynamics world modeling. The combination of progress prediction and diffusion-based policy refinement yields substantial improvements in success rates and real-world generalization on both simulated benchmarks and physical robot deployment.
abstract: Most existing vision-language-action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation lack progress awareness, typically relying on hand-crafted heuristics for task termination. The paper introduces ProgressVLA with two main contributions: (1) a progress estimator pre-trained on unsupervised video-text robotic datasets achieving low prediction residual (0.07 on a scale of [0, 1]) with zero-shot real-world generalization, and (2) an inverse dynamics world model that applies maximal progress regularization to guide action refinement. The researchers demonstrate substantial improvements in success rates and generalization over strong baselines through experiments on CALVIN and LIBERO benchmarks plus real-world robot deployment.
notes: Alert: Majumdar/Bajcsy. Interesting — VLA with diffusion + progress awareness for robotic manipulation

title: ManipArena: Comprehensive Real-world Evaluation of Reasoning-Oriented Generalist Robot Manipulation
authors: Y Sun et al
headline: Standardized real-world benchmark for evaluating VLA and world model manipulation systems
summary: This paper introduces ManipArena, a comprehensive evaluation framework for vision-language-action models and world models in robotic manipulation. The framework comprises 20 diverse reasoning-oriented tasks with 10,812 expert trajectories, supports multi-level generalization testing, and bridges simulation and real-world execution through high-quality 3D scanning. Rich sensory diagnostics and synchronized real-to-sim environments enable fair, reproducible evaluation of embodied intelligence systems.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models and world models have recently emerged as promising paradigms for general-purpose robotic intelligence, yet their progress is hindered by the lack of reliable evaluation protocols that reflect real-world deployment. Existing benchmarks are largely simulator-centric, which provide controllability but fail to capture the reality gap caused by perception noise, complex contact dynamics, hardware constraints, and system latency. Moreover, fragmented real-world evaluations across different robot platforms prevent fair and reproducible comparison. To address these challenges, we introduce ManipArena, a standardized evaluation framework designed to bridge simulation and real-world execution. ManipArena comprises 20 diverse tasks across 10,812 expert trajectories emphasizing reasoning-oriented manipulation tasks requiring semantic and spatial reasoning, supports multi-level generalization through controlled out-of-distribution settings, and incorporates long-horizon mobile manipulation beyond tabletop scenarios. The framework further provides rich sensory diagnostics, including low-level motor signals, and synchronized real-to-sim environments constructed via high-quality 3D scanning. Together, these features enable fair, realistic, and reproducible evaluation for both VLA and world model approaches, providing a scalable foundation for diagnosing and advancing embodied intelligence systems.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Interesting — VLA and world model evaluation benchmark for reasoning-oriented manipulation

title: AnchorVLA4D: an Anchor-Based Spatial-Temporal Vision-Language-Action Model for Robotic Manipulation
authors: J Zhu et al
headline: VLA with anchor images preserves spatial context and temporal memory in manipulation
summary: This paper proposes AnchorVLA4D, a vision-language-action model that addresses spatial perception and memory limitations through visual anchoring. The system uses anchor images to preserve initial scene context and employs a spatial encoder to reveal geometric relationships throughout manipulation episodes. Built on a Qwen2.5-VL backbone with diffusion-based action generation, the approach requires no additional sensing and achieves significant performance improvements with minimal computational overhead.
abstract: The paper's abstract describes a new robotic manipulation system that addresses spatial perception and memory limitations in Vision-Language-Action models. Key points include: Main Contribution: The authors propose using visual anchors as a means to enhance spatial and temporal reasoning within VLA policies for robotic manipulation. Problem Addressed: Current VLA systems struggle because they process only single frames without spatial detail and lack memory of past context, causing them to forget occluded objects and lose spatial orientation. Solution: AnchorVLA4D augments visual input with an anchor image to preserve initial scene context and adds a spatial encoder to reveal geometric relationships throughout manipulation episodes. Technical Details: Built on a Qwen2.5-VL backbone with diffusion-based action generation, it requires no additional sensing modalities and introduces minimal computational overhead. Results: The approach achieved a 13.6% improvement on the Simpler WidowX benchmark and demonstrated an 80% average success rate on real-world robotic tasks.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Interesting — VLA with spatial-temporal reasoning for manipulation

title: Ada3Drift: Adaptive Training-Time Drifting for One-Step 3D Visuomotor Robotic Manipulation
authors: C Xu et al
matched_interests: Diffusion models|Verification of visuomotor policies|Imitation learning
headline: Training-time diffusion drifting enables single-step multimodal visuomotor policy generation
summary: This paper introduces Ada3Drift, addressing the speed-fidelity trade-off in diffusion-based visuomotor policies. While single-step flow matching methods are fast but collapse multimodal behaviors, iterative diffusion policies are slow. Ada3Drift shifts refinement from inference to training via a learned drifting field that attracts actions toward expert modes while repelling alternatives. The approach achieves state-of-the-art single-step generation (1 NFE) from 3D point clouds with 10x fewer function evaluations than diffusion baselines.
abstract: Diffusion-based visuomotor policies effectively capture multimodal action distributions through iterative denoising, but their high inference latency limits real-time robotic control. Recent flow matching and consistency-based methods achieve single-step generation, yet sacrifice the ability to preserve distinct action modes, collapsing multimodal behaviors into averaged, often physically infeasible trajectories. We observe that the compute budget asymmetry in robotics (offline training vs. real-time inference) naturally motivates recovering this multimodal fidelity by shifting iterative refinement from inference time to training time. Building on this insight, we propose Ada3Drift, which learns a training-time drifting field that attracts predicted actions toward expert demonstration modes while repelling them from other generated samples, enabling high-fidelity single-step generation (1 NFE) from 3D point cloud observations. To handle the few-shot robotic regime, Ada3Drift further introduces a sigmoid-scheduled loss transition from coarse distribution learning to mode-sharpening refinement, and multi-scale field aggregation that captures action modes at varying spatial granularities. Experiments on three simulation benchmarks (Adroit, Meta-World, and RoboTwin) and real-world robotic manipulation tasks demonstrate that Ada3Drift achieves state-of-the-art performance while requiring 10× fewer function evaluations than diffusion-based alternatives.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — flow-matching style single-step generation for visuomotor policies

title: DiffusionAnything: End-to-End In-context Diffusion Learning for Unified Navigation and Pre-Grasp Motion
authors: I Zhura et al
headline: Unified diffusion policy handles multi-scale navigation and manipulation with minimal task data
summary: This paper presents DiffusionAnything, a unified image-space diffusion policy addressing both meter-scale navigation and centimeter-scale manipulation with multi-scale feature modulation. The framework requires only 5 minutes of self-supervised data per task and incorporates three key innovations: multi-scale FiLM conditioning, trajectory-aligned depth prediction, and self-supervised attention from AnyTraverse. Operating purely from RGB input with minimal memory (2.0 GB, 10 Hz), the approach achieves robust zero-shot generalization to novel scenes.
abstract: Efficiently predicting motion plans directly from vision remains a fundamental challenge in robotics, where planning typically requires explicit goal specification and task-specific design. Recent vision-language-action (VLA) models infer actions directly from visual input but demand massive computational resources, extensive training data, and fail zero-shot in novel scenes. We present a unified image-space diffusion policy handling both meter-scale navigation and centimeter-scale manipulation via multi-scale feature modulation, with only 5 minutes of self-supervised data per task. Three key innovations drive the framework: (1) Multi-scale FiLM conditioning on task mode, depth scale, and spatial attention enables task-appropriate behavior in a single model; (2) trajectory-aligned depth prediction focuses metric 3D reasoning along generated waypoints; (3) self-supervised attention from AnyTraverse enables goal-directed inference without vision-language models and depth sensors. Operating purely from RGB input (2.0 GB memory, 10 Hz), the model achieves robust zero-shot generalization to novel scenes while remaining suitable for onboard deployment.
notes: Alert: Majumdar citation. High — unified diffusion policy for navigation and manipulation

title: Towards Practical World Model-based Reinforcement Learning for Vision-Language-Action Models
authors: Z Zhang et al
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Reinforcement learning|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: World model RL enables sample-efficient VLA training with interleaved view consistency
summary: This paper introduces VLA-MBPO, a framework for efficient reinforcement learning of vision-language-action models within interactive world models. The approach adapts unified multimodal models for world modeling, implements interleaved view decoding for multi-view consistency, and uses chunk-level branched rollout to reduce error compounding. Theoretical analysis and experiments demonstrate substantial improvements in policy performance and sample efficiency across simulated and real-world robotic tasks.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models show strong generalization for robotic control, but finetuning them with reinforcement learning (RL) is constrained by the high cost and safety risks of real-world interaction. The researchers introduce VLA-MBPO, a framework addressing challenges in training VLA models within interactive world models. The approach incorporates three main strategies: adapting unified multimodal models for efficient world modeling, implementing an interleaved view decoding mechanism for multi-view consistency, and utilizing chunk-level branched rollout to reduce error compounding. The authors demonstrate through theoretical analysis and experimentation that their method substantially enhances policy performance and sample efficiency across both simulated and real-world robotic tasks.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — world model RL for VLA policy improvement

title: Simulation Distillation: Pretraining World Models in Simulation for Rapid Real-World Adaptation
authors: J Levy et al
headline: Sim-to-real world models distill reward/value priors for fast real-world adaptation
summary: This paper introduces Simulation Distillation (SimDist), a sim-to-real framework that transfers world models from simulation into latent space representations. The approach distills reward and value models directly from simulation, providing dense planning signals without requiring value learning during real-world deployment. Real-world adaptation reduces to short-horizon system identification, avoiding long-horizon credit assignment problems, and demonstrates substantial improvements in data efficiency and stability on manipulation and locomotion tasks.
abstract: Simulation-to-real transfer remains a central challenge in robotics, as mismatches between simulated and real-world dynamics often lead to failures. While reinforcement learning offers a principled mechanism for adaptation, existing sim-to-real finetuning methods struggle with exploration and long-horizon credit assignment in the low-data regimes typical of real-world robotics. We introduce Simulation Distillation (SimDist), a sim-to-real framework that distills structural priors from a simulator into a latent world model and enables rapid real-world adaptation via online planning and supervised dynamics finetuning. By transferring reward and value models directly from simulation, SimDist provides dense planning signals from raw perception without requiring value learning during deployment. As a result, real-world adaptation reduces to short-horizon system identification, avoiding long-horizon credit assignment and enabling fast, stable improvement. Across precise manipulation and quadruped locomotion tasks, SimDist substantially outperforms prior methods in data efficiency, stability, and final performance.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — world models for sim-to-real transfer and rapid adaptation

title: Verifiable Error Bounds for Physics-Informed Neural Network Solutions of Lyapunov and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Equations
authors: J Liu
headline: PINNs for HJB equations yield verifiable error bounds enabling certified optimal control
summary: This paper establishes verifiable error bounds for Physics-Informed Neural Networks approximating Lyapunov and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equations. Key contributions include demonstrating that small PDE residuals yield relative error bounds, providing computable error estimates, and establishing certified bounds on optimal value functions. One-sided residual bounds ensure the PINN approximation constitutes a valid Lyapunov or control Lyapunov function, enabling certified learning-based optimal control.
abstract: Many core problems in nonlinear systems analysis and control can be recast as solving partial differential equations (PDEs) such as Lyapunov and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equations. The paper presents verifiable error bounds for Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) approximations of Lyapunov and HJB equations.
