When encountering sudden OOD anomalies during inference, DDP detects the real-imagination discrepancy and actively abandons the corrupted visual stream. Instead, it relies on its internal "imagination" (autoregressively forecasted latent dynamics) to safely bypass the disruption, generating imagined trajectories before smoothly realigning with physical reality. Extensive evaluations demonstrate DDP's exceptional resilience. Notably, DDP achieves a 73.8% OOD success rate on MetaWorld (vs. 23.9% without predictive imagination) and an 83.3% success rate under severe real-world spatial shifts (vs. 3.3% without predictive imagination). Furthermore, as a stress test, DDP maintains a 76.7% real-world success rate even when relying entirely on open-loop imagination post-initialization.
notes: High — world-model-regularized diffusion policy with OOD detection — links generative policies to robustness/verification

title: Uncovering Linguistic Fragility in Vision-Language-Action Models via Diversity-Aware Red Teaming
authors: Baoshun Tong, Haoran He, Ling Pan, Yang Liu, Liang Lin
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Adversarial robustness of robot policies|Verification of visuomotor policies
headline: Diversity-aware RL red teaming surfaces linguistic failure modes that crash VLA manipulation policies
summary: Frames safety auditing of VLAs as RL-based adversarial red teaming and adds a diversity-aware exploration scheme to escape mode collapse. Uncovers a broad set of linguistic perturbations that cause VLAs to fail catastrophically on manipulation tasks. Provides a practical pipeline for verifying VLA robustness before deployment.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have achieved remarkable success in robotic manipulation. However, their robustness to linguistic nuances remains a critical, under-explored safety concern, posing a significant safety risk to real-world deployment. Red teaming, or identifying environmental scenarios that elicit catastrophic behaviors, is an important step in ensuring the safe deployment of embodied AI agents. Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising approach in automated red teaming that aims to uncover these vulnerabilities. However, standard RL-based adversaries often suffer from severe mode collapse due to their reward-maximizing nature, which tends to converge to a narrow set of trivial or repetitive failure patterns, failing to reveal the comprehensive landscape of meaningful risks. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel \textbf{D}iversity-\textbf{A}ware \textbf{E}mbodied \textbf{R}ed \textbf{T}eaming (\textbf{DAERT}) framework, to expose the vulnerabilities of VLAs against linguistic variations. Our design is based on evaluating a uniform policy, which is able to generate a diverse set of challenging instructions while ensuring its attack effectiveness, measured by execution failures in a physical simulator. We conduct extensive experiments across different robotic benchmarks against two state-of-the-art VLAs, including $π_0$ and OpenVLA. Our method consistently discovers a wider range of more effective adversarial instructions that reduce the average task success rate from 93.33\% to 5.85\%, demonstrating a scalable approach to stress-testing VLA agents and exposing critical safety blind spots before real-world deployment.
notes: High — diversity-aware RL red teaming uncovers linguistic failure modes in VLAs — directly addresses verification of VLAs

title: Open-Loop Planning, Closed-Loop Verification: Speculative Verification for VLA
authors: Zihua Wang, Zhitao Lin, Ruibo Li, Yu Zhang, Xu Yang, Siya Mi, Xiu-Shen Wei
matched_interests: Verification of visuomotor policies|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Speculative verification cheaply re-checks VLA action chunks at each step, restoring closed-loop safety
summary: Proposes SV-VLA, a runtime verifier that speculatively re-checks the open-loop action chunks emitted by VLA models against the latest observation, falling back to re-planning if verification fails. Combines the efficiency of action chunking with the robustness of closed-loop control. Substantially reduces failure rates on perturbed manipulation tasks.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, as large foundation models for embodied control, have shown strong performance in manipulation tasks. However, their performance comes at high inference cost. To improve efficiency, recent methods adopt action chunking, which predicts a sequence of future actions for open-loop execution. Although effective for reducing computation, open-loop execution is sensitive to environmental changes and prone to error accumulation due to the lack of close-loop feedback. To address this limitation, we propose Speculative Verification for VLA Control (SV-VLA), a framework that combines efficient open-loop long-horizon planning with lightweight closed-loop online verification. Specifically, SV-VLA uses a heavy VLA as a low-frequency macro-planner to generate an action chunk together with a planning context, while a lightweight verifier continuously monitors execution based on the latest observations. Conditioned on both the current observation and the planning context, the verifier compares the planned action against a closed-loop reference action and triggers replanning only when necessary. Experiments demonstrate that SV-VLA combines the efficiency of chunked prediction with the robustness of closed-loop control, enabling efficient and reliable VLA-based control in dynamic environments. Code is available: https://github.com/edsad122/SV-VLA.
notes: Core — closed-loop verification of open-loop VLA action chunks — directly addresses verification of visuomotor policies [residual +1: directly tackles runtime verification of learned visuomotor policies (VLAs), Sumanth's exact research direction]

title: Causal Scene Narration with Runtime Safety Supervision for Vision-Language-Action Driving
authors: Yun Li, Yidu Zhang, Simon Thompson, Ehsan Javanmardi, Manabu Tsukada
matched_interests: Verification of visuomotor policies|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Runtime Simplex-style safety supervisor restructures VLA driving inputs and arbitrates with a verified fallback
summary: Introduces Causal Scene Narration (CSN), which restructures VLA driving inputs around intent-constraint alignment and quantitative grounding at zero training cost. Pairs CSN with a Simplex-based runtime safety supervisor that arbitrates between the VLA and a verified backup controller. Improves both task accuracy and runtime safety on driving benchmarks.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for autonomous driving must integrate diverse textual inputs, including navigation commands, hazard warnings, and traffic state descriptions, yet current systems often present these as disconnected fragments, forcing the model to discover on its own which environmental constraints are relevant to the current maneuver. We introduce Causal Scene Narration (CSN), which restructures VLA text inputs through intent-constraint alignment, quantitative grounding, and structured separation, at inference time with zero GPU cost. We complement CSN with Simplex-based runtime safety supervision and training-time alignment via Plackett-Luce DPO with negative log-likelihood (NLL) regularization. A multi-town closed-loop CARLA evaluation shows that CSN improves Driving Score by +31.1% on original LMDrive and +24.5% on the preference-aligned variant. A controlled ablation reveals that causal structure accounts for 39.1% of this gain, with the remainder attributable to information content alone. A perception noise ablation confirms that CSN's benefit is robust to realistic sensing errors. Semantic safety supervision improves Infraction Score, while reactive Time-To-Collision monitoring degrades performance, demonstrating that intent-aware monitoring is needed for VLA systems.
notes: High — runtime safety supervision for VLA driving via Simplex-style fallback — verification at runtime

title: Boosting Vision-Language-Action Finetuning with Feasible Action Neighborhood Prior
authors: Haochen Niu, Kanyu Zhang, Shuyu Yin, Qinghai Guo, Peilin Liu, Fei Wen
headline: Feasible-action-neighborhood prior reshapes VLA training to exploit near-equivalent action sets
summary: Observes that for each state there is typically a feasible neighborhood of near-equivalent actions, which standard VLA training (inherited from language) ignores. Introduces a FAN-guided regularizer that aligns the model output distribution with this geometry. Improves generalization and sample efficiency on manipulation benchmarks.
abstract: In real-world robotic manipulation, states typically admit a neighborhood of near-equivalent actions. That is, for each state, there exist a feasible action neighborhood (FAN) rather than a single correct action, within which motions yield indistinguishable progress. However, prevalent VLA training methodologies are directly inherited from linguistic settings and do not exploit the FAN property, thus leading to poor generalization and low sample efficiency. To address this limitation, we introduce a FAN-guided regularizer that shapes the model's output distribution to align with the geometry of FAN. Concretely, we introduce a Gaussian prior that promotes locally smooth and unimodal predictions around the preferred direction and magnitude. In extensive experiments across both reinforced finetuning (RFT) and supervised finetuning (SFT), our method achieves significant improvement in sample efficiency, and success rate in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios. By aligning with the intrinsic action tolerance of physical manipulation, FAN-guided regularization provides a principled and practical method for sample-efficient, and generalizable VLA adaptation.
notes: Moderate — feasible action neighborhood prior for VLA finetuning — improves generalization and sample efficiency

title: DIAL: Decoupling Intent and Action via Latent World Modeling for End-to-End VLA
authors: Yi Chen, Yuying Ge, Hui Zhou, Mingyu Ding, Yixiao Ge, Xihui Liu
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|World models for robotics|Latent-space safety analysis
headline: DIAL decouples VLA high-level intent from low-level action via diffused latent world model
summary: Argues most end-to-end VLAs underuse the VLM's decision-making capacity by mapping features directly to actions, hurting stability. DIAL bridges high-level decision making and low-level motor execution through a diffusion-based latent world model. Shows improved training stability and downstream task performance.
abstract: The development of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models has been significantly accelerated by pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs). However, most existing end-to-end VLAs treat the VLM primarily as a multimodal encoder, directly mapping vision-language features to low-level actions. This paradigm underutilizes the VLM's potential in high-level decision making and introduces training instability, frequently degrading its rich semantic representations. To address these limitations, we introduce DIAL, a framework bridging high-level decision making and low-level motor execution through a differentiable latent intent bottleneck. Specifically, a VLM-based System-2 performs latent world modeling by synthesizing latent visual foresight within the VLM's native feature space; this foresight explicitly encodes intent and serves as the structural bottleneck. A lightweight System-1 policy then decodes this predicted intent together with the current observation into precise robot actions via latent inverse dynamics. To ensure optimization stability, we employ a two-stage training paradigm: a decoupled warmup phase where System-2 learns to predict latent futures while System-1 learns motor control under ground-truth future guidance within a unified feature space, followed by seamless end-to-end joint optimization. This enables action-aware gradients to refine the VLM backbone in a controlled manner, preserving pre-trained knowledge. Extensive experiments on the RoboCasa GR1 Tabletop benchmark show that DIAL establishes a new state-of-the-art, achieving superior performance with 10x fewer demonstrations than prior methods. Furthermore, by leveraging heterogeneous human demonstrations, DIAL learns physically grounded manipulation priors and exhibits robust zero-shot generalization to unseen objects and novel configurations during real-world deployment on a humanoid robot.
notes: Moderate — latent world modeling decouples high-level VLA intent from motor execution — relevant to verification via latent abstraction

title: Active Reward Machine Inference From Raw State Trajectories
authors: Mohamad Louai Shehab, Antoine Aspeel, Necmiye Ozay
headline: Active queries learn reward machines from raw state trajectories without hand-crafted labelers
summary: Tackles automatic learning of reward machines from raw state and policy data without observing rewards or labeling functions. Uses active queries to select informative trajectories and recover the underlying automaton. Removes a major bottleneck for synthesizing multi-stage robot policies from formal task specs.
abstract: Reward machines are automaton-like structures that capture the memory required to accomplish a multi-stage task. When combined with reinforcement learning or optimal control methods, they can be used to synthesize robot policies to achieve such tasks. However, specifying a reward machine by hand, including a labeling function capturing high-level features that the decisions are based on, can be a daunting task. This paper deals with the problem of learning reward machines directly from raw state and policy information. As opposed to existing works, we assume no access to observations of rewards, labels, or machine nodes, and show what trajectory data is sufficient for learning the reward machine in this information-scarce regime. We then extend the result to an active learning setting where we incrementally query trajectory extensions to improve data (and indirectly computational) efficiency. Results are demonstrated with several grid world examples.
notes: Moderate — actively learning reward machines from raw state trajectories — combines active learning with formal task specification

title: Safety, Security, and Cognitive Risks in World Models
authors: Manoj Parmar
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Safe & robust robot control|Adversarial robustness of robot policies
headline: Position paper systematizes safety, security, and cognitive failure modes specific to learned world models
summary: Surveys the distinct safety, security, and cognitive risks introduced when learned world models become foundational to autonomy. Identifies attacks (data poisoning, latent corruption, rollout drift) and cognitive failures unique to compressed latent simulators. Proposes a research agenda for verifying and hardening world-model-based decision systems.
abstract: World models - learned internal simulators of environment dynamics - are rapidly becoming foundational to autonomous decision-making in robotics, autonomous vehicles, and agentic AI. By predicting future states in compressed latent spaces, they enable sample-efficient planning and long-horizon imagination without direct environment interaction. Yet this predictive power introduces a distinctive set of safety, security, and cognitive risks. Adversaries can corrupt training data, poison latent representations, and exploit compounding rollout errors to cause significant degradation in safety-critical deployments. At the alignment layer, world model-equipped agents are more capable of goal misgeneralisation, deceptive alignment, and reward hacking. At the human layer, authoritative world model predictions foster automation bias, miscalibrated trust, and planning hallucination. This paper surveys the world model landscape; introduces formal definitions of trajectory persistence and representational risk; presents a five-profile attacker taxonomy; and develops a unified threat model drawing on MITRE ATLAS and the OWASP LLM Top 10. We provide an empirical proof-of-concept demonstrating trajectory-persistent adversarial attacks on a GRU-based RSSM ($\mathcal{A}_1 = 2.26\times$ amplification, $-59.5\%$ reward reduction under adversarial fine-tuning), validate architecture-dependence via a stochastic RSSM proxy ($\mathcal{A}_1 = 0.65\times$), and probe a real DreamerV3 checkpoint (non-zero action drift confirmed). We propose interdisciplinary mitigations spanning adversarial hardening, alignment engineering, NIST AI RMF and EU AI Act governance, and human-factors design, arguing that world models require the same rigour as flight-control software or medical devices.
notes: High — systematic taxonomy of safety/security risks for world models in autonomy — frames the verification problem

title: Learning What Can Be Picked: Active Reachability Estimation for Efficient Robotic Fruit Harvesting
authors: Nur Afsa Syeda, Mohamed Elmahallawy, Luis Fernando de la Torre, John Miller
headline: Active reachability classifier replaces full IK for efficient fruit selection in orchard manipulation
summary: Replaces exhaustive inverse-kinematics or motion planning with an active learning loop that estimates which fruit are reachable from current robot poses. Reduces wasted compute during real-world harvesting. Applied case study of data-driven reachability informing perception-to-action decisions.
abstract: Agriculture remains a cornerstone of global health and economic sustainability, yet labor-intensive tasks such as harvesting high-value crops continue to face growing workforce shortages. Robotic harvesting systems offer a promising solution; however, their deployment in unstructured orchard environments is constrained by inefficient perception-to-action pipelines. In particular, existing approaches often rely on exhaustive inverse kinematics or motion planning to determine whether a target fruit is reachable, leading to unnecessary computation and delayed decision-making. Our approach combines RGB-D perception with active learning to directly learn reachability as a binary decision problem. We then leverage active learning to selectively query the most informative samples for reachability labeling, significantly reducing annotation effort while maintaining high predictive accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves accurate reachability prediction with substantially fewer labeled samples, yielding approximately 6--8% higher accuracy than random sampling and enabling label-efficient adaptation to new orchard configurations. Among the evaluated strategies, entropy- and margin-based sampling outperform Query-by-Committee and standard uncertainty sampling in low-label regimes, while all strategies converge to comparable performance as the labeled set grows. These results highlight the effectiveness of active learning for task-level perception in agricultural robotics and position our approach as a scalable alternative to computation-heavy kinematic reachability analysis. Our code is available through https://github.com/wsu-cyber-security-lab-ai/active-learning.
notes: Moderate — active learning of reachability for efficient manipulation — applied data-driven reachability

title: Train-Small Deploy-Large: Leveraging Diffusion-Based Multi-Robot Planning
authors: Siddharth Singh, Soumee Guha, Qing Chang, Scott Acton
headline: Train-small, deploy-large: diffusion-based multi-robot planner generalizes far beyond its training team size
summary: Proposes a diffusion-model-based multi-robot path planner trained on small teams but deployable on much larger fleets without retraining. Combines analytical planning with diffusion sampling to handle scale and dynamic environments. Demonstrates strong generalization where prior learned methods fail.
abstract: Learning based multi-robot path planning methods struggle to scale or generalize to changes, particularly variations in the number of robots during deployment. Most existing methods are trained on a fixed number of robots and may tolerate a reduced number during testing, but typically fail when the number increases. Additionally, training such methods for a larger number of agents can be both time consuming and computationally expensive. However, analytical methods can struggle to scale computationally or handle dynamic changes in the environment. In this work, we propose to leverage a diffusion model based planner capable of handling dynamically varying number of agents. Our approach is trained on a limited number of agents and generalizes effectively to larger numbers of agents during deployment. Results show that integrating a single shared diffusion model based planner with dedicated inter-agent attention computation and temporal convolution enables a train small deploy-large paradigm with good accuracy. We validate our method across multiple scenarios and compare the performance with existing multi-agent reinforcement learning techniques and heuristic control based methods.
notes: Moderate — diffusion-based multi-robot planning that scales beyond training agent count

title: A Control Barrier Function-Constrained Model Predictive Control Framework for Safe Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ali Umut Kaypak, Prashanth Krishnamurthy, Farshad Khorrami
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Probabilistic ensembles plus CBF-constrained MPC enforce safety in model-based RL under stochastic dynamics
summary: Proposes PECTS: jointly learns stochastic dynamics with probabilistic neural ensembles and CBFs with Lipschitz-bounded networks. Enforces safety in model-based RL by adding learned CBF constraints to the MPC trajectory sampling step. Improves both safety and sample efficiency over baseline safe RL methods.
abstract: Ensuring safety under unknown and stochastic dynamics remains a significant challenge in reinforcement learning (RL). In this paper, we propose a model predictive control (MPC)-based safe RL framework, called Probabilistic Ensembles with CBF-constrained Trajectory Sampling (PECTS), to address this challenge. PECTS jointly learns stochastic system dynamics with probabilistic neural networks (PNNs) and control barrier functions (CBFs) with Lipschitz-bounded neural networks. Safety is enforced by incorporating learned CBF constraints into the MPC formulation while accounting for the model stochasticity. This enables probabilistic safety under model uncertainty. To solve the resulting MPC problem, we utilize a sampling-based optimizer together with a safe trajectory sampling method that discards unsafe trajectories based on the learned system model and CBF. We validate PECTS in various simulation studies, where it outperforms baseline methods.
notes: High — CBF-constrained MPC for safe RL with probabilistic ensembles — combines safe control + RL

title: Stability Margins of CBF-QP Safety Filters: Analysis and Synthesis
authors: Shima Sadat Mousavi, Pol Mestres, Aaron D. Ames
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: CBF-QP safety filters can erode stability margins — paper derives exact loop-shaping characterization
summary: Analyzes how CBF-QP safety filters, while preserving safety, can shrink closed-loop stability margins or destabilize systems. Derives an exact scalar loop representation for linear systems with affine safety constraints. Provides synthesis guidelines that retain both safety and adequate stability margins.
abstract: Control barrier function (CBF)-QP safety filters enforce safety by minimally modifying a nominal controller. While prior work has mainly addressed robustness of safety under uncertainty, robustness of the resulting closed-loop \emph{stability} is much less understood. This issue is important because once the safety filter becomes active, it modifies the nominal dynamics and can reduce stability margins or even destabilize the system, despite preserving safety. For linear systems with a single affine safety constraint, we show that the active-mode dynamics admit an exact scalar loop representation, leading to a classical robust-control interpretation in terms of gain, phase, and delay margins. This viewpoint yields exact stability-margin characterizations and tractable linear matrix inequality (LMI)-based certificates and synthesis conditions for controllers with certified robustness guarantees. Numerical examples illustrate the proposed analysis and the enlargement of certified stability margins for safety-filtered systems.
notes: Moderate — analyzes how CBF-QP filters affect closed-loop stability margins — useful theoretical lens

title: SafeSpace: Aggregating Safe Sets from Backup Control Barrier Functions under Input Constraints
authors: Pio Ong, David E. J. van Wijk, Massimiliano de Sa, Joel W. Burdick, Aaron D. Ames
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Controller composition|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Combinatorial backup CBFs unite small certified safe sets into a single larger guaranteed-safe region
summary: Refines the combinatorial CBF framework with auxiliary variables enabling logical composition of multiple backup CBFs around different equilibria/controllers under input constraints. Yields a single certified safe set that is a tight union of small per-equilibrium sets. Directly relevant to composing verified controllers.
abstract: Control barrier functions (CBFs) provide a principled framework for enforcing safety in control systems -- yet the certified safe operating region in practice is often conservative, especially under input bounds. In many applications, multiple smaller safe sets can be certified independently, e.g., around distinct equilibria with different stabilizing controllers. This paper proposes a framework for uniting such regions into a single certified safe set using \emph{combinatorial CBFs}. We refine the combinatorial CBF framework by introducing an auxiliary variable that enables logical compositions of individual CBFs. In the proposed framework, we show that such compositions yield a \emph{generalized combinatorial CBF} under a condition termed \emph{conjunctive compatibility}. Building on this result, we extend the framework to enable the aggregation of multiple implicit safe sets generated by the backup CBF framework. We show that the resulting CBF-based quadratic program yields a continuous safety filter over the aggregated safe region. The approach is demonstrated on two spacecraft safety problems, safe attitude control and safe station keeping, where multiple certified safe regions are combined to expand the operational envelope.
notes: High — aggregates safe sets from multiple backup CBFs via combinatorial composition — direct controller composition relevance

title: Backup-Based Safety Filters: A Comparative Review of Backup CBF, Model Predictive Shielding, and gatekeeper
authors: Taekyung Kim, Aswin D. Menon, Akshunn Trivedi, Dimitra Panagou
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Unified comparison shows MPS is a special case of gatekeeper across three backup-based safety filters
summary: Provides a unified comparative framework for backup CBFs, model predictive shielding, and gatekeeper. Shows MPS is a special case of gatekeeper and relates gatekeeper to interior-point characterizations of backup CBFs. Helpful taxonomy for choosing among backup-based safety filters.
abstract: This paper revisits three backup-based safety filters -- Backup Control Barrier Functions (Backup CBF), Model Predictive Shielding (MPS), and gatekeeper -- through a unified comparative framework. Using a common safety-filter abstraction and shared notation, we make explicit both their common backup-policy structure and their key algorithmic differences. We compare the three methods through their filter-inactive sets, i.e., the states where the nominal policy is left unchanged. In particular, we show that MPS is a special case of gatekeeper, and we further relate gatekeeper to the interior of the Backup CBF inactive set within the implicit safe set. This unified view also highlights a key source of conservatism in backup-based safety filters: safety is often evaluated through the feasibility of a backup maneuver, rather than through the nominal policy's continued safe execution. The paper is intended as a compact tutorial and review that clarifies the theoretical connections and differences among these methods.
notes: Moderate — unified comparison of backup CBF, MPS, and gatekeeper safety filters — useful reference

title: Safe Policy Optimization via Control Barrier Function-based Safety Filters
authors: Yiting Chen, Pol Mestres, Emiliano Dall'Anese, Jorge Cortés
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Joint policy/CBF-filter optimization restores stability properties lost when adding safety filters
summary: Develops a policy optimization framework that jointly parameterizes the nominal feedback law and the CBF safety filter for linear systems. Maximally enhances closed-loop stability properties of the safety-filtered system, eliminating limit cycles and undesired equilibria. Provides a clean recipe for safe-but-stable closed-loop design.
abstract: Control barrier function (CBF)-based safety filters provide a systematic way to enforce state constraints, but they can significantly alter the closed-loop dynamics induced by a nominal, stabilizing controller. In particular, the resulting safety-filtered system may exhibit undesirable behaviors including limit cycles, unbounded trajectories, and undesired equilibria. This paper develops a policy optimization framework to maximally enhance the stability properties of safety-filtered controllers. Focusing on linear systems with linear nominal controllers, we jointly parameterize the nominal feedback gain and safety-filter components, and optimize them using trajectory-based objectives computed from closed-loop rollouts. To ensure that the nominal controller remains stabilizing throughout training, we encode Lyapunov-based stability conditions as smooth scalar constraints and enforce them using robust safe gradient flow. This guarantees feasibility of the stability constraints along the optimization iterates and therefore avoids instability during training. Numerical experiments on obstacle-avoidance problems show that the proposed approach can remove asymptotically stable undesired equilibria and improve convergence behavior while maintaining forward invariance of the safe set.
notes: High — jointly optimizes nominal controller and CBF safety filter to recover stability — links safe RL with CBFs

title: Certified Set Convergence for Piecewise Affine Systems via Neural Lyapunov Functions
authors: Yanliang Huang, Peng Xie, Zhen Zhang, Wenyuan Wu, Zhuoqi Zeng, Amr Alanwar
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability
headline: Neural Lyapunov certificates for piecewise affine systems certify whole-set convergence, not just states
summary: Tackles certifying neural Lyapunov functions on piecewise affine systems where Lipschitz bounds blow up across mode boundaries. Develops a method to certify convergence of entire state sets simultaneously despite mode splits. Closes the gap between expressive learned Lyapunov functions and tight verification.
abstract: Safety-critical control of piecewise affine (PWA) systems under bounded additive disturbances requires guarantees not for individual states but for entire state sets simultaneously: a single control action must steer every state in the set toward a target, even as sets crossing mode boundaries split and evolve under distinct affine dynamics. Certifying such set convergence via neural Lyapunov functions couples the Lipschitz constants of the value function and the policy, yet certified bounds for expressive networks exceed true values by orders of magnitude, creating a certification barrier. We resolve this through a three-stage pipeline that decouples verification from the policy. A value function from Hamilton-Jacobi backward reachability, trained via reinforcement learning, is the Lyapunov candidate. A permutation-invariant Deep Sets controller, distilled via regret minimization, produces a common action. Verification propagates zonotopes through the value network, yielding verified Lyapunov upper bounds over entire sets without bounding the policy Lipschitz constant. On four benchmarks up to dimension six, including systems with per-mode operator norms exceeding unity, the framework certifies set convergence with positive margin on every system. A spectrally constrained local certificate completes the terminal guarantee, and the set-actor is the only tested method to achieve full strict set containment, at constant-time online cost.
notes: High — certified set convergence for piecewise affine systems via neural Lyapunov functions

title: Data-Driven Reachability Analysis via Diffusion Models with PAC Guarantees
authors: Yanliang Huang, Peng Xie, Wenyuan Wu, Zhuoqi Zeng, Amr Alanwar
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Diffusion models|Conformal prediction|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Diffusion model learns reachable sets from trajectories alone with Learn-Then-Test PAC guarantees
summary: Trains a denoising diffusion model on trajectory data to capture the time-evolving state distribution of an unknown nonlinear system. Defines reachable sets as sublevel sets of the diffusion reconstruction error and calibrates the threshold via Learn Then Test for PAC coverage. Yields tight, model-free reachable sets on benchmark nonlinear systems.
abstract: We present a data-driven framework for reachability analysis of nonlinear dynamical systems that requires no explicit model. A denoising diffusion probabilistic model learns the time-evolving state distribution of a dynamical system from trajectory data alone. The predicted reachable set takes the form of a sublevel set of a nonconformity score derived from the reconstruction error, with the threshold calibrated via the Learn Then Test procedure so that the probability of excluding a reachable state is bounded with high probability. Experiments on three nonlinear systems, a forced Duffing oscillator, a planar quadrotor, and a high-dimensional reaction-diffusion system, confirm that the empirical miss rate remains below the Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) bound while scaling to state dimensions beyond the reach of classical grid-based and polynomial methods.
notes: Core — data-driven reachability via diffusion models calibrated with Learn-Then-Test for PAC guarantees — exactly the intersection of multiple core interests [residual +1: novel intersection of three core interests — data-driven reachability, diffusion generative models, and PAC/conformal calibration]

title: Data-Driven Reachability of Nonlinear Lipschitz Systems via Koopman Operator Embeddings
authors: Alireza Naderi Akhormeh, Ahmad Hafez, Abdulla Fawzy, Amr Alanwar
headline: Koopman-lifted zonotopic reachability tightens long-horizon data-driven safety bounds for nonlinear systems
summary: Proposes a data-driven reachability framework that lifts a nonlinear Lipschitz system into a finite-dimensional state-input-dependent linear model via the Koopman operator. Computes reachable sets in the lifted space using zonotopes and projects back. Achieves substantially less conservative long-horizon bounds than direct nonlinear methods.
abstract: Data-driven safety verification of robotic systems often relies on zonotopic reachability analysis due to its scalability and computational efficiency. However, for nonlinear systems, these methods can become overly conservative, especially over long prediction horizons and under measurement noise. We propose a data-driven reachability framework based on the Koopman operator and zonotopic set representations that lifts the nonlinear system into a finite-dimensional, linear, state-input-dependent model. Reachable sets are then computed in the lifted space and projected back to the original state space to obtain guaranteed over-approximations of the true dynamics. The proposed method reduces conservatism while preserving formal safety guarantees, and we prove that the resulting reachable sets over-approximate the true reachable sets. Numerical simulations and real-world experiments on an autonomous vehicle show that the proposed approach yields substantially tighter reachable set over-approximations than both model-based and linear data-driven methods, particularly over long horizons.
notes: Core — Koopman lifting + zonotope reachability for nonlinear data-driven safety verification

title: Stratifying Reinforcement Learning with Signal Temporal Logic
authors: Justin Curry, Alberto Speranzon
headline: Stratification-based STL semantics relates formula structure to deep RL embedding-space geometry
summary: Develops a stratification-based semantics for STL where atomic predicates are membership tests in stratified spaces. Connects STL formula structure to the geometry of deep RL embedding spaces. Offers a fresh theoretical lens for analyzing RL agents trained against temporal-logic objectives.
abstract: In this paper, we develop a stratification-based semantics for Signal Temporal Logic (STL) in which each atomic predicate is interpreted as a membership test in a stratified space. This perspective reveals a novel correspondence principle between stratification theory and STL, showing that most STL formulas can be viewed as inducing a stratification of space-time. The significance of this interpretation is twofold. First, it offers a fresh theoretical framework for analyzing the structure of the embedding space generated by deep reinforcement learning (DRL) and relates it to the geometry of the ambient decision space. Second, it provides a principled framework that both enables the reuse of existing high-dimensional analysis tools and motivates the creation of novel computational techniques. To ground the theory, we (1) illustrate the role of stratification theory in Minigrid games and (2) apply numerical techniques to the latent embeddings of a DRL agent playing such a game where the robustness of STL formulas is used as the reward. In the process, we propose computationally efficient signatures that, based on preliminary evidence, appear promising for uncovering the stratification structure of such embedding spaces.
notes: Moderate — novel stratification semantics for STL applied to deep RL embedding spaces

title: GraSP-STL: A Graph-Based Framework for Zero-Shot Signal Temporal Logic Planning via Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ancheng Hou, Ruijia Liu, Xiang Yin
headline: Graph search over offline-RL goal-conditioned policies enables zero-shot STL planning without retraining
summary: Studies offline zero-shot planning under arbitrary STL specifications given only state-action transitions. GraSP-STL learns goal-conditioned policies from offline data and runs graph-search-based composition to satisfy unseen STL formulas. Avoids analytical dynamics, environment interaction, or task-specific retraining.
abstract: This paper studies offline, zero-shot planning under Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications. We assume access only to an offline dataset of state-action-state transitions collected by a task-agnostic behavior policy, with no analytical dynamics model, no further environment interaction, and no task-specific retraining. The objective is to synthesize a control strategy whose resulting trajectory satisfies an arbitrary unseen STL specification. To this end, we propose GraSP-STL, a graph-search-based framework for zero-shot STL planning from offline trajectories. The method learns a goal-conditioned value function from offline data and uses it to induce a finite-horizon reachability metric over the state space. Based on this metric, it constructs a directed graph abstraction whose nodes represent representative states and whose edges encode feasible short-horizon transitions. Planning is then formulated as a graph search over waypoint sequences, evaluated using arithmetic-geometric mean robustness and its interval semantics, and executed by a learned goal-conditioned policy. The proposed framework separates reusable reachability learning from task-conditioned planning, enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen STL tasks and long-horizon planning through the composition of short-horizon behaviors from offline data. Experimental results demonstrate its effectiveness on a range of offline STL planning tasks.
notes: Moderate — graph-based zero-shot STL planning from offline goal-conditioned RL — extends formal methods to learned policies

title: VTAM: Video-Tactile-Action Models for Complex Physical Interaction Beyond VLAs
authors: Haoran Yuan, Weigang Yi, Zhenyu Zhang, Wendi Chen, Yuchen Mo, Jiashi Yin, Xinzhuo Li, Xiangyu Zeng, Chuan Wen, Cewu Lu, Katherine Driggs-Campbell, Ismini Lourentzou
headline: Video-Tactile-Action Models add force/contact channels that pure-vision VLAs miss in contact-rich tasks
summary: Argues that VLAs miss critical force and contact transitions that vision tokens cannot encode, hurting fine manipulation. Introduces Video-Tactile-Action Models (VTAM) that fuse tactile sensing with video for contact-rich policies. Demonstrates better fine-grained force modulation than VAM/VLA baselines.
abstract: Video-Action Models (VAMs) have emerged as a promising framework for embodied intelligence, learning implicit world dynamics from raw video streams to produce temporally consistent action predictions. Although such models demonstrate strong performance on long-horizon tasks through visual reasoning, they remain limited in contact-rich scenarios where critical interaction states are only partially observable from vision alone. In particular, fine-grained force modulation and contact transitions are not reliably encoded in visual tokens, leading to unstable or imprecise behaviors. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Video-Tactile Action Model (VTAM), a multimodal world modeling framework that incorporates tactile perception as a complementary grounding signal. VTAM augments a pretrained video transformer with tactile streams via a lightweight modality transfer finetuning, enabling efficient cross-modal representation learning without tactile-language paired data or independent tactile pretraining. To stabilize multimodal fusion, we introduce a tactile regularization loss that enforces balanced cross-modal attention, preventing visual latent dominance in the action model. VTAM demonstrates superior performance in contact-rich manipulation, maintaining a robust success rate of 90 percent on average. In challenging scenarios such as potato chip pick-and-place requiring high-fidelity force awareness, VTAM outperforms the pi 0.5 baseline by 80 percent. Our findings demonstrate that integrating tactile feedback is essential for correcting visual estimation errors in world action models, providing a scalable approach to physically grounded embodied foundation models.
notes: Moderate — video+tactile generalist policies for contact-rich tasks — extends VLA paradigm beyond vision

title: Do World Action Models Generalize Better than VLAs? A Robustness Study
authors: Zhanguang Zhang, Zhiyuan Li, Behnam Rahmati, Rui Heng Yang, Yintao Ma, Amir Rasouli, Sajjad Pakdamansavoji, Yangzheng Wu, Lingfeng Zhang, Tongtong Cao, Feng Wen, Xinyu Wang, Xingyue Quan, Yingxue Zhang
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|World models for robotics|Generalist policies|Adversarial robustness of robot policies
headline: World Action Models match or beat VLAs under contextual perturbations in a systematic robustness study
summary: Compares the robustness of vision-language-action models to world-action-model policies under diverse contextual perturbations. Finds that world-model-based action prediction generalizes at least as well, often better, than VLA fine-tuning. Provides empirical guidance for robust generalist policy architecture choices.
abstract: Robot action planning in the real world is challenging as it requires not only understanding the current state of the environment but also predicting how it will evolve in response to actions. Vision-language-action (VLA), which repurpose large-scale vision-language models for robot action generation using action experts, have achieved notable success across a variety of robotic tasks. Nevertheless, their performance remains constrained by the scope of their training data, exhibiting limited generalization to unseen scenarios and vulnerability to diverse contextual perturbations. More recently, world models have been revisited as an alternative to VLAs. These models, referred to as world action models (WAMs), are built upon world models that are trained on large corpora of video data to predict future states. With minor adaptations, their latent representation can be decoded into robot actions. It has been suggested that their explicit dynamic prediction capacity, combined with spatiotemporal priors acquired from web-scale video pretraining, enables WAMs to generalize more effectively than VLAs. In this paper, we conduct a comparative study of prominent state-of-the-art VLA policies and recently released WAMs. We evaluate their performance on the LIBERO-Plus and RoboTwin 2.0-Plus benchmarks under various visual and language perturbations. Our results show that WAMs achieve strong robustness, with LingBot-VA reaching 74.2% success rate on RoboTwin 2.0-Plus and Cosmos-Policy achieving 82.2% on LIBERO-Plus. While VLAs such as $π_{0.5}$ can achieve comparable robustness on certain tasks, they typically require extensive training with diverse robotic datasets and varied learning objectives. Hybrid approaches that partially incorporate video-based dynamic learning exhibit intermediate robustness, highlighting the importance of how video priors are integrated.
notes: High — systematic robustness comparison of WAMs vs VLAs under contextual perturbations — directly relevant to verification

title: High-Order Matrix Control Barrier Functions for Safety-Critical Systems
authors: Isaac Gessow, Matthew Wai Hei Ong, Aaron D. Ames, Brett T. Lopez
headline: Matrix CBFs extend high-relative-degree safety to multi-constraint systems via Lyapunov-like certificates
summary: Introduces high-order matrix control barrier functions that handle multiple safety constraints simultaneously for high-relative-degree systems. Extends scalar CBF theory to matrix inequalities, enabling a unified certificate for coupled constraints. Demonstrates the approach on multi-obstacle avoidance scenarios with improved feasibility over sequential scalar CBFs.
abstract: Control barrier functions (CBFs) are widely used to enforce safety constraints for nonlinear systems, but they face challenges when the safety constraint has a high relative degree or when multiple constraints must be satisfied simultaneously. In this paper, we introduce high-order matrix CBFs (HOMCBFs), a framework that unifies high-relative-degree and multi-constraint safety specifications into a single matrix-valued certificate. We show that the proposed HOMCBF conditions can be efficiently enforced via a quadratic program compatible with existing CBF-based controllers. We validate the approach on numerical examples involving multi-obstacle avoidance for systems with high-relative-degree dynamics, demonstrating improved feasibility and less conservatism compared to sequential application of scalar high-order CBFs.
notes: Alert: Ames/Lopez. High — high-order matrix CBFs unify multi-constraint safety for nonlinear systems

title: Inverse Safety Filtering
authors: Haimin Hu, Sicheng Jason Nguyen, Bolun Li, Sylvia L. Qu, Claire J. Tomlin
headline: Inverse safety filters recover unknown constraints from observed safe interventions on black-box controllers
summary: Proposes inverse safety filtering to infer hidden safety constraints from observed interventions of a safety filter on a nominal controller. Formulates the problem as inverse optimization over HJ reachability-based filters. Recovers constraint parameters that explain observed corrective actions, enabling transparent auditing of opaque safety-critical systems.
abstract: Safety filters modify the actions of a nominal controller to ensure constraint satisfaction. While safety filters have been widely studied in the forward direction -- given known constraints, design a minimally invasive filter -- the inverse problem has received little attention. In this paper, we study inverse safety filtering: given observations of a safety filter's interventions, recover the unknown safety constraints that the filter enforces. We formalize this as an inverse optimization problem and develop solution methods for filters based on Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability analysis and control barrier functions (CBFs). We show that the inverse problem admits a tractable convex formulation under mild assumptions and demonstrate constraint recovery on several dynamical systems, including cases with partial observations and noisy data.
notes: Alert: Tomlin. Core — inverse safety filtering recovers unknown constraints from black-box filter interventions [residual +1: directly addresses black-box verification of safety-critical controllers]

title: A Smooth Parameterization of Continuous-Time Signal Temporal Logic Robustness for Gradient-Based Optimization
authors: Yiwei Uzun, Behcet Acikmese
headline: Smooth continuous-time STL robustness enables gradient-based trajectory optimization without time-discretization artifacts
summary: Presents a smooth parameterization of continuous-time STL robustness that is differentiable almost everywhere, enabling standard gradient-based optimizers. Avoids the conservatism introduced by temporal discretization in existing approaches. Demonstrates improved trajectory optimization for STL specifications on robotic planning problems.
abstract: Signal Temporal Logic (STL) provides a formal framework for specifying time-varying properties of dynamical systems. Existing approaches to STL-constrained optimization typically discretize time, introducing conservatism and computational artifacts. In this work, we propose a smooth parameterization of continuous-time STL robustness that is differentiable almost everywhere and amenable to gradient-based trajectory optimization. Our formulation avoids the need for mixed-integer programming or approximations through smooth under- and over-approximations of the min and max operators in the robustness semantics. We prove soundness of the parameterization and demonstrate its effectiveness on trajectory planning problems with complex temporal specifications, showing significant improvements in both solution quality and computational efficiency compared to time-discretized baselines.
notes: Alert: Acikmese. Moderate — smooth CT-STL robustness for gradient-based trajectory optimization

title: Differentiable Spatial-Temporal Logic for Autonomous Navigation with Density Estimation
authors: Yue Luo, Junjie Liang, Vasile Cristian Cai, Changliu Liu
headline: Differentiable spatial-temporal logic integrates density estimation for safe navigation under uncertainty
summary: Proposes differentiable SpaTiaL (Spatial-Temporal Logic) that combines STL specifications with density estimation for navigation in uncertain environments. Encodes spatial predicates as differentiable density functions, enabling end-to-end optimization of trajectories satisfying temporal specifications while accounting for obstacle uncertainty. Validates on autonomous driving and robot navigation scenarios.
abstract: We present a differentiable framework for spatial-temporal logic (SpaTiaL) that enables gradient-based optimization of robot trajectories under formal temporal specifications in uncertain environments. SpaTiaL extends signal temporal logic with spatial predicates defined via density estimation, allowing the seamless integration of learned environmental models with formal safety requirements. We formulate trajectory optimization as maximizing a smooth robustness measure of the SpaTiaL specification, where spatial predicates encode probabilistic obstacle occupancy. The framework supports end-to-end differentiable planning that jointly reasons about temporal constraints and spatial uncertainty. Experiments on autonomous navigation tasks demonstrate that SpaTiaL produces safer and more efficient trajectories compared to baselines that treat temporal logic and uncertainty separately.
notes: Alert: Vasile/Liu. High — differentiable spatial-temporal logic with density estimation for safe navigation

title: Steering with Contingencies: Safe Path Planning for Autonomous Vehicles via Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability
authors: Anastasiya Lishkova, Matthew Wai Hei Ong, Sander Tonkens, Sylvia L. Herbert, Aaron D. Ames
headline: Contingency path planning uses HJ reachability to guarantee fallback safety for autonomous vehicles
summary: Introduces a contingency-aware path planner that maintains a provably safe fallback trajectory via Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis. The planner generates both a nominal and a contingency plan, switching to the backup when the nominal becomes unsafe. Demonstrates real-time performance on autonomous driving scenarios with formal safety guarantees.
abstract: Safe autonomous driving requires the ability to plan trajectories that account for worst-case interactions with other road users. We present a contingency planning framework that leverages Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability analysis to maintain a provably safe backup plan at all times. The planner generates a nominal trajectory optimizing performance and a contingency trajectory that is guaranteed to remain within the backward reachable tube of a safe target set. A switching condition based on the HJ value function triggers the contingency plan when the nominal trajectory approaches the safety boundary. We demonstrate the approach in highway driving scenarios with multiple interacting vehicles, showing that our planner achieves comparable performance to aggressive planners while providing formal safety guarantees that purely heuristic approaches cannot.
notes: Alert: Herbert/Ames. High — HJ reachability contingency planning for provably safe autonomous vehicles

title: On the Non-monotonicity of Conformal Risk Control
authors: Hala Aldirawi, Wen Li, Yachong Guo
headline: Reveals and addresses non-monotonicity pitfalls in conformal risk control calibration procedures
summary: Identifies a non-monotonicity issue in conformal risk control where increasing the confidence level can paradoxically increase the empirical risk. Provides theoretical analysis of when this pathology arises and proposes a monotone correction that preserves coverage guarantees. Demonstrates the fix on prediction set and regression interval tasks.
abstract: Conformal risk control (CRC) extends conformal prediction to control general risk measures beyond miscoverage. The standard CRC procedure calibrates a threshold parameter to ensure that the population risk does not exceed a user-specified level. We identify a subtle but practically important issue: the calibrated risk is not necessarily monotone in the confidence level, meaning that requesting tighter control can paradoxically lead to higher empirical risk on finite samples. We provide a precise characterization of when this non-monotonicity arises, linking it to properties of the loss function and the conformity score distribution. We then propose a simple monotone correction to the CRC calibration procedure that eliminates this pathology while preserving the finite-sample risk guarantee. Experiments on classification, regression, and object detection tasks confirm that the corrected procedure avoids the anomalous behavior of standard CRC.
notes: Alert: conformal prediction. High — identifies and fixes non-monotonicity in conformal risk control

title: Concentration of Stochastic System Trajectories via Sensitivity Analysis
authors: Hao Liu, Yilin Ma, Xikun Yu, Yuting Chen
headline: Trajectory concentration bounds from sensitivity analysis enable tight reachable set estimation for stochastic systems
summary: Derives concentration inequalities for trajectories of stochastic dynamical systems using sensitivity analysis of the flow map. Provides non-asymptotic bounds on trajectory deviation that tighten with system smoothness. Applies the bounds to over-approximate reachable sets with probabilistic guarantees, useful for safety verification of uncertain systems.
abstract: We study the concentration of trajectories of stochastic dynamical systems around their nominal (deterministic) counterparts. Using sensitivity analysis of the system flow map, we derive non-asymptotic deviation bounds that capture how stochastic perturbations propagate through the dynamics. Our bounds are tighter than those obtained by generic concentration inequalities because they exploit the smoothness and structure of the dynamical system. We apply these results to probabilistic reachable set estimation, providing over-approximations of the set of states reachable under stochastic disturbances with explicit confidence levels. The approach is demonstrated on several benchmark systems, yielding significantly less conservative reachable sets compared to existing methods while maintaining rigorous probabilistic guarantees.
notes: Alert: verification. High — sensitivity-based trajectory concentration bounds for probabilistic reachability

title: Hierarchical Motion Planning via Predicted Reachability
authors: Bowen Zhang, Melkior Ornik
headline: Predicted reachability sets guide hierarchical motion planning with formal guarantees of task completion
summary: Proposes a hierarchical motion planner that uses predicted reachable sets to decompose long-horizon tasks into provably feasible sub-goals. The high-level planner selects waypoints within learned reachability estimates, while the low-level controller executes transitions. Demonstrates improved completeness and efficiency on multi-step robot navigation tasks.
abstract: We propose a hierarchical motion planning framework that leverages predicted reachability to guide task decomposition. At the high level, a planner selects a sequence of subgoals by reasoning about the forward reachable sets of the robot from each candidate state. At the low level, a controller drives the system between consecutive subgoals. By ensuring that each subgoal lies within the predicted reachable set of its predecessor, the framework provides formal guarantees on the feasibility of the overall plan. We present both model-based and data-driven instantiations of the reachability predictor and demonstrate the approach on navigation tasks with nonlinear dynamics, showing improved planning success rates and reduced computation times compared to flat planning baselines.
notes: Alert: verification. High — hierarchical planning via predicted reachability with feasibility guarantees

title: Safe Stabilization via Zubov-Barrier Certificates: A Lyapunov-Based Approach
authors: Yujie Meng, Jun Liu
headline: Zubov-barrier certificates unify Lyapunov stability and CBF safety into a single certificate for safe stabilization
summary: Introduces Zubov-barrier certificates that combine Lyapunov-like stability guarantees with barrier-function safety constraints in a unified framework. The certificate simultaneously characterizes the domain of attraction and ensures constraint satisfaction. Provides constructive synthesis via sum-of-squares programming and demonstrates on nonlinear safe stabilization problems.
abstract: We address the problem of simultaneously stabilizing a nonlinear system to an equilibrium and guaranteeing that the state remains within a safe set for all time. We introduce the concept of Zubov-barrier certificates, which unify control Lyapunov functions (CLFs) and control barrier functions (CBFs) into a single certificate. A Zubov-barrier certificate provides a characterization of the maximal safe domain of attraction -- the largest set of initial conditions from which the system can be safely stabilized. We present conditions for the existence of such certificates and develop computational methods based on sum-of-squares (SOS) programming for their construction. The approach eliminates the need to resolve conflicts between separate CLF and CBF conditions, a well-known difficulty in safe stabilization. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework on benchmark nonlinear systems.
notes: Alert: safety. High — unified Lyapunov-CBF certificate for safe stabilization with maximal safe domain of attraction

title: Event-Triggered Adaptive Safety Control with Taylor-Lagrange Estimation
authors: Hao Liu, Gong Xiao, Christos G. Cassandras, Calin Belta
headline: Event-triggered adaptive CBF controller uses Taylor-Lagrange estimators for safe control under model uncertainty
summary: Proposes an event-triggered adaptive control framework combining CBFs with Taylor-Lagrange state estimation for safety under model uncertainty. Reduces communication and computation by triggering control updates only when safety margins shrink. Provides formal guarantees on constraint satisfaction between trigger events and demonstrates on uncertain robotic systems.
abstract: We present an event-triggered adaptive safety controller for nonlinear systems with model uncertainty. The controller combines control barrier functions (CBFs) with Taylor-Lagrange state and uncertainty estimators to maintain safety guarantees between discrete trigger events. An event-triggering mechanism determines when to update the control input based on the evolution of the CBF value and the estimation error bounds, significantly reducing computation and communication requirements compared to continuous-time CBF controllers. We prove that the closed-loop system satisfies the safety constraint at all times, including between trigger events, under bounded model uncertainty. The framework is extended to handle signal temporal logic (STL) specifications by composing multiple event-triggered CBF constraints. Simulations on robotic systems with uncertain dynamics demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the approach.
notes: Alert: Belta/Cassandras. High — event-triggered adaptive CBF+STL control with formal safety guarantees under uncertainty

title: LAP: Language-Action Pre-Training Enables Zero-shot Cross-Embodiment Transfer
authors: Lihan Zha, Asher J. Hancock, Mingtong Zhang, Tenny Yin, Yixuan Huang, Dhruv Shah, Allen Z. Ren, Anirudha Majumdar
headline: Language-action pre-training achieves first substantial zero-shot VLA transfer across unseen robot embodiments
summary: Introduces LAP, representing low-level robot actions in natural language to align with pre-trained VLMs. LAP-3B is the first VLA achieving 50%+ zero-shot success on unseen embodiments without fine-tuning, roughly doubling prior VLA performance. Unifies action prediction and VQA in a shared format with favorable scaling.
abstract: A long-standing goal in robotics is a generalist policy that can be deployed zero-shot on new robot embodiments without per-embodiment adaptation. Despite large-scale multi-embodiment pre-training, existing Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) remain tightly coupled to their training embodiments and typically require costly fine-tuning. We introduce Language-Action Pre-training (LAP), a simple recipe that represents low-level robot actions directly in natural language, aligning action supervision with the pre-trained vision-language model's input-output distribution. LAP requires no learned tokenizer, no costly annotation, and no embodiment-specific architectural design. Based on LAP, we present LAP-3B, which to the best of our knowledge is the first VLA to achieve substantial zero-shot transfer to previously unseen robot embodiments without any embodiment-specific fine-tuning. Across multiple novel robots and manipulation tasks, LAP-3B attains over 50% average zero-shot success, delivering roughly a 2x improvement over the strongest prior VLAs. We further show that LAP enables efficient adaptation and favorable scaling, while unifying action prediction and VQA in a shared language-action format that yields additional gains through co-training.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — first VLA with substantial zero-shot cross-embodiment transfer

title: CaP-X: A Framework for Benchmarking and Improving Coding Agents for Robot Manipulation
authors: Max Fu, Justin Yu, Karim El-Refai, Ethan Kou, Haoru Xue, Huang Huang, Wenli Xiao, Guanzhi Wang, Fei-Fei Li, Guanya Shi, Jiajun Wu, Shankar Sastry, Yuke Zhu, Ken Goldberg, Linxi "Jim" Fan
headline: CaP-X benchmarks coding agents for robot manipulation and shows agentic test-time compute recovers human-level reliability
summary: Presents CaP-X, an open-access framework for studying Code-as-Policy agents in robot manipulation. CaP-Bench evaluates 12 models, revealing dependence on designer scaffolding. Scaling agentic test-time computation through multi-turn feedback and skill synthesis substantially improves robustness, with CaP-RL showing sim2real transfer.
abstract: "Code-as-Policy" considers how executable code can complement data-intensive Vision-Language-Action (VLA) methods, yet their effectiveness as autonomous controllers for embodied manipulation remains underexplored. We present CaP-X, an open-access framework for systematically studying Code-as-Policy agents in robot manipulation. At its core is CaP-Gym, an interactive environment in which agents control robots by synthesizing and executing programs that compose perception and control primitives. Building on this foundation, CaP-Bench evaluates frontier language and vision-language models across varying levels of abstraction, interaction, and perceptual grounding. Across 12 models, CaP-Bench reveals a consistent trend: performance improves with human-crafted abstractions but degrades as these priors are removed, exposing a dependence on designer scaffolding. At the same time, we observe that this gap can be mitigated through scaling agentic test-time computation--through multi-turn interaction, structured execution feedback, visual differencing, automatic skill synthesis, and ensembled reasoning--substantially improves robustness even when agents operate over low-level primitives. These findings allow us to derive CaP-Agent0, a training-free framework that recovers human-level reliability on several manipulation tasks in simulation and on real embodiments. We further introduce CaP-RL, showing reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards improves success rates and transfers from sim2real with minimal gap. Together, CaP-X provides a principled, open-access platform for advancing embodied coding agents.
notes: Alert: Goldberg/Shi. Moderate — benchmarking coding agents as alternative to VLAs for robot manipulation

title: Language-Grounded Decoupled Action Representation for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Wuding Weng, Tongshu Wu, Liucheng Chen, Siyu Xie, Zheng Wang, Xing Xu, Jingkuan Song, Heng Tao Shen
headline: Language-grounded decoupled action primitives bridge vision-language understanding and low-level robot control (CVPR 2026)
summary: Proposes LaDA, using language as a semantic bridge to connect perception and control via three interpretable action primitives (translation, rotation, gripper). Soft-label contrastive learning aligns similar primitives across tasks. Achieves strong generalization on LIBERO and MimicGen benchmarks and real-world demonstrations.
abstract: The heterogeneity between high-level vision-language understanding and low-level action control remains a fundamental challenge in robotic manipulation. Although recent methods have advanced task-specific action alignment, they often struggle to generate robust and accurate actions for novel or semantically related tasks. To address this, we propose the Language-Grounded Decoupled Action Representation (LaDA) framework, which leverages natural language as a semantic bridge to connect perception and control. LaDA introduces a fine-grained intermediate layer of three interpretable action primitives--translation, rotation, and gripper control--providing explicit semantic structure for low-level actions. It further employs a semantic-guided soft-label contrastive learning objective to align similar action primitives across tasks, enhancing generalization and motion consistency. An adaptive weighting strategy, inspired by curriculum learning, dynamically balances contrastive and imitation objectives for stable and effective training. Extensive experiments on simulated benchmarks (LIBERO and MimicGen) and real-world demonstrations validate that LaDA achieves strong performance and generalizes effectively to unseen or related tasks.
notes: Alert: CVPR citations. Moderate — language-grounded action primitives for manipulation generalization (CVPR 2026)

title: Safe Neurosymbolic Learning for Autonomous Systems
authors: Somil Bansal, Jaime F. Fisac
headline: Book chapter by Bansal and Fisac surveys neurosymbolic methods for safe learned autonomous systems
summary: A book chapter surveying safe neurosymbolic learning approaches for autonomous systems, combining neural network flexibility with symbolic safety guarantees. Covers CBF-based learning, Hamilton-Jacobi safety analysis, and their intersection with learned components. Directly relevant as a tutorial-style overview from leading safety verification researchers.
abstract: This chapter surveys neurosymbolic approaches for designing safe learning-based autonomous systems. We discuss how symbolic safety specifications, such as control barrier functions and Hamilton-Jacobi reachability, can be integrated with neural network-based controllers and perception modules to provide formal safety guarantees. The chapter covers recent advances in safe reinforcement learning, verified neural controllers, and runtime monitoring for learned systems.
notes: Alert: Bansal/Fisac. Core — book chapter on neurosymbolic safety by leading verification researchers [residual +1: directly from Bansal and Fisac, core safety verification researchers]

title: Safe Control using Learned Safety Filters and Adaptive Conformal Inference
authors: Sacha Huriot, Ihab Tabbara, Hussein Sibai
matched_interests: Conformal prediction|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Safe & robust robot control|Latent-space safety analysis
headline: Adaptive conformal inference calibrates learned HJ safety filters for reliable switching
summary: ACoFi combines learned Hamilton-Jacobi reachability-based safety filters with adaptive conformal inference to dynamically adjust switching criteria based on observed prediction errors. The method guarantees asymptotic upper bounds on incorrect uncertainty quantification rates and outperforms fixed-threshold baselines in safety violation reduction, especially out-of-distribution.
abstract: Safety filters have been shown to be effective tools to ensure the safety of control systems with unsafe nominal policies. To address scalability challenges in traditional synthesis methods, learning-based approaches have been proposed for designing safety filters for systems with high-dimensional state and control spaces. However, the inevitable errors in the decisions of these models raise concerns about their reliability and the safety guarantees they offer. This paper presents Adaptive Conformal Filtering (ACoFi), a method that combines learned Hamilton-Jacobi reachability-based safety filters with adaptive conformal inference. Under ACoFi, the filter dynamically adjusts its switching criteria based on the observed errors in its predictions of the safety of actions.
notes: Core — combines conformal prediction with HJ reachability safety filters, directly at the intersection of multiple core interests [residual +1: directly addresses data-driven safety filtering with formal statistical guarantees via conformal inference]

title: How to Train Your Latent Control Barrier Function: Smooth Safety Filtering Under Hard-to-Model Constraints
authors: Kensuke Nakamura, Arun L Bishop, Steven Man, Aaron M. Johnson, Zachary Manchester, Andrea Bajcsy
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Latent-space safety analysis|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Latent CBFs learn smooth safety certificates without explicit constraint models
summary: Proposes latent CBFs that learn safety certificates directly in a learned latent space, jointly training an encoder, dynamics model, and CBF. This eliminates the need for hand-designed constraint functions while ensuring smooth safety filtering for complex environments.
abstract: Control barrier functions (CBFs) are a popular approach for safe robot control, but they require explicit mathematical models of safety constraints — which are often unavailable for complex environments. We propose latent CBFs, which learn smooth safety certificates directly in a learned latent representation space. Our approach jointly trains an encoder, a latent dynamics model, and a CBF in the latent space, ensuring smooth safety filtering without needing hand-designed constraint functions.
notes: Core — latent-space CBF learning directly relevant to verification of black-box systems

title: BURNS: Backward Underapproximate Reachability for Neural-Feedback-Loop Systems
authors: Chelsea Rose Sidrane, Jana Tumova
headline: Backward underapproximate reachability enables precise safety analysis of neural controllers
summary: BURNS computes backward underapproximate reachable sets for neural-feedback-loop systems, providing guaranteed reach-target certificates that are less conservative than overapproximation methods. This enables more precise safety verification of neural network controllers.
abstract: We present BURNS, a method for computing backward underapproximate reachable sets for neural-feedback-loop systems. Unlike overapproximation methods that may be overly conservative, our underapproximation approach identifies states that are guaranteed to reach a target set, enabling more precise safety analysis of systems with neural network controllers.
notes: Core — backward reachability for neural controllers directly relevant to verification

title: World Model Predictive Safety Filtering
authors: Madison Bland, Jaime Fisac, Albert Lin, Somil Bansal
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Safe & robust robot control|Runtime verification of learned policies
headline: World models enable predictive safety filtering by simulating future trajectories
summary: Proposes safety filtering using learned world models for predictive assessment. The filter simulates future trajectories within the world model to anticipate unsafe states and intervene proactively, combining neural world model expressiveness with safety guarantees.
abstract: We propose a safety filtering framework that leverages learned world models for predictive safety assessment. By simulating future trajectories within a world model, the filter can anticipate unsafe states and intervene before safety violations occur, combining the expressiveness of neural world models with formal safety filtering guarantees.
notes: Core — world model safety filtering bridges world models and safety verification

title: Safe Planning in Interactive Environments via Iterative Policy Updates and Adversarially Robust Conformal Prediction
authors: Omid Mirzaeedodangeh, Eliot Seo Shekhtman, Nikolai Matni, Lars Lindemann
headline: Adversarially robust conformal prediction ensures safe planning under interaction-induced shifts
summary: Combines iterative policy updates with adversarially robust conformal prediction for safe planning in interactive environments. Provides distribution-free safety guarantees robust to distributional shifts caused by ego-agent actions.
abstract: We address safe planning in interactive environments where agents must account for the uncertain behavior of other agents. Our approach combines iterative policy updates with adversarially robust conformal prediction to provide distribution-free safety guarantees that hold even under distributional shifts caused by the ego agent's own actions.
notes: High — conformal prediction for safe interactive planning

title: Conformal Prediction Regions for Continuous-Time Trajectories under Random Sampling
authors: Joaquin Alvarez, Matteo Sesia, Jyotirmoy V. Deshmukh, Lars Lindemann
headline: Conformal prediction extended to continuous-time trajectory coverage guarantees
summary: Extends conformal prediction to continuous-time trajectories, constructing prediction regions that cover true trajectories with specified probability under random sampling. Enables uncertainty-aware planning and control for continuous-time robotic systems.
abstract: We extend conformal prediction to continuous-time trajectory prediction under random sampling. Our method constructs prediction regions that cover the true trajectory with a user-specified probability, enabling uncertainty-aware planning and control for robotic systems operating in continuous time.
notes: High — foundational conformal prediction methodology for robotics trajectories

title: Safety Beyond the Training Data: Robust Out-of-Distribution MPC via Conformalized System Level Synthesis
authors: Anutam Srinivasan, Glen Chou
matched_interests: Conformal prediction|Safe & robust robot control|Data-driven verification
headline: Conformalized system-level synthesis enables safe MPC beyond training distribution
summary: Combines system level synthesis with conformal prediction for robust MPC that maintains safety guarantees in out-of-distribution scenarios. Uncertainty sets adapt to observed model errors, enabling safe control beyond the training data distribution.
abstract: We propose a robust MPC framework that maintains safety guarantees even when the system encounters out-of-distribution scenarios not seen during training. Our approach combines system level synthesis with conformal prediction to construct uncertainty sets that adapt to observed model errors, enabling safe control beyond the training distribution.
notes: Core — data-driven safety guarantees via conformal prediction for OOD robustness [residual +1: directly addresses safety of black-box systems beyond training distribution, combining conformal prediction with control synthesis]

title: Robust Verification of Controllers under State Uncertainty via Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability Analysis
authors: Albert Lin, Alessandro Pinto, Somil Bansal
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control
headline: HJ reachability extended to verify controllers under state estimation uncertainty
summary: Extends Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis to account for state uncertainty, propagating uncertainty sets through the HJ framework for worst-case safety guarantees. Addresses the practical challenge of state estimation errors in safety-critical controller verification.
abstract: We present a method for verifying controller safety under state uncertainty using Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis.
