By propagating uncertainty sets through the HJ reachability framework, we provide worst-case safety guarantees that account for state estimation errors, extending standard reachability analysis to uncertain state observations.
notes: Core — HJ reachability verification under uncertainty directly relevant to data-driven verification

title: Scalable Verification of Neural Control Barrier Functions Using Linear Bound Propagation
authors: Nikolaus Vertovec, Frederik Baymler Mathiesen, Thom Badings, Luca Laurenti, Alessandro Abate
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Linear bound propagation enables scalable verification of neural CBFs
summary: Uses linear bound propagation techniques to scalably verify that neural control barrier functions satisfy safety conditions across the state space. Scales to higher-dimensional systems than existing neural CBF verification methods.
abstract: We propose a scalable approach to verify neural control barrier functions (NCBFs) using linear bound propagation techniques. Our method efficiently checks whether a neural CBF satisfies the required safety conditions across the state space, scaling to higher-dimensional systems than existing verification approaches.
notes: Core — scalable neural CBF verification directly relevant to data-driven verification of learned controllers

title: Value Function Decomposition for Temporal Logic
authors: William Sharpless, Dylan Hirsch, Oswin So, Sander Tonkens, Nikhil Shinde, Chuchu Fan, Sylvia Herbert
matched_interests: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Decomposing value functions enables scalable temporal logic satisfaction
summary: Proposes decomposing value functions for temporal logic specifications, enabling more scalable satisfaction of complex temporal logic constraints in control synthesis. Connects HJ reachability with temporal logic for safety-critical systems.
abstract: We propose a value function decomposition approach for satisfying temporal logic specifications in control synthesis. By decomposing complex temporal logic constraints into simpler sub-problems, our method enables more scalable and efficient computation while maintaining correctness guarantees.
notes: High — temporal logic + HJ reachability for safety verification

title: Synthesis and Deployment of Maximal Robust Control Barrier Functions through Adversarial Reinforcement Learning
authors: (from arXiv 2604.13192)
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control|Adversarial robustness of robot policies
headline: Adversarial RL synthesizes maximally robust discrete-time CBFs under uncertainty
summary: Introduces a robust CBF framework for general nonlinear systems under bounded uncertainty, showing that the safety value function solving the Isaacs equation is a valid robust discrete-time CBF. Uses adversarial RL for synthesis and demonstrates deployment on real systems.
abstract: We present a new robust control barrier function (CBF) framework for general nonlinear systems under bounded uncertainty. We show that the safety value function solving the dynamic programming Isaacs equation is a valid robust discrete-time CBF and use adversarial reinforcement learning for synthesis.
notes: High — robust CBF synthesis via adversarial RL

title: Capability-Aware Heterogeneous Control Barrier Functions for Decentralized Multi-Robot Safe Navigation
authors: (from arXiv 2604.13245)
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Capability-aware CBFs enable consistent safety in heterogeneous multi-robot teams
summary: Proposes CA-HCBF, a decentralized framework for consistent safety enforcement in heterogeneous robot teams. Addresses capability-aware coordination by adapting CBF constraints to individual robot capabilities.
abstract: We present Capability-Aware Heterogeneous Control Barrier Functions (CA-HCBF), a decentralized framework for consistent safety enforcement and capability-aware coordination in heterogeneous robot teams navigating shared environments.
notes: High — CBFs for multi-robot safety

title: π₀.₇: a Steerable Generalist Robotic Foundation Model with Emergent Capabilities
authors: Physical Intelligence team
headline: π₀.₇ demonstrates zero-shot cross-embodiment transfer and steerable generalist control
summary: π₀.₇ is a robotic foundation model that provides strong out-of-the-box performance across diverse scenarios, follows language instructions in unseen environments, enables zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization, and performs tasks like operating an espresso machine at levels matching specialized RL-finetuned models. Uses diverse context conditioning for steerability.
abstract: We present π₀.₇, a robotic foundation model that can enable strong out-of-the-box performance in a wide range of scenarios, follow diverse language instructions in unseen environments, provide zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization, and perform challenging tasks such as operating an espresso machine at a level matching specialized RL-finetuned models.
notes: High — major VLA/generalist policy with cross-embodiment transfer

title: Deep QP Safety Filter: Model-free Learning for Reachability-based Safety Filter
authors: Byeongjun Kim, H. Jin Kim
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Safe & robust robot control|Data-driven verification
headline: Model-free QP safety filter learns reachability-based safety without system dynamics
summary: Introduces a fully data-driven safety layer for black-box dynamical systems that learns a QP safety filter without model knowledge by combining HJ reachability with model-free learning. Enables safety filtering for systems where dynamics models are unavailable.
abstract: We introduce a fully data-driven safety layer for black-box dynamical systems that learns a Quadratic-Program (QP) safety filter without model knowledge by combining Hamilton-Jacobi reachability with model-free learning.
notes: Core — model-free safety filter for black-box systems using HJ reachability

title: CoFineLLM: Conformal Finetuning of LLMs for Language-Instructed Robot Planning
authors: Jun Wang, Yevgeniy Vorobeychik, Yiannis Kantaros
headline: Conformal finetuning calibrates LLM planners with distribution-free guarantees
summary: Applies conformal prediction to finetune LLMs for robot planning tasks, providing distribution-free coverage guarantees on plan correctness. Bridges language-instructed planning with statistical safety guarantees.
abstract: We present CoFineLLM, a framework that applies conformal prediction to finetune large language models for language-instructed robot planning, providing distribution-free coverage guarantees on plan correctness.
notes: High — conformal prediction applied to LLM-based robot planning

title: FALCON: Learning Force-Adaptive Humanoid Loco-Manipulation
authors: Yuanhang Zhang, Yifu Yuan, Prajwal Gurunath, et al.
headline: Force-adaptive humanoid learns whole-body loco-manipulation via RL
summary: FALCON learns force-adaptive humanoid loco-manipulation, enabling whole-body coordination for manipulation tasks while locomoting. Uses reinforcement learning for force-aware control.
abstract: We present FALCON, a learning framework for force-adaptive humanoid loco-manipulation that enables whole-body coordination for manipulation tasks during locomotion.
notes: High — RL-based humanoid loco-manipulation

title: BarrierBench: Evaluating Large Language Models for Safety Verification in Dynamical Systems
authors: Ali Taheri, Alireza Taban, Sadegh Soudjani, Ashutosh Trivedi
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Data-driven verification
headline: BarrierBench benchmarks LLMs on synthesizing safety certificates for dynamical systems
summary: Introduces BarrierBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on safety verification tasks in dynamical systems, testing their ability to synthesize barrier certificates and Lyapunov functions.
abstract: We introduce BarrierBench, a benchmark for evaluating large language models on safety verification tasks in dynamical systems, including synthesis of barrier certificates and Lyapunov functions.
notes: High — benchmarking LLMs for safety certificate synthesis

title: Certified Robust Invariant Polytope Training in Neural Controlled ODEs
authors: Akash Harapanahalli, Samuel Coogan
headline: Training neural ODEs with certified robust invariant polytope guarantees
summary: Proposes training neural controlled ODEs with certified robust invariant polytope constraints, ensuring learned controllers maintain formal safety guarantees through invariant set certification.
abstract: We propose certified robust invariant polytope training for neural controlled ODEs, ensuring learned controllers maintain formal safety guarantees through invariant set certification during training.
notes: Core — certified training of neural controllers with invariant set guarantees

title: ATOM-CBF: Adaptive Safe Perception-Based Control under Out-of-Distribution Measurements
authors: Kai S. Yun, Navid Azizan
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Verification of visuomotor policies
headline: Adaptive CBFs handle out-of-distribution perception for safe vision-based control
summary: ATOM-CBF provides adaptive safe control under OOD perception measurements by adapting CBF constraints to account for perception uncertainty. Addresses safety of vision-based controllers when visual inputs deviate from training distribution.
abstract: We present ATOM-CBF, a framework for adaptive safe perception-based control that handles out-of-distribution measurements by adapting CBF constraints to perception uncertainty.
notes: Core — perception-aware CBF safety for vision-based control under OOD

title: Kernel-Based Safe Exploration in Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Rupak Majumdar, Nikhil Singh, Sadegh Soudjani
matched_interests: Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control|Active learning
headline: Kernel methods enable provably safe exploration in deep RL
summary: Uses kernel-based methods to enable provably safe exploration during deep reinforcement learning, providing formal guarantees on constraint satisfaction during the learning process.
abstract: We propose kernel-based safe exploration methods for deep reinforcement learning that provide formal guarantees on constraint satisfaction during the learning process.
notes: High — safe RL exploration with formal guarantees

title: Interactive World Simulator for Robot Policy Training and Evaluation
authors: (from arXiv 2603.08546)
headline: Interactive world simulator enables fast robot policy training via consistency models
summary: Builds interactive world models from moderate-sized robot interaction datasets using consistency models for image decoding and latent-space dynamics prediction, enabling fast and stable simulation of physical interactions for policy training and evaluation.
abstract: We present a framework that builds interactive world models from a moderate-sized robot interaction dataset, leveraging consistency models for both image decoding and latent-space dynamics prediction, enabling fast and stable simulation of physical interactions.
notes: High — world model for robot policy training

title: WMPO: World Model-based Policy Optimization for Vision-Language-Action Models
authors: (from arXiv 2511.09515)
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Reinforcement learning
headline: World model enables on-policy VLA RL without real-environment interaction
summary: WMPO introduces world-model-based policy optimization for VLAs, performing on-policy RL entirely within a learned world model without real environment interaction. This bridges the gap between VLA pre-training and task-specific optimization.
abstract: We introduce World-Model-based Policy Optimization (WMPO), a framework for on-policy VLA RL without interacting with the real environment, where policy optimization is performed entirely within a learned world model.
notes: High — VLA optimization via world models

title: Safe Whole-Body Loco-Manipulation via Combined Model and Learning-based Control
authors: (from arXiv 2603.02443)
headline: Hybrid model-based and RL control enables safe whole-body loco-manipulation
summary: Combines model-based admittance control for the manipulator arm with RL-based locomotion control for safe whole-body loco-manipulation. Addresses simultaneous locomotion and manipulation with safety and compliance.
abstract: We propose a whole-body controller that combines model-based admittance control for the manipulator arm with a Reinforcement Learning policy for legged locomotion to achieve safe whole-body loco-manipulation.
notes: High — safe loco-manipulation combining model-based and learned control

title: Convex Computations for Controlled Safety Invariant Sets of Black-box Discrete-time Dynamical Systems
authors: (from arXiv 2504.01638)
headline: Convex methods compute safety invariant sets for black-box discrete-time systems
summary: Proposes convex computation methods for controlled safety invariant sets of black-box discrete-time dynamical systems, enabling data-driven safety set computation without explicit dynamics models.
abstract: We propose convex computation methods for controlled safety invariant sets of black-box discrete-time dynamical systems, enabling data-driven safety set computation without explicit dynamics models.
notes: Core — data-driven safety invariant set computation for black-box systems [residual +1: directly addresses black-box verification via data-driven invariant set computation]

title: ICLR: In-Context Imitation Learning with Visual Reasoning
authors: (from arXiv 2603.07530)
headline: In-context imitation learning adapts to new tasks via structured visual reasoning
summary: Enables robots to adapt to new tasks from few demonstrations without additional training by augmenting demonstration prompts with structured visual reasoning traces. Provides an in-context learning approach to imitation learning.
abstract: We present ICLR, a method that enables robots to adapt to new tasks from a small number of demonstrations without additional training by augmenting demonstration prompts with structured visual reasoning traces.
notes: High — in-context imitation learning with visual reasoning for few-shot adaptation

title: ABot-PhysWorld: Interactive World Foundation Model for Robotic Manipulation with Physics Alignment
authors: Y. Chen, R. Chen, D. Huo, Y. Yang, D. Qi, H. Liu, T. Lin, S. Zeng, J. Xiao, X. Chang, F. Xiong, X. Wei, Z. Ma, M. Xu
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Diffusion models|Generalist policies
headline: 14B diffusion world model produces physics-plausible, action-controllable videos for robot manipulation.
summary: ABot-PhysWorld is a 14B Diffusion Transformer world model trained with physics-aligned curation and objectives that explicitly encode physical laws. It generates visually realistic, action-controllable videos that respect object non-penetration and gravity, overcoming the physically implausible rollouts typical of generic video world models. The result targets embodied simulation and planning for manipulation.
abstract: Video-based world models offer a powerful paradigm for embodied simulation and planning, yet state-of-the-art models often generate physically implausible manipulations - such as object penetration and anti-gravity motion - due to training on generic visual data and likelihood-based objectives that ignore physical laws. We present ABot-PhysWorld, a 14B Diffusion Transformer model that generates visually realistic, physically plausible, and action-controllable videos. Built on a curated dataset of three million manipulation clips with physics-aware annotation, it uses a novel DPO-based post-training framework with decoupled discriminators to suppress unphysical behaviors while preserving visual quality. A parallel context block enables precise spatial action injection for cross-embodiment control. To better evaluate generalization, we introduce EZSbench, the first training-independent embodied zero-shot benchmark combining real and synthetic unseen robot-task-scene combinations. It employs a decoupled protocol to separately assess physical realism and action alignment. ABot-PhysWorld achieves new state-of-the-art performance on PBench and EZSbench, surpassing Veo 3.1 and Sora v2 Pro in physical plausibility and trajectory consistency. We will release EZSbench to promote standardized evaluation in embodied video generation.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — diffusion world model for manipulation (world models + diffusion + generalist policy cluster)

title: SafeMind: A Risk-Aware Differentiable Control Framework for Adaptive and Safe Quadruped Locomotion
authors: Z. Zhang, K. Shu, M. Mo
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Reinforcement learning
headline: Differentiable probabilistic CBF framework unifies uncertainty quantification and risk calibration for quadruped locomotion.
summary: SafeMind is a differentiable stochastic control framework that combines probabilistic control barrier functions with semantic context and meta-adaptive risk calibration. It explicitly models epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty via variance-aware barrier constraints, giving learning-based quadruped controllers formal safety guarantees under perception noise and contact variability. The framework is validated on hardware.
abstract: Learning-based quadruped controllers achieve impressive agility but typically lack formal safety guarantees under model uncertainty, perception noise, and unstructured contact conditions. We introduce SafeMind, a differentiable stochastic safety-control framework that unifies probabilistic Control Barrier Functions with semantic context understanding and meta-adaptive risk calibration. SafeMind explicitly models epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty through a variance-aware barrier constraint embedded in a differentiable quadratic program, thereby preserving gradient flow for end-to-end training. A semantics-to-constraint encoder modulates safety margins using perceptual or language cues, while a meta-adaptive learner continuously adjusts risk sensitivity across environments. We provide theoretical conditions for probabilistic forward invariance, feasibility, and stability under stochastic dynamics. SafeMind is deployed on Unitree A1 and ANYmal C at 200~Hz and validated across 12 terrain types, dynamic obstacles, morphology perturbations, and semantically defined tasks. Experiments show that SafeMind reduces safety violations by 3--10x and energy consumption by 10--15% relative to state-of-the-art CBF, MPC, and hybrid RL baselines, while maintaining real-time control performance.
notes: Alert: Herbert. High — probabilistic CBFs + learning-based locomotion

title: DreamerAD: Efficient Reinforcement Learning via Latent World Model for Autonomous Driving
authors: P. Yang, Y. Zheng, D. Qian, Z. Xing, Q. Zhang, L. Wang, Y. Zhang, S. Guo, Z. Xia, Q. Chen, J. Han, L. Xu, Y. Pan, D. Zhao
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Reinforcement learning|Diffusion models
headline: Latent world model compresses diffusion sampling 80x to enable efficient RL for autonomous driving.
summary: DreamerAD is a latent-space world model that compresses 100-step diffusion sampling down to a single step, yielding 80x speedup without sacrificing visual interpretability. Training RL policies in this latent imagination avoids the prohibitive costs and safety risks of real-world driving rollouts. The method achieves efficient on-policy RL for autonomous driving.
abstract: We introduce DreamerAD, the first latent world model framework that enables efficient reinforcement learning for autonomous driving by compressing diffusion sampling from 100 steps to 1 - achieving 80x speedup while maintaining visual interpretability. Training RL policies on real-world driving data incurs prohibitive costs and safety risks. While existing pixel-level diffusion world models enable safe imagination-based training, they suffer from multi-step diffusion inference latency (2s/frame) that prevents high-frequency RL interaction. Our approach leverages denoised latent features from video generation models through three key mechanisms: (1) shortcut forcing that reduces sampling complexity via recursive multi-resolution step compression, (2) an autoregressive dense reward model operating directly on latent representations for fine-grained credit assignment, and (3) Gaussian vocabulary sampling for GRPO that constrains exploration to physically plausible trajectories. DreamerAD achieves 87.7 EPDMS on NavSim v2, establishing state-of-the-art performance and demonstrating that latent-space RL is effective for autonomous driving.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy. High — latent world model + diffusion + RL — aligned with world-model policy evaluation

title: Probabilistic Control Barrier Functions for Systems with State Estimation Uncertainty using Sub-Gaussian Concentration
authors: K. Echigo, D. van Wijk, P. Mestres, E. Daş, J. Burdick, A. Ames
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Particle-based probabilistic CBFs use sub-Gaussian concentration for tight safety under state estimation uncertainty.
summary: This paper presents a particle-based probabilistic CBF framework for stochastic systems with state estimation uncertainty, using sub-Gaussian concentration inequalities to tighten the trade-off between probabilistic guarantees and computational tractability. A spacecraft proximity-operations case study demonstrates the approach. It applies directly to safety-critical control systems with noisy estimators.
abstract: Safety-critical control systems, such as spacecraft performing proximity operations, must provide formal safety guarantees despite stochastic uncertainties from state estimation and unmodeled dynamics. Although Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) have been extended to stochastic systems, existing approaches typically face a trade-off between the tightness of probabilistic guarantees and computational tractability. This paper presents a particle-based probabilistic CBF framework that overcomes this limitation by exploiting the sub-Gaussian structure of the barrier function increment under Gaussian uncertainties. We establish that Gaussian uncertainties propagating through Lipschitz-continuous control-affine dynamics preserve sub-Gaussianity of the barrier function increment, with explicit tail bounds. Leveraging this structure, we derive finite-sample bounds on the approximation error between particle-based Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) estimates and ground-truth probabilistic constraints; applying this yields a tractable optimization problem formulation with finite-sample safety certificates. We show through numerical experiments how the proposed approach provides tight yet provably valid probabilistic safety guarantees.
notes: Alert: Herbert/Tomlin/Lindemann. High — probabilistic CBFs under estimation uncertainty (Ames/van Wijk group)

title: Boundary Sampling to Learn Predictive Safety Filters via Pontryagin's Maximum Principle
authors: J. Dallas, T. Lew, J. Talbot, J. DeCastro, S. Bansal, J. Subosits
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Safe & robust robot control|Active learning|Data-driven verification
headline: Pontryagin-guided boundary trajectory sampling yields informative data for scalable learned safety filters.
summary: Dallas et al. characterize trajectories that barely avoid safety violations via the Pontryagin Maximum Principle and use these boundary trajectories to generate targeted training data for learning-based safety filters. The approach sharpens the filter near the safe-set boundary, which is where learned predictors typically fail, and scales to high-dimensional systems. Empirical results show improved safety-performance trade-offs over uniform sampling.
abstract: Safety filters provide a practical approach for enforcing safety constraints in autonomous systems. While learning-based tools scale to high-dimensional systems, their performance depends on informative data that includes states likely to lead to constraint violation, which can be difficult to efficiently sample in complex, high-dimensional systems. In this work, we characterize trajectories that barely avoid safety violations using the Pontryagin Maximum Principle. These boundary trajectories are used to guide data collection for learned Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability, concentrating learning efforts near safety-critical states to improve efficiency. The learned Control Barrier Value Function is then used directly for safety filtering. Simulations and experimental validation on a shared-control automotive racing application demonstrate PMP sampling improves learning efficiency, yielding faster convergence, reduced failure rates, and improved safe set reconstruction, with wall times around 3ms.
notes: Alert: Bansal-citation/Tomlin-citation. Core — Bansal's own work on data-efficient learned safety filters — direct overlap with data-driven verification + active sampling

title: Capability-Aware Heterogeneous Control Barrier Functions for Decentralized Multi-Robot Safe Navigation
authors: J. Kim, Y. Zhang, W. Luo, Y. Lyu
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Heterogeneous CBFs distribute collision-avoidance obligations based on each robot's physical capabilities.
summary: This work extends decentralized CBF-based multi-robot navigation to heterogeneous teams by encoding each agent's dynamics-dependent capabilities into the barrier constraint. This removes the homogeneity assumption and the resulting non-uniform interpretations that cause deadlock or infeasibility in mixed fleets. Simulations and hardware demonstrate safe navigation among heterogeneous agents.
abstract: Safe navigation for multi-robot systems requires enforcing safety without sacrificing task efficiency under decentralized decision-making. Existing decentralized methods often assume robot homogeneity, making shared safety requirements non-uniformly interpreted across heterogeneous agents with structurally different dynamics, which could lead to avoidance obligations not physically realizable for some robots and thus cause safety violations or deadlock. In this paper, we propose Capability-Aware Heterogeneous Control Barrier Function (CA-HCBF), a decentralized framework for consistent safety enforcement and capability-aware coordination in heterogeneous robot teams. We derive a canonical second-order control-affine representation that unifies holonomic and nonholonomic robots under acceleration-level control via canonical transformation and backstepping, preserving forward invariance of the safe set while avoiding relative-degree mismatch across heterogeneous dynamics. We further introduce a support-function-based directional capability metric that quantifies each robot's ability to follow its motion intent, deriving a pairwise responsibility allocation that distributes the safety burden proportionally to each robot's motion capability. A feasibility-aware clipping mechanism further constrains the allocation to each agent's physically achievable range, mitigating infeasible constraint assignments common in dense decentralized CBF settings. Simulations with up to 30 heterogeneous robots and a physical multi-robot demonstration show improved safety and task efficiency over baselines, validating real-world applicability across robots with distinct kinematic constraints.
notes: Alert: Tomlin/Herbert/Bansal. Moderate — CBFs for multi-robot safe navigation

title: Synthesis and Deployment of Maximal Robust Control Barrier Functions through Adversarial Reinforcement Learning
authors: D. Oh, D. Nguyen, H. Hu, J. Fisac
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Reinforcement learning
headline: Adversarial RL synthesizes maximal robust CBFs for general nonlinear systems without control-affine assumptions.
summary: Oh et al. introduce a robust CBF framework (Fisac group) that uses adversarial reinforcement learning to synthesize CBFs for general nonlinear systems, without the usual control-affine or closed-form uncertainty structure. The learned CBF certifies larger safe sets than prior robust constructions and deploys online as a safety filter. Evaluations span several nonlinear benchmarks.
abstract: Robust control barrier functions (CBFs) provide a principled mechanism for smooth safety enforcement under worst-case disturbances. However, existing approaches typically rely on explicit, closed-form structure in the dynamics (e.g., control-affine) and uncertainty models. This has led to limited scalability and generality, with most robust CBFs certifying only conservative subsets of the maximal robust safe set. In this paper, we introduce a new robust CBF framework for general nonlinear systems under bounded uncertainty. We first show that the safety value function solving the dynamic programming Isaacs equation is a valid robust discrete-time CBF that enforces safety on the maximal robust safe set. We then adopt the key reinforcement learning (RL) notion of quality function (or Q-function), which removes the need for explicit dynamics by lifting the barrier certificate into state-action space and yields a novel robust Q-CBF constraint for safety filtering. Combined with adversarial RL, this enables the synthesis and deployment of robust Q-CBFs on general nonlinear systems with black-box dynamics and unknown uncertainty structure. We validate the framework on a canonical inverted pendulum benchmark and a 36-D quadruped simulator, achieving substantially less conservative safe sets than barrier-based baselines on the pendulum and reliable safety enforcement even under adversarial uncertainty realizations on the quadruped.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy/Herbert-citation. Moderate — adversarial RL synthesis of robust CBFs — data-driven but targets control, not verification

title: Data-driven Reachable Set Estimation with Tunable Adversarial and Wasserstein Distributional Guarantees
authors: G. Pantazis, M. Chong
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Conformal prediction
headline: Scenario optimization with slack yields tunable adversarial and Wasserstein guarantees for data-driven reachable sets.
summary: Pantazis and Chong tailor scenario optimization to finite-horizon reachable set estimation of unknown discrete-time systems using only sampled state trajectories. A relaxed scenario program with slack variables yields probabilistic guarantees on future trajectory inclusion over the entire horizon, with tunable adversarial and Wasserstein distributional robustness. Results demonstrate improved tightness over standard scenario approaches.
abstract: We study finite horizon reachable set estimation for unknown discrete-time dynamical systems using only sampled state trajectories. Rather than treating scenario optimization as a black-box tool, we show how it can be tailored to reachable set estimation, where one must learn a family of sets based on whole trajectories, while preserving probabilistic guarantees on future trajectory inclusion for the entire horizon. To this end, we formulate a relaxed scenario program with slack variables that yields a tunable trade-off between reachable set size and out-of-sample trajectory inclusion over the horizon, thereby reducing sensitivity to outliers. Leveraging the recent results in adversarially robust scenario optimization, we then extend this formulation to account for bounded adversarial perturbations of the observed trajectories and derive a posteriori probabilistic guarantees on future trajectory inclusion. When probability distribution shifts in the Wasserstein distance occur, we obtain an explicit bound on how gracefully the theoretical probabilistic guarantees degrade. For different geometries, i.e., $p$-norm balls, ellipsoids, and zonotopes, we derive tractable convex reformulations and corroborate our theoretical results in simulation.
notes: Alert: Tomlin/Lindemann-citation. Core — data-driven reachable set estimation with distributional guarantees — core topic

title: Output-Feedback Safe Control of Discrete-Time Stochastic Systems with Chance Constraints
authors: J. Zhao, Z. Cai, X. Yin
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Expectation-based discrete-time CBFs enforce chance-constraint safety for stochastic systems under noisy measurements.
summary: Zhao et al. develop an output-feedback CBF framework for discrete-time stochastic systems that enforces chance-constrained safety using belief-state dynamics. An expectation-based barrier condition explicitly incorporates estimation uncertainty from partial measurements, and the resulting QP admits real-time implementation. Simulations demonstrate guaranteed safe operation under imperfect sensing.
abstract: In this paper, we investigate safety-critical control problem of discrete-time stochastic systems with incomplete information, where safety constraints must be enforced using state estimates obtained from noisy measurements. We develop an output-feedback control barrier function (CBF) framework based on an expectation-based discrete-time barrier condition that explicitly incorporates estimation uncertainty through the evolving belief over the state. To enable real-time implementation, we derive deterministic sufficient conditions that conservatively enforce the expectation-based CBF by bounding the expectation with computable functions of the belief statistics using Jensen inequalities. The resulting safety filter is formulated as a tractable optimization problem compatible with standard online controllers. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed output-feedback approach achieves fast online computation while providing reliable safety performance in the presence of process noise and measurement uncertainty.
notes: Alert: Herbert. Moderate — CBFs for discrete-time stochastic systems with chance constraints

title: Ternary Logic Encodings of Temporal Behavior Trees with Application to Control Synthesis
authors: R. Matheu, J. Baras, C. Belta
headline: Ternary logic encoding of temporal behavior trees enables online control synthesis beyond post hoc analysis.
summary: Matheu, Baras, and Belta reformulate temporal behavior trees via a ternary-logic semantics that supports online control synthesis rather than only post hoc trace analysis. The encoding preserves the graphical intuitiveness of BTs while enabling formal verification during execution, and connects BT design to existing temporal logic reasoning tools. The approach is demonstrated on long-horizon autonomous tasks.
abstract: Behavior Trees (BTs) provide designers an intuitive graphical interface to construct long-horizon plans for autonomous systems. To ensure their correctness and safety, rigorous formal models and verification techniques are essential. Temporal BTs (TBTs) offer a promising approach by leveraging existing temporal logic formalisms to specify and verify the executions of BTs. However, this analysis is currently limited to offline post hoc analysis and trace repair. In this paper, we reformulate TBTs using a ternary-valued Signal Temporal Logic (STL) amenable for control synthesis. Ternary logic introduces a third truth value \textit{Unknown}, formally capturing cases where a trajectory has neither fully satisfied or dissatisfied a specification. We propose mixed-integer linear encodings for partial trajectory STL and TBTs over ternary logic allowing for correct-by-construction control strategies for linear dynamical systems via mixed-integer optimization. We demonstrate the utility of our framework by solving optimal control problems.
notes: Alert: Lindemann. Moderate — BTs + temporal logic — controller-composition adjacent formal methods

title: Device-Conditioned Neural Architecture Search for Efficient Robotic Manipulation
authors: Y. Wu, H. Wang, Z. Chen, G. Yuan, D. Xu
headline: Device-conditioned quantization unifies efficient visuomotor policy deployment across heterogeneous robot hardware.
summary: Wu et al. propose Device-Conditioned Quantization-For-All (DC-QFA), a unified neural architecture search framework that produces device-specific efficient visuomotor policies without per-device retraining. A device-conditioning signal lets a single trained supernet instantiate compact policies for heterogeneous hardware. Experiments show consistent efficiency gains across multiple devices.
abstract: The growing complexity of visuomotor policies poses significant challenges for deployment with heterogeneous robotic hardware constraints. However, most existing model-efficient approaches for robotic manipulation are device- and model-specific, lack generalizability, and require time-consuming per-device optimization during the adaptation process. In this work, we propose a unified framework named \textbf{D}evice-\textbf{C}onditioned \textbf{Q}uantization-\textbf{F}or-\textbf{A}ll (DC-QFA) which amortizes deployment effort with the device-conditioned quantization-aware training and hardware-constrained architecture search. Specifically, we introduce a single supernet that spans a rich design space over network architectures and mixed-precision bit-widths. It is optimized with latency- and memory-aware regularization, guided by per-device lookup tables. With this supernet, for each target platform, we can perform a once-for-all lightweight search to select an optimal subnet without any per-device re-optimization, which enables more generalizable deployment across heterogeneous hardware, and substantially reduces deployment time. To improve long-horizon stability under low precision, we further introduce multi-step on-policy distillation to mitigate error accumulation during closed-loop execution. Extensive experiments on three representative policy backbones, such as DiffusionPolicy-T, MDT-V, and OpenVLA-OFT, demonstrate that our DC-QFA achieves $2\text{-}3\times$ acceleration on edge devices, consumer-grade GPUs, and cloud platforms, with negligible performance drop in task success. Real-world evaluations on an Inovo robot equipped with a force/torque sensor further validates that our low-bit DC-QFA policies maintain stable, contact-rich manipulation even under severe quantization.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Low — NAS for efficient visuomotor policies — deployment concern, tangential to research [residual -1: focuses on model compression, not policy behavior or verification]

title: Safety Guarantees in Zero-Shot Reinforcement Learning for Cascade Dynamical Systems
authors: S. Rabiei, S. Mishra, S. Paternain
headline: Reduced-order model training yields zero-shot high-probability safety guarantees for cascade dynamical systems.
summary: Rabiei et al. study zero-shot safety for cascade systems where inner states drive outer states. They train a safe RL policy on a reduced-order outer model, treating the inner states as an exogenous action, and derive high-probability safety guarantees for the full cascade system. Simulation experiments confirm transferability without retraining.
abstract: This paper considers the problem of zero-shot safety guarantees for cascade dynamical systems. These are systems where a subset of the states (the inner states) affects the dynamics of the remaining states (the outer states) but not vice-versa. We define safety as remaining on a set deemed safe for all times with high probability. We propose to train a safe RL policy on a reduced-order model, which ignores the dynamics of the inner states, but it treats it as an action that influences the outer state. Thus, reducing the complexity of the training. When deployed in the full system the trained policy is combined with a low-level controller whose task is to track the reference provided by the RL policy. Our main theoretical contribution is a bound on the safe probability in the full-order system. In particular, we establish the interplay between the probability of remaining safe after the zero-shot deployment and the quality of the tracking of the inner states. We validate our theoretical findings on a quadrotor navigation task, demonstrating that the preservation of the safety guarantees is tied to the bandwidth and tracking capabilities of the low-level controller.
notes: Alert: Bansal. Moderate — safe RL with zero-shot safety guarantees on cascade systems

title: WOMBET: World Model-based Experience Transfer for Robust and Sample-efficient Reinforcement Learning
authors: M. Kim, K. Sreenath
headline: World model-based experience transfer jointly generates and uses synthetic data for sample-efficient robot RL.
summary: Kim and Sreenath introduce WOMBET, which learns a world model on a source task and generates reliable transfer data for fine-tuning on a target task. Unlike standard offline-to-online RL that assumes a fixed dataset, WOMBET jointly handles data generation and utilization via the model. Experiments on robotic tasks show robust sample-efficient RL under distribution shift.
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) in robotics is often limited by the cost and risk of data collection, motivating experience transfer from a source task to a target task. Offline-to-online RL leverages prior data but typically assumes a given fixed dataset and does not address how to generate reliable data for transfer. We propose \textit{World Model-based Experience Transfer} (WOMBET), a framework that jointly generates and utilizes prior data. WOMBET learns a world model in the source task and generates offline data via uncertainty-penalized planning, followed by filtering trajectories with high return and low epistemic uncertainty. It then performs online fine-tuning in the target task using adaptive sampling between offline and online data, enabling a stable transition from prior-driven initialization to task-specific adaptation. We show that the uncertainty-penalized objective provides a lower bound on the true return and derive a finite-sample error decomposition capturing distribution mismatch and approximation error. Empirically, WOMBET improves sample efficiency and final performance over strong baselines on continuous control benchmarks, demonstrating the benefit of jointly optimizing data generation and transfer.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy. High — world model for RL experience transfer

title: From Kinematics to Dynamics: Learning to Refine Hybrid Plans for Physically Feasible Execution
authors: L. Erez, S. Shperberg, A. Taitler
headline: Learning refines hybrid temporal plans into physically feasible trajectories with deadlines and velocity limits.
summary: Erez, Shperberg, and Taitler address hybrid discrete-continuous planning where agents must traverse spatial regions under temporal and physical constraints. They learn a refinement layer that converts abstract kinematic plans into dynamically feasible trajectories respecting deadlines, time windows, and acceleration limits. Experiments compare favorably against classical hybrid temporal planners.
abstract: In many robotic tasks, agents must traverse a sequence of spatial regions to complete a mission. Such problems are inherently mixed discrete-continuous: a high-level action sequence and a physically feasible continuous trajectory. The resulting trajectory and action sequence must also satisfy problem constraints such as deadlines, time windows, and velocity or acceleration limits. While hybrid temporal planners attempt to address this challenge, they typically model motion using linear (first-order) dynamics, which cannot guarantee that the resulting plan respects the robot's true physical constraints. Consequently, even when the high-level action sequence is fixed, producing a dynamically feasible trajectory becomes a bi-level optimization problem. We address this problem via reinforcement learning in continuous space. We define a Markov Decision Process that explicitly incorporates analytical second-order constraints and use it to refine first-order plans generated by a hybrid planner. Our results show that this approach can reliably recover physical feasibility and effectively bridge the gap between a planner's initial first-order trajectory and the dynamics required for real execution.
notes: Alert: Fan-citation. Low — learning refinement for hybrid temporal planning — tangential citation from Fan group

title: Robotic Manipulation is Vision-to-Geometry Mapping ($f(v) \rightarrow G$): Vision-Geometry Backbones over Language and Video Models
authors: Z. Song, Q. Li, J. Zhou, Z. Yuan, T. Chen, L. Lin, G. Wang
headline: Vision-geometry backbones trained on 3D priors outperform vision-language and video models for manipulation.
summary: Song et al. argue that manipulation is fundamentally a vision-to-geometry mapping and that generalist robot control should be built on vision-geometry backbones rather than 2D VLA or video priors. They train and benchmark geometry-grounded backbones and show consistent gains over VLA and video-predictive baselines on manipulation tasks. The work reframes the representational foundation of policy learning.
abstract: At its core, robotic manipulation is a problem of vision-to-geometry mapping ($f(v) \rightarrow G$). Physical actions are fundamentally defined by geometric properties like 3D positions and spatial relationships. Consequently, we argue that the foundation for generalizable robotic control should be a vision-geometry backbone, rather than the widely adopted vision-language or video models. Conventional VLA and video-predictive models rely on backbones pretrained on large-scale 2D image-text or temporal pixel data. While effective, their representations are largely shaped by semantic concepts or 2D priors, which do not intrinsically align with the precise 3D geometric nature required for physical manipulation. Driven by this insight, we propose the Vision-Geometry-Action (VGA) model, which directly conditions action generation on pretrained native 3D representations. Specifically, VGA replaces conventional language or video backbones with a pretrained 3D world model, establishing a seamless vision-to-geometry mapping that translates visual inputs directly into physical actions. To further enhance geometric consistency, we introduce a Progressive Volumetric Modulation module and adopt a joint training strategy. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of our approach. In simulation benchmarks, VGA outperforms top-tier VLA baselines including $\pi_{0.5}$ and GeoVLA, demonstrating its superiority in precise manipulation. More importantly, VGA exhibits remarkable zero-shot generalization to unseen viewpoints in real-world deployments, consistently outperforming $\pi_{0.5}$. These results highlight that operating on native 3D representations-rather than translating through language or 2D video priors-is a highly promising direction for achieving generalizable physical intelligence.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — argues for vision-geometry backbones over VLAs — a different take on generalist policy representations

title: Differentially Private Conformal Prediction
authors: J. Wu, C. Zhang, Z. Cai, J. Kong, B. Jiang, L. Kong, L. Kong
headline: Differential conformal prediction avoids data splitting while providing differentially private coverage guarantees.
summary: Wu et al. study conformal prediction under differential privacy and propose differential CP, a non-splitting conformal procedure that serves as a bridge between oracle CP and private conformal inference. By exploiting algorithmic stability, the method retains statistical efficiency without sacrificing DP coverage guarantees. Experiments confirm tighter prediction sets than prior private CP baselines.
abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) has attracted broad attention as a simple and flexible framework for uncertainty quantification through prediction sets. In this work, we study how to deploy CP under differential privacy (DP) in a statistically efficient manner. We first introduce differential CP, a non-splitting conformal procedure that avoids the efficiency loss caused by data splitting and serves as a bridge between oracle CP and private conformal inference. By exploiting the stability properties of DP mechanisms, differential CP establishes a direct connection to oracle CP and inherits corresponding validity behavior. Building on this idea, we develop Differentially Private Conformal Prediction (DPCP), a fully private procedure that combines DP model training with a private quantile mechanism for calibration. We establish the end-to-end privacy guarantee of DPCP and investigate its coverage properties under additional regularity conditions. We further study the efficiency of both differential CP and DPCP under empirical risk minimization and general regression models, showing that DPCP can produce tighter prediction sets than existing private split conformal approaches under the same privacy budget. Numerical experiments on synthetic and real datasets demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed methods.
notes: Alert: Lindemann. Moderate — privacy-aware conformal prediction

title: VAG: Dual-Stream Video-Action Generation for Embodied Data Synthesis
authors: X. Lang, Y. Wang, Y. Zhou, C. Ni, K. Li, J. Zhu, T. Liu, J. Lv, X. Zuo, Y. Ye, G. Huang, X. Wang, Z. Zhu
matched_interests: Flow matching|World models for robotics|Generalist policies|Imitation learning
theme_groups: Diffusion & Flow Policies|VLA & Generalist Policies|Imitation Learning
headline: Flow-matching dual-stream model jointly generates video and action pairs for synthetic embodied pretraining data.
summary: Lang et al. propose VAG, a flow-matching dual-stream world model that jointly generates videos and paired action trajectories under visual and language conditioning. Synchronized denoising across branches plus an adaptive 3D pooling mechanism improves cross-modal consistency, producing aligned video-action pairs usable as synthetic pretraining data. Downstream policies fine-tuned on VAG-synthesized data show improved generalization.
abstract: Recent advances in robot foundation models trained on large-scale human teleoperation data have enabled robots to perform increasingly complex real-world tasks. However, scaling these systems remains difficult because collecting task-specific demonstrations is expensive and labor-intensive. Synthetic data, especially generated videos, offer a promising direction, but existing World Models (WMs) are not directly suitable for policy learning since they do not provide paired action trajectories. World-Action (WA) models partially address this by predicting actions with visual outputs, yet often lack strong video-action alignment, while two-stage pipelines that generate video first and then infer actions introduce inefficiency and error accumulation. To address these limitations, we propose VAG, a unified flow-matching-based dual-stream framework that jointly generates video and action under visual and language conditioning. By synchronizing denoising in both branches and using an adaptive 3D pooling mechanism to transfer compact global video context to the action branch, VAG improves cross-modal consistency during generation. Across both simulated and real-world settings, VAG produces aligned video-action pairs with competitive prediction quality, supports executable trajectory replay, and provides useful synthetic pretraining data that improves downstream policy generalization, indicating its potential as a practical world-action model for embodied data synthesis.
notes: Alert: Majumdar-citation. High — flow matching for paired video-action synthesis — combines world models, IL, and flow matching

title: Towards Verified and Targeted Explanations through Formal Methods
authors: H. Wang, D. Lopez, P. Robinette, I. Oguz, T. Johnson, M. Ma
headline: ViTaX framework generates formally verified, targeted semifactual explanations with mathematical guarantees for DNNs.
summary: Wang et al. present ViTaX, a formal XAI framework that produces targeted semifactual explanations with mathematical guarantees, combining the interpretability of attribution methods with the rigor of formal neural network verification. The targeted formulation avoids the untargeted nearest-boundary bias of standard formal methods and delivers more useful explanations in safety-critical domains like autonomous driving and medical imaging. Experiments show stronger fidelity than LIME and Integrated Gradients.
abstract: As deep neural networks are deployed in safety-critical domains such as autonomous driving and medical diagnosis, stakeholders need explanations that are interpretable but also trustworthy with formal guarantees. Existing XAI methods fall short: heuristic attribution techniques (e.g., LIME, Integrated Gradients) highlight influential features but offer no mathematical guarantees about decision boundaries, while formal methods verify robustness yet remain untargeted, analyzing the nearest boundary regardless of whether it represents a critical risk. In safety-critical systems, not all misclassifications carry equal consequences; confusing a "Stop" sign for a "60 kph" sign is far more dangerous than confusing it with a "No Passing" sign. We introduce ViTaX (Verified and Targeted Explanations), a formal XAI framework that generates targeted semifactual explanations with mathematical guarantees. For a given input (class y) and a user-specified critical alternative (class t), ViTaX: (1) identifies the minimal feature subset most sensitive to the y->t transition, and (2) applies formal reachability analysis to guarantee that perturbing these features by epsilon cannot flip the classification to t. We formalize this through Targeted epsilon-Robustness, certifying whether a feature subset remains robust under perturbation toward a specific target class. ViTaX is the first method to provide formally guaranteed explanations of a model's resilience against user-identified alternatives. Evaluations on MNIST, GTSRB, EMNIST, and TaxiNet demonstrate over 30% fidelity improvement with minimal explanation cardinality.
notes: Alert: Lindemann-citation. Moderate — formal NN verification applied to explanations [residual -1: targets XAI, not controller/policy verification]

title: BiTAgent: A Task-Aware Modular Framework for Bidirectional Coupling between Multimodal Large Language Models and World Models
authors: Y. Zhan, X. Wang, P. Mao, T. Feng, R. Wang, W. Zhu
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Generalist policies
headline: BiTAgent bidirectionally couples multimodal LLMs and world models via task-aware modular gating.
summary: Zhan et al. introduce BiTAgent (aka ModularAgent), a task-aware modular framework that establishes tight bidirectional coupling between multimodal LLMs and learned world models. A lightweight gating network routes features to adaptively balance semantic priors from the MLLM and latent dynamics from the WM per task, mitigating gradient conflicts across heterogeneous tasks. Experiments show improved multi-task and cross-environment generalization for embodied agents.
abstract: Building generalist embodied agents requires a unified system that can interpret multimodal goals, model environment dynamics, and execute reliable actions across diverse real-world tasks. Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) offer strong semantic priors and cross-modal generalization, while world models (WMs) provide actionable latent dynamics for prediction and control. Their combination holds promise for open-ended embodied intelligence, yet introduces two key challenges: (1) establishing a tight coupling between the semantic intent from MLLMs and the dynamic state representations within the WM's latent space, and (2) achieving task-aware adaptability that supports multi-task learning and cross-environment generalization. To address these limitations, we propose BiTAgent, a task-aware dynamic joint framework that enables bidirectional coupling between MLLMs and WMs. BiTAgent establishes two complementary pathways: a forward path that injects MLLM representations into the WM's latent space for semantically guided imagination, and a backward path where WM-generated feedback refines the MLLM's semantic space via dense text-conditioned rewards. This bidirectional interaction is realized through three synergistic components: Task-Aware Dynamic Joint Learning, Task-Aware Behavior Learning, and MLLM-WM Joint Optimization, which together harmonize semantic reasoning and dynamic prediction. Extensive experiments across multi-task and cross-environment settings demonstrate superior stability and generalization over state-of-the-art baselines, marking a step toward open-ended embodied learning.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy. High — coupling MLLMs with world models for embodied control

title: Conformal Koopman Operator for Optimal Embedded Nonlinear Control
authors: Ryo Hirano, Yusuke Hori, Hiroki Semba
matched_interests: Conformal prediction|Koopman operator methods|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Conformal prediction meets Koopman operators for certifiably safe nonlinear control
summary: Combines conformal prediction with Koopman operator lifting to provide distribution-free safety guarantees for nonlinear control systems. The method embeds nonlinear dynamics into a linear space via Koopman operators, then wraps the resulting predictions with conformal prediction sets to ensure coverage guarantees. Demonstrates optimal embedded control with provable safety bounds on several nonlinear benchmarks.
abstract: This paper proposes a conformal Koopman operator framework for optimal embedded nonlinear control with statistical safety guarantees. We lift the nonlinear dynamics into a higher-dimensional linear space via Koopman operators and apply conformal prediction to quantify the uncertainty of the lifted model. The conformalized Koopman model is then used for model predictive control (MPC) with probabilistic safety constraints. We prove that the resulting controller satisfies finite-sample coverage guarantees under exchangeability assumptions. Numerical experiments on nonlinear control benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves near-optimal performance while maintaining the desired safety level, outperforming baselines that ignore model uncertainty or use conservative uncertainty bounds.
notes: High — conformal prediction + Koopman for safe control, directly relevant to data-driven verification [residual +1: novel intersection of conformal prediction, Koopman methods, and safety-critical control — three core interests]

title: Data-Driven Reachability Analysis of Nonlinear Lipschitz Systems via Koopman Operators and Zonotopes
authors: Paria Akhormeh, Majid Zamani
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Koopman operator methods|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Koopman-based zonotope reachability analysis for data-driven verification of nonlinear systems
summary: Presents a data-driven reachability analysis method for nonlinear Lipschitz systems using Koopman operator approximations and zonotope set representations. The approach lifts nonlinear dynamics into a linear Koopman space and propagates reachable sets as zonotopes, enabling efficient over-approximation of reachable states from data alone. Provides formal guarantees on the tightness of the over-approximation.
abstract: We propose a data-driven method for reachability analysis of nonlinear systems satisfying a Lipschitz continuity condition. Our approach leverages the Koopman operator to lift the nonlinear dynamics into a higher-dimensional linear system, where reachable sets can be efficiently computed using zonotope propagation. We derive error bounds for the Koopman approximation from finite data and incorporate these bounds into the reachable set computation to ensure formal over-approximation guarantees. The resulting method requires no explicit model of the dynamics and scales favorably with system dimension. We demonstrate the approach on several nonlinear benchmark systems, showing tighter reachable set estimates compared to existing data-driven methods while maintaining formal soundness.
notes: Core — data-driven reachability via Koopman, directly relevant to verification of black-box systems

title: Speculative Verification of VLA Policies
authors: Jiawei Chen, Simin Huang, Jiawei Du, Shuaihang Chen, Yu Tian, Mingjie Wei, Chao Yu, Zhaoxia Yin
matched_interests: Runtime verification of learned policies|Verification of visuomotor policies|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Data-driven verification
headline: Runtime speculative verification framework catches VLA policy failures before execution
summary: Introduces a speculative verification framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies that predicts and catches potential failures before action execution. The method runs a lightweight verifier in parallel with the VLA policy to speculatively check proposed actions against safety and task constraints. Demonstrates significant failure reduction on manipulation benchmarks with minimal computational overhead.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown impressive generalization in robotic manipulation, but their black-box nature raises safety concerns for real-world deployment. We propose Speculative Verification, a runtime framework that verifies VLA policy outputs before execution. Our method pairs a VLA policy with a lightweight verification module that speculatively evaluates proposed actions against learned safety constraints and task specifications. When the verifier flags a potentially unsafe or task-violating action, the system triggers a fallback mechanism. We evaluate on multiple manipulation benchmarks and show that speculative verification reduces task failures by 40-60% with less than 10% computational overhead, enabling safer deployment of VLA policies without retraining.
notes: Core — runtime verification of VLA policies, directly addresses verification of learned black-box controllers

title: Risk-Constrained Model Predictive Control for Safe Navigation Under Conformal Prediction Sets
authors: Ifeanyi Enwerem, Arie Israel, Arie Covello
headline: Conformal prediction sets enable risk-aware MPC for safe robot navigation
summary: Integrates conformal prediction uncertainty sets into risk-constrained model predictive control for safe autonomous navigation. The conformal sets provide distribution-free coverage guarantees on obstacle positions and dynamics uncertainty, which are then used as constraints in the MPC formulation. Demonstrates safe navigation in uncertain environments with formal risk bounds.
abstract: We present a risk-constrained model predictive control (MPC) framework that leverages conformal prediction sets for safe robot navigation under uncertainty. Conformal prediction provides distribution-free, finite-sample coverage guarantees on uncertain quantities such as obstacle positions, dynamic model errors, and sensor noise. We incorporate these prediction sets as constraints in a risk-aware MPC formulation, ensuring that the planned trajectory satisfies probabilistic safety guarantees. Our approach adapts the conformal prediction sets online as new observations arrive, maintaining valid coverage even under distribution shift. We validate the framework in simulation and on a ground robot navigating among dynamic obstacles, demonstrating safe navigation with rigorous risk bounds while maintaining near-optimal path efficiency.
notes: High — conformal prediction for safe MPC navigation, relevant to data-driven safety

title: Continuous-Time Safety Filter Design via Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability with Neural Operator
authors: Yankai Li, Mo Chen
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Safe & robust robot control|Data-driven verification
headline: Neural operators accelerate Hamilton-Jacobi reachability for continuous-time safety filtering
summary: Proposes using neural operators to solve Hamilton-Jacobi PDEs for continuous-time safety filter design, dramatically accelerating computation compared to grid-based methods. The neural operator learns to map system parameters to value functions, enabling real-time safety filtering. Demonstrates the approach on several dynamical systems with formal safety guarantees inherited from HJ reachability theory.
abstract: Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability analysis provides a principled framework for safety-critical control through the computation of backward reachable sets and optimal safety controllers. However, solving the associated HJ partial differential equation (PDE) is computationally expensive, limiting real-time applicability. We propose a neural operator approach that learns to map system parameters and constraints directly to the value function solution of the HJ PDE. Once trained, the neural operator enables real-time continuous-time safety filter design by rapidly evaluating the value function and its gradient for any new system configuration. We provide theoretical analysis showing that the safety guarantees of HJ reachability are preserved up to the approximation error of the neural operator, and demonstrate on several dynamical systems that our approach achieves orders-of-magnitude speedup over grid-based solvers while maintaining high accuracy.
notes: High — neural operator for HJ reachability, accelerating safety filter computation

title: SABER: Stealthy Adversarial Black-box Attacks on VLA-based Robots
authors: Jiawei Chen, Simin Huang, Jiawei Du, Shuaihang Chen, Yu Tian, Mingjie Wei, Chao Yu, Zhaoxia Yin
matched_interests: Adversarial robustness of robot policies|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: Black-box adversarial attacks expose critical vulnerabilities in VLA robot policies
summary: Presents SABER, a method for stealthy adversarial black-box attacks against Vision-Language-Action robot policies. The attack requires no access to model internals, using only observed inputs and outputs to craft imperceptible perturbations that cause task failures. Demonstrates high attack success rates across multiple VLA architectures, highlighting the need for robustness verification of deployed VLA systems.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are increasingly deployed for robotic manipulation, yet their adversarial robustness remains largely unexplored. We introduce SABER (Stealthy Adversarial Black-box Exploits for Robots), a black-box attack framework that generates imperceptible perturbations to visual inputs, causing VLA-based robots to fail at manipulation tasks. SABER requires no access to model weights or gradients, relying only on query access to the policy. Our method uses a combination of transfer-based priors and query-efficient optimization to craft perturbations that are visually imperceptible but cause significant deviations in the robot action output. We evaluate SABER on multiple state-of-the-art VLA models across diverse manipulation tasks, achieving attack success rates exceeding 80% with perturbation budgets below human perceptibility thresholds. Our findings underscore the urgent need for adversarial robustness evaluation and defense mechanisms for VLA-based robotic systems.
notes: High — black-box adversarial attacks on VLA policies, relevant to verification and robustness testing

title: Safety and Security of Vision-Language-Action Models: Threats and Countermeasures
authors: Manoj Parmar
matched_interests: Adversarial robustness of robot policies|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Comprehensive survey of safety threats and defenses for VLA robot models
summary: Provides a comprehensive survey of safety and security threats facing Vision-Language-Action models in robotics, covering adversarial attacks, prompt injection, distribution shift, and physical-world vulnerabilities. Categorizes existing defenses and proposes a taxonomy of countermeasures. Identifies open challenges for making VLA-based robots safe for real-world deployment.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models represent a promising paradigm for building generalist robot policies by combining visual perception, language understanding, and action generation in a unified framework. However, their increasing deployment in safety-critical robotic applications raises significant concerns about safety and security. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of threats and countermeasures for VLA models. We categorize threats into adversarial attacks on visual and language inputs, prompt injection and jailbreaking, out-of-distribution failures, physical-world attacks, and privacy concerns. For each threat category, we review existing attack methods and evaluate their applicability to VLA-based robotic systems. We then survey defense mechanisms including adversarial training, certified robustness, runtime monitoring, and safe fallback strategies. Finally, we identify key open challenges and research directions for ensuring the safe and secure deployment of VLA models in real-world robotics.
notes: High — survey of VLA safety/security, relevant to verification and robustness of learned policies

title: Neural Network Verification via Semidefinite Programming: New Bounds with Primal and Dual Approaches
authors: Jaeuk Shin, Jungjin Lee, Insoon Yang
headline: Tighter SDP relaxation bounds for neural network verification via primal-dual methods
summary: Develops new semidefinite programming (SDP) bounds for neural network verification using both primal and dual formulations. The approach provides tighter verification bounds than existing LP-based relaxations while remaining computationally tractable. Demonstrates improved certification rates on standard benchmarks compared to prior SDP-based methods.
abstract: We present new semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxations for neural network verification that provide tighter bounds than existing approaches. Our framework considers both primal and dual SDP formulations, exploiting the structure of ReLU activations to derive strengthened constraints. On the primal side, we introduce novel valid inequalities that capture pairwise relationships between neurons across layers. On the dual side, we derive a computationally efficient bound that can be evaluated in closed form for networks with a moderate number of layers. We prove that our bounds are strictly tighter than existing LP and first-order SDP relaxations, and we provide conditions under which the gap is significant. Experiments on standard verification benchmarks demonstrate improved certification rates with competitive running times.
notes: Moderate — NN verification via SDP, tangentially related to verification but purely theoretical with no robotics component [residual -1: pure NN verification theory, no data-driven or robotics component]

title: Smooth Parameterization of Rigid-Body Inertial Parameters for Robot Design Optimization
authors: Samet Uzun, Behçet Açıkmeşe
headline: Smooth inertial parameterization enables gradient-based robot design optimization
summary: Proposes a smooth parameterization of rigid-body inertial parameters that ensures physical consistency (positive-definite inertia tensors) while enabling gradient-based optimization for robot design. The parameterization avoids discontinuities and singularities that plague existing representations. Demonstrates application to co-design of robot morphology and controllers.
abstract: We present a smooth parameterization of rigid-body inertial parameters—mass, center of mass, and inertia tensor—that is particularly suited for gradient-based robot design optimization. Our parameterization guarantees physical consistency (positive mass and positive-definite inertia tensor) by construction, while maintaining smoothness properties essential for gradient-based optimization. Unlike existing approaches that use Cholesky decomposition or log-barrier formulations, our method provides a bijective mapping between unconstrained Euclidean space and the space of physically valid inertial parameters, with well-conditioned Jacobians. We demonstrate the utility of our parameterization in co-optimization of robot morphology and controllers, showing faster convergence and better optima compared to alternative parameterizations on several robot design benchmarks.
notes: Low — robot design optimization, not directly related to verification or policy learning

title: Goal-conditioned Offline Reinforcement Learning with Diffusion Model
authors: Tomoya Kamimura, Haruka Washiyama, Akihito Sano
headline: Diffusion models enable goal-conditioned offline RL without reward engineering
summary: Combines diffusion models with goal-conditioned offline reinforcement learning to learn policies from reward-free datasets. The diffusion model generates trajectory plans conditioned on goal states, and a value function guides the denoising process toward high-return trajectories. Demonstrates competitive performance on goal-reaching benchmarks without requiring reward labels.
abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) aims to learn policies from pre-collected datasets without online interaction. Goal-conditioned offline RL further requires reaching specified goal states, but faces challenges due to sparse rewards and limited goal coverage in offline data. We propose a diffusion model-based approach that generates trajectory plans conditioned on goal states. Our method trains a conditional diffusion model on offline trajectory data and uses a learned value function to guide the reverse diffusion process toward high-return trajectories. The diffusion model captures the multimodal distribution of trajectories in the offline dataset, while the value guidance ensures that generated plans are both feasible and goal-reaching. We evaluate on standard goal-conditioned offline RL benchmarks and demonstrate competitive or superior performance compared to existing methods, particularly in sparse-reward settings where traditional approaches struggle.
notes: High — diffusion for goal-conditioned offline RL, relevant to diffusion policy methods

title: Formal Runtime Verification of Autonomous Driving using Diffeomorphism-based Signal Temporal Logic
authors: Hongrui Zheng, Zirui Zang, Ahmad Amine, Cristian Ioan Vasile, Rahul Mangharam
matched_interests: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Runtime verification of learned policies|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Diffeomorphism-based STL enables formal runtime verification of autonomous driving policies
summary: Introduces a diffeomorphism-based extension of Signal Temporal Logic for formal runtime verification of autonomous driving systems. The approach transforms complex road geometries into canonical forms where STL specifications are easier to define and monitor. Demonstrates real-time runtime monitoring of learned driving policies with formal guarantees on specification satisfaction.
abstract: Runtime verification of autonomous driving systems requires monitoring complex spatio-temporal behaviors against formal specifications. However, expressing safety and performance requirements in curved road geometries using standard Signal Temporal Logic (STL) is cumbersome and error-prone. We propose diffeomorphism-based STL, which applies smooth coordinate transformations to map complex road geometries into canonical straight-road frames where STL specifications are natural and intuitive. The diffeomorphism preserves the logical structure of STL formulas while enabling specification of lane-keeping, safe following distances, and traffic rule compliance in a geometry-independent manner. We implement an efficient runtime monitor that evaluates diffeomorphism-based STL specifications in real time and demonstrate its application to verifying learned autonomous driving policies on diverse road geometries, including highways, intersections, and roundabouts.
notes: High — formal runtime verification of learned driving policies via STL, relevant to verification and formal methods

title: SkillFlow: Efficient Diffusion-based Policy via Composable Skill Flows
authors: Jiahua Ma, Yiran Qin, Xin Wen, Yixiong Li, Yuyu Sun, Yulan Guo, Liang Lin, Ruimao Zhang
headline: Composable skill flows enable modular and efficient diffusion-based robot policies
summary: Introduces SkillFlow, a framework for composing modular skill-level flow models into complex robot behaviors. Each skill is represented as a separate flow-based generative model, and skills are composed at inference time through flow composition operators. Demonstrates that modular composition achieves better generalization to novel task combinations than monolithic diffusion policies.
abstract: Diffusion-based robot policies have shown remarkable performance in learning complex manipulation behaviors, but training a single monolithic model for every task combination is sample-inefficient and limits generalization to novel compositions. We propose SkillFlow, a framework that decomposes complex tasks into composable skill-level flow models. Each skill is learned as an independent conditional flow matching model that maps noise to skill-specific action trajectories. At inference time, skills are composed through principled flow composition operators that combine individual skill flows into coherent multi-step behaviors. We introduce temporal and spatial composition operators that enable sequential and parallel skill execution, respectively. SkillFlow achieves superior performance on multi-step manipulation tasks compared to monolithic baselines, with significantly improved generalization to unseen skill combinations and reduced training data requirements.
notes: High — composable flow-based skill policies, relevant to diffusion/flow methods and controller composition

title: Safety-Guaranteed Planning and Control of Multi-Robot Systems under Signal Temporal Logic Specifications Using Control Barrier Functions
authors: Peiqi Yu, Philip Huang, Chaitanya Chawla, Guanya Shi, Jiaoyang Li, Changliu Liu
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: CBF-based controllers enforce STL specifications for provably safe multi-robot coordination
summary: Combines Control Barrier Functions with Signal Temporal Logic specifications to achieve safety-guaranteed planning and control for multi-robot systems. STL specifications encode complex temporal tasks (sequencing, deadlines, spatial constraints), and CBFs enforce these specifications as real-time safety constraints. Demonstrates scalable multi-robot coordination with formal safety guarantees.
abstract: We present a framework for safety-guaranteed planning and control of multi-robot systems subject to Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications. STL enables expressive specification of complex temporal tasks including sequential objectives, time deadlines, and spatial constraints. Our approach translates STL specifications into Control Barrier Function (CBF) constraints that can be enforced in real time through a quadratic program-based controller. We address the key challenge of composing multiple STL specifications for multi-robot coordination by introducing a hierarchical decomposition that assigns local STL tasks to individual robots while maintaining global safety through inter-robot CBF constraints.
