Flow-based density estimators prove most effective, outperforming SOTA failure detection baselines in speed and accuracy across diverse manipulation tasks.
abstract: Recent years have witnessed impressive robotic manipulation systems driven by advances in imitation learning and generative modeling, such as diffusion- and flow-based approaches. As robot policy performance increases, so does the complexity and time horizon of achievable tasks, inducing unexpected and diverse failure modes that are difficult to predict a priori. To enable trustworthy policy deployment in safety-critical human environments, reliable runtime failure detection becomes important during policy inference. However, most existing failure detection approaches rely on prior knowledge of failure modes and require failure data during training, which imposes a significant challenge in practicality and scalability. In response to these limitations, we present FAIL-Detect, a modular two-stage approach for failure detection in imitation learning-based robotic manipulation. To accurately identify failures from successful training data alone, we frame the problem as sequential out-of-distribution (OOD) detection. We first distill policy inputs and outputs into scalar signals that correlate with policy failures and capture epistemic uncertainty. FAIL-Detect then employs conformal prediction (CP) as a versatile framework for uncertainty quantification with statistical guarantees. Empirically, we thoroughly investigate both learned and post-hoc scalar signal candidates on diverse robotic manipulation tasks. Our experiments show learned signals to be mostly consistently effective, particularly when using our novel flow-based density estimator. Furthermore, our method detects failures more accurately and faster than state-of-the-art (SOTA) failure detection baselines.
notes: Core — CP-based runtime failure detection for IL policies without failure data; directly relevant to data-driven verification of visuomotor policies [residual +1: directly addresses verification of learned manipulation policies using conformal prediction]

title: ROVER: Regulator-Driven Robust Temporal Verification of Black-Box Robot Policies
authors: Kristy Sakano, Jianyu An, Dinesh Manocha, Huan Xu
headline: Regulator-in-the-loop temporal verification of black-box robot policies using STL specifications
summary: ROVER introduces a regulator-driven approach for temporal verification of black-box autonomous robot policies using prioritized Signal Temporal Logic specifications. It uses Total Robustness Value and Largest Robustness Value metrics to quantify performance, and introduces Average Violation Robustness Value. Regulator-guided retraining increased STL satisfaction rates by 43.8% on average across driving and mobile robot navigation.
abstract: We present a novel, regulator-driven approach for the temporal verification of black-box autonomous robot policies, inspired by real-world certification processes where regulators often evaluate observable behavior without access to model internals. Central to our method is a regulator-in-the-loop approach that evaluates execution traces from black-box policies against temporal safety requirements. These requirements, expressed as prioritized Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications, characterize behavior changes over time and encode domain knowledge into the verification process. We use Total Robustness Value (TRV) and Largest Robustness Value (LRV) to quantify average performance and worst-case adherence, and introduce Average Violation Robustness Value (AVRV) to measure average specification violation. Together, these metrics guide targeted retraining and iterative model improvement. Our approach accommodates diverse temporal safety requirements, capturing persistence, sequencing, and response across two distinct domains. Across six STL specifications in both scenarios, regulator-guided retraining increased satisfaction rates by an average of 43.8%. Real-world validation on a TurtleBot3 robot demonstrates a 27% improvement in smooth-navigation satisfaction.
notes: Core — black-box temporal verification using STL; directly addresses data-driven verification of black-box policies [residual +1: directly addresses black-box verification of robot policies]

title: Safety with Agency: Human-Centered Safety Filter with Application to AI-Assisted Motorsports
authors: Donggeon David Oh, Justin Lidard, Haimin Hu, Himani Sinhmar, Elle Lazarski, Deepak Edakkattil Gopinath, Emily Sumner, Jonathan Decastro, Guy Rosman, Naomi Leonard, Jaime Fernández Fisac
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: Neural safety value function learned via black-box interactions enforces Q-CBF safety without dynamics knowledge
summary: Proposes a human-centered safety filter built on a neural safety value function learned through black-box interactions. At deployment, it enforces a novel Q-CBF safety constraint requiring no dynamics knowledge. User study in high-fidelity racing shows improved safety and satisfaction without compromising human agency, outperforming conventional safety filters.
abstract: We propose a human-centered safety filter (HCSF) for shared autonomy that significantly enhances system safety without compromising human agency. Our HCSF is built on a neural safety value function, which we first learn scalably through black-box interactions and then use at deployment to enforce a novel state-action control barrier function (Q-CBF) safety constraint. Since this Q-CBF safety filter does not require any knowledge of the system dynamics for both synthesis and runtime safety monitoring and intervention, our method applies readily to complex, black-box shared autonomy systems. Notably, our HCSF CBF-based interventions modify the human actions minimally and smoothly. We validate our approach in a comprehensive in-person user study using Assetto Corsa, a high-fidelity car racing simulator with black-box dynamics.
notes: Core — Q-CBF safety filter learned from black-box interactions, no dynamics needed; directly relevant to data-driven verification [residual +1: black-box safety filter learned without dynamics model, core to research focus]

title: Safe Probabilistic Planning for Human-Robot Interaction using Conformal Risk Control
authors: Jake Gonzales, Kazuki Mizuta, Karen Leung, Lillian J. Ratliff
matched_interests: Conformal prediction|Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: Conformal risk control dynamically adjusts CBF safety margins for human-robot interaction
summary: Combines CBFs with conformal risk control to provide formal safety guarantees during human-robot interaction. The approach quantifies and controls prediction errors in CBF safety values and dynamically adjusts safety margins based on interaction context. Experiments show significant reduction in collision rates while maintaining high success rates.
abstract: In this paper, we present a novel probabilistic safe control framework for human-robot interaction that combines control barrier functions (CBFs) with conformal risk control to provide formal safety guarantees while considering complex human behavior. The approach uses conformal risk control to quantify and control the prediction errors in CBF safety values and establishes formal guarantees on the probability of constraint satisfaction during interaction. We introduce an algorithm that dynamically adjusts the safety margins produced by conformal risk control based on the current interaction context.
notes: Core — conformal risk control + CBFs for safe HRI with formal guarantees

title: AnySafe: Adapting Latent Safety Filters at Runtime via Safety Constraint Parameterization in the Latent Space
authors: Sankalp Agrawal, Junwon Seo, Kensuke Nakamura, Ran Tian, Andrea Bajcsy
matched_interests: Latent-space safety analysis|Data-driven verification|Conformal prediction|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability
headline: Runtime-adaptive latent safety filters with conformal calibration for arbitrary constraint images
summary: Proposes constraint-parameterized latent safety filters that adapt to user-specified safety constraints at runtime. Safety constraints are defined by conditioning on image encodings with latent-space similarity, aligned through conformal calibration. Trained entirely in world model imagination, enabling adaptation to arbitrary constraints at deployment. Demonstrated on Franka manipulator.
abstract: Recent works have shown that foundational safe control methods, such as Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability analysis, can be applied in the latent space of world models. While this enables the synthesis of latent safety filters for hard-to-model vision-based tasks, they assume that the safety constraint is known a priori and remains fixed during deployment, limiting the safety filter adaptability across scenarios. To address this, we propose constraint-parameterized latent safety filters that can adapt to user-specified safety constraints at runtime. Our key idea is to define safety constraints by conditioning on an encoding of an image that represents a constraint, using a latent-space similarity measure. The notion of similarity to failure is aligned in a principled way through conformal calibration, which controls how closely the system may approach the constraint representation.
notes: Core — latent safety filters + conformal calibration + HJ reachability; extends Bajcsy group latent safety work [residual +1: combines latent-space verification, conformal prediction, and HJ reachability — intersection of 3+ core interests]

title: Deployment-Time Reliability of Learned Robot Policies
authors: Christopher Agia
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Verification of visuomotor policies|Runtime verification of learned policies
headline: Dissertation on runtime monitoring, interpretability, and reliable long-horizon execution for learned policies
summary: Investigates deployment-time reliability through three mechanisms: runtime monitoring that detects failures without failure data, a data-centric interpretability framework using influence functions, and feasibility-aware task planning for long-horizon execution. The dissertation addresses core challenges of distribution shift and compounding errors in learned manipulation policies.
abstract: Recent advances in learning-based robot manipulation have produced policies with remarkable capabilities. Yet, reliability at deployment remains a fundamental barrier to real-world use, where distribution shift, compounding errors, and complex task dependencies collectively undermine system performance. This dissertation investigates how the reliability of learned robot policies can be improved at deployment time through mechanisms that operate around them.
notes: Core — runtime monitoring and interpretability for learned policies; directly relevant to verification of visuomotor policies

title: ROBOGATE: Adaptive Failure Discovery for Safe Robot Policy Deployment via Two-Stage Boundary-Focused Sampling
authors: Azuki Kim
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Verification of visuomotor policies|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: Adaptive failure boundary discovery reveals 97% cross-simulator gap in VLA policy deployment
summary: ROBOGATE uses physics simulation with two-stage adaptive sampling to discover failure boundaries in high-dimensional parameter spaces. Testing 8 VLA policies including fine-tuned GR00T N1.6 reveals a 97.65 percentage point cross-simulator gap (MuJoCo vs Isaac Sim), underscoring the deployment validation challenge for Physical AI.
abstract: Deploying learned robot manipulation policies in industrial settings requires rigorous pre-deployment validation, yet exhaustive testing across high-dimensional parameter spaces is intractable. We present ROBOGATE, a deployment risk management framework that combines physics-based simulation with a two-stage adaptive sampling strategy to efficiently discover failure boundaries in the operational parameter space.
notes: Core — pre-deployment failure boundary discovery for VLA policies; directly relevant to data-driven verification

title: Computationally and Sample Efficient Safe Reinforcement Learning Using Adaptive Conformal Prediction
authors: Hao Zhou, Yanze Zhang, Wenhao Luo
matched_interests: Conformal prediction|Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Active learning
headline: Adaptive conformal prediction + CBFs enable provably sample-efficient safe RL exploration
summary: Proposes a sample-efficient episodic safe learning framework combining Quadrature Fourier Features for GP approximation, Adaptive Conformal Prediction for uncertainty quantification, and CBFs for safety constraints. An optimism-based exploration strategy integrates with ACP-based CBFs for safe exploration and near-optimal nonlinear control.
abstract: Safety is a critical concern in learning-enabled autonomous systems especially when deploying these systems in real-world scenarios. An important challenge is accurately quantifying the uncertainty of unknown models to generate provably safe control policies that facilitate the gathering of informative data. In this paper, we propose a provably sample efficient episodic safe learning framework that remains robust across various model choices with quantified uncertainty for online control tasks.
notes: Core — ACP + CBFs for safe RL; combines conformal prediction, safety, and active learning

title: CN-CBF: Composite Neural Control Barrier Function for Safe Robot Navigation in Dynamic Environments
authors: Bojan Derajic, Sebastian Bernhard, Wolfgang Hönig
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Composite neural CBFs trained via HJ reachability improve safe navigation by 18% over baselines
summary: Proposes combining multiple neural CBFs into a composite CBF for dynamic obstacle avoidance. Individual CBFs are trained via HJ reachability to approximate optimal safe sets. A residual architecture guarantees non-intersection with failure sets. Evaluated on ground robots and quadrotors in simulation and hardware.
abstract: Safe navigation of autonomous robots remains one of the core challenges in the field. We propose a simple yet effective neural CBF design method for safe robot navigation in dynamic environments using composite CBFs trained via Hamilton-Jacobi reachability.
notes: High — neural CBFs via HJ reachability for dynamic navigation; combines two core method interests

title: SafeMimic: Towards Safe and Autonomous Human-to-Robot Imitation for Mobile Manipulation
authors: Arpit Bahety, Arnav Balaji, Ben Abbatematteo, Roberto Martin-Martin
matched_interests: Safe & robust robot control|Imitation learning|Data-driven verification
headline: Safety Q-function ensemble verifies actions before execution in one-shot human-to-robot imitation
summary: SafeMimic learns mobile manipulation from a single human video by parsing demonstrations, translating to egocentric reference, and verifying candidate actions for safety using an ensemble of safety Q-functions trained in simulation. Includes backtracking when safe progression fails. Demonstrated across seven tasks.
abstract: We present SAFEMIMIC, a framework to learn new mobile manipulation skills safely and autonomously from a single third-person human video. SafeMimic verifies actions for safety before execution in a receding horizon fashion using an ensemble of safety Q-functions trained in simulation.
notes: High — safety verification of actions during IL from human video; combines safety and imitation learning

title: Demystifying Diffusion Policies: Action Memorization and Simple Lookup Table Alternatives
authors: Chengyang He, Xu Liu, Gadiel Sznaier Camps, Guillaume Sartoretti, Mac Schwager
headline: Diffusion policies memorize action lookup tables; lightweight ALT matches performance with OOD detection
summary: Reveals that diffusion policies essentially memorize action lookup tables from training data rather than learning to generalize actions. Proposes Action Lookup Table (ALT) using contrastive encoders, matching diffusion policy performance with 0.34% inference time. ALT also provides explicit OOD runtime monitoring, relevant to policy verification.
abstract: Diffusion policies have demonstrated remarkable dexterity and robustness in robot manipulation tasks. In this paper, we offer a surprising hypothesis: diffusion policies essentially memorize an action lookup table. We propose ALT, a lightweight alternative that matches performance with 0.0034 of the inference time and includes an explicit OOD flag for runtime monitoring.
notes: High — insight into diffusion policy behavior + OOD monitoring; relevant to understanding and verifying diffusion policies

title: FlowCorrect: Efficient Interactive Correction of Generative Flow Policies for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Edgar Welte, Yitian Shi, Rosa Wolf, Maximillian Gilles, Rania Rayyes
headline: Sparse human corrections adapt flow-matching policies at deployment without retraining
summary: FlowCorrect enables deployment-time adaptation of flow-matching manipulation policies from sparse corrective pose nudges via VR interface, without retraining. Achieves 80% success on previously failed cases while preserving performance on solved scenarios. Demonstrates sample-efficient human-in-the-loop correction.
abstract: Generative manipulation policies can fail catastrophically under deployment-time distribution shift, yet many failures are near-misses. We propose FlowCorrect, a modular interactive imitation learning approach that enables deployment-time adaptation of flow-matching manipulation policies from sparse, relative human corrections without retraining.
notes: High — deployment-time flow policy correction; relevant to flow matching and practical policy adaptation

title: Uncertainty Comes for Free: Human-in-the-Loop Policies with Diffusion Models
authors: Zhanpeng He, Yifeng Cao, Matei Ciocarlie
headline: Diffusion policy generative process yields free uncertainty metric for selective human assistance
summary: Leverages the inherent generative process of diffusion policies to compute uncertainty metrics, enabling the agent to request human help only when necessary. The same uncertainty method supports efficient data collection for fine-tuning. Validated in simulation and real-world.
abstract: We propose a method that allows diffusion policies to actively seek human assistance only when necessary by leveraging the generative process to compute an uncertainty-based metric, without requiring any operator interaction during training.
notes: High — uncertainty quantification from diffusion policies; relevant to verification and monitoring

title: CCDP: Composition of Conditional Diffusion Policies with Guided Sampling
authors: Amirreza Razmjoo, Sylvain Calinon, Michael Gienger, Fan Zhang
headline: Guided sampling composes conditional diffusion policies for recovery from failures
summary: Enhanced sampling strategy refines the diffusion policy distribution to avoid previously unsuccessful actions. Uses diffusion model decomposition to break long-horizon problems into manageable sub-problems, enabling dynamic adaptation. Validated on door opening, manipulation, and button-searching tasks.
abstract: We propose an enhanced sampling strategy that refines the sampling distribution to avoid previously unsuccessful actions. We leverage diffusion model decomposition to break down the primary problem into multiple smaller, more manageable sub-problems.
notes: High — composing diffusion policies with failure recovery; combines diffusion models and controller composition

title: World Action Models are Zero-shot Policies
authors: Seonghyeon Ye, Yunhao Ge, Kaiyuan Zheng, Shenyuan Gao, Sihyun Yu, George Kurian, Suneel Indupuru, You Liang Tan, Chuning Zhu, Jiannan Xiang, Ayaan Malik, Kyungmin Lee, William Liang, Nadun Ranawaka, Jiasheng Gu, Yinzhen Xu, Guanzhi Wang, Fengyuan Hu, Avnish Narayan, Johan Bjorck, Jing Wang, Gwanghyun Kim, Dantong Niu, Ruijie Zheng, Yuqi Xie, Jimmy Wu, Qi Wang, Ryan Julian, Danfei Xu, Yilun Du, Yevgen Chebotar, Scott Reed, Jan Kautz, Yuke Zhu, Linxi Fan, Joel Jang
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Diffusion models|Generalist policies
headline: DreamZero: 14B video diffusion world-action model achieves 2x generalization over VLAs at 7Hz
summary: DreamZero jointly models video and action via a pretrained video diffusion backbone, learning physical dynamics through future world state prediction. Achieves 2x improvement in generalization over VLAs in real experiments. Enables cross-embodiment transfer with 30 minutes of play data while retaining zero-shot generalization.
abstract: State-of-the-art VLA models excel at semantic generalization but struggle to generalize to unseen physical motions. We introduce DreamZero, a World Action Model built upon a pretrained video diffusion backbone. By jointly modeling video and action, DreamZero learns diverse skills from heterogeneous data, achieving over 2x improvement in generalization.
notes: High — world action models as policies; major advance in generalist robot policy paradigm

title: WorldEval: World Model as Real-World Robot Policies Evaluator
authors: Yaxuan Li, Yichen Zhu, Junjie Wen, Chaomin Shen, Yi Xu
headline: World models serve as scalable proxy evaluators for real-world robot policy safety
summary: Proposes using world models as scalable proxy evaluators for real-world robot policy performance and safety detection, reducing the need for expensive real-world evaluation. Relevant to verification as it enables systematic policy evaluation.
abstract: The field of robotics has made significant strides toward developing generalist robot manipulation policies. However, evaluating these policies in real-world scenarios remains time-consuming and challenging, particularly as the number of tasks scales and environmental conditions change. In this work, we demonstrate that world models can serve as a scalable, reproducible, and reliable proxy for real-world robot policy evaluation. A key challenge is generating accurate policy videos from world models that faithfully reflect the robot actions. We observe that directly inputting robot actions or using high-dimensional encoding methods often fails to generate action-following videos. To address this, we propose Policy2Vec, a simple yet effective approach to turn a video generation model into a world simulator that follows latent action to generate the robot video. We then introduce WorldEval, an automated pipeline designed to evaluate real-world robot policies entirely online. WorldEval effectively ranks various robot policies and individual checkpoints within a single policy, and functions as a safety detector to prevent dangerous actions by newly developed robot models.
notes: High — world model for policy evaluation and safety; relevant to verification paradigm

title: Unified World Models: Coupling Video and Action Diffusion for Pretraining on Large Robotic Datasets
authors: Chuning Zhu, Raymond Yu, Siyuan Feng, Benjamin Burchfiel, Paarth Shah, Abhishek Gupta
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Diffusion models|Imitation learning
headline: Unified transformer couples video and action diffusion for scalable robot pretraining from heterogeneous data
summary: UWM integrates action and video diffusion in one transformer with independent diffusion timesteps per modality. Can flexibly serve as policy, forward/inverse dynamics, or video generator. Enables leveraging action-free video data and produces more robust policies than pure imitation learning.
abstract: We present Unified World Models (UWM), a framework that allows for leveraging both video and action data for policy learning. A UWM integrates an action diffusion process and a video diffusion process within a unified transformer architecture.
notes: High — unified diffusion world model for robot learning; significant RSS 2025 paper

title: FORMULA: FORmation MPC with neUral barrier Learning for safety Assurance
authors: Qintong Xie, Weishu Zhan, Peter Chin
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Neural CBFs + MPC enable scalable safety-aware multi-robot formation control
summary: FORMULA integrates MPC with CLFs for stability and neural CBFs for decentralized safety in multi-robot formation control. Eliminates manual safety constraint design and enables formation-preserving navigation in complex environments.
abstract: This paper presents FORMULA, a safe distributed, learning-enhanced predictive control framework that integrates MPC with Control Lyapunov Functions for stability and neural network-based CBFs for decentralized safety.
notes: Moderate — neural CBFs for multi-robot formation; relevant to CBF methods but less connected to data-driven verification [residual -1: multi-robot formation focus, no data-driven verification component]

title: RISE: Self-Improving Robot Policy with Compositional World Model
authors: Jiazhi Yang, Kunyang Lin, Jinwei Li, Wencong Zhang, Tianwei Lin, Longyan Wu, Zhizhong Su, Hao Zhao, Ya-Qin Zhang, Li Chen, Ping Luo, Xiangyu Yue, Hongyang Li
headline: Compositional world model enables self-improving robot policy without additional demonstrations
summary: Uses a compositional world model for robot policy self-improvement through iterative refinement without requiring new demonstrations.
abstract: Despite the sustained scaling on model capacity and data acquisition, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models remain brittle in contact-rich and dynamic manipulation tasks, where minor execution deviations can compound into failures. While reinforcement learning (RL) offers a principled path to robustness, on-policy RL in the physical world is constrained by safety risk, hardware cost, and environment reset. To bridge this gap, we present RISE, a scalable framework of robotic reinforcement learning via imagination. At its core is a Compositional World Model that predicts multi-view future via a controllable dynamics model, and evaluates imagined outcomes with a progress value model. These components are integrated into a closed-loop self-improving pipeline that continuously generates imaginary rollouts, estimates advantages, and updates the policy without costly physical interaction. Across three real-world tasks, RISE yields more than +35% absolute performance increase.
notes: High — compositional world model for policy improvement; relevant to world models for robotics

title: LiLo-VLA: Compositional Long-Horizon Manipulation via Linked Object-Centric Policies
authors: Yue Yang, Shuo Cheng, Yu Fang, Homanga Bharadhwaj, Mingyu Ding, Gedas Bertasius, Daniel Szafir
headline: Modular VLA with object-centric composition outperforms Pi0.5 by 41% on long-horizon manipulation
summary: LiLo-VLA decouples transport from interaction: a Reaching Module handles global motion while an object-centric VLA handles manipulation. Achieves 69% on 21-task benchmark (outperforming Pi0.5 by 41%) and 85% on 8 real-world tasks through dynamic replanning and skill reuse.
abstract: We propose LiLo-VLA, a modular framework capable of zero-shot generalization to novel long-horizon tasks. Our approach decouples transport from interaction with dynamic replanning and skill reuse.
notes: High — modular VLA composition for long-horizon tasks; relevant to controller composition and VLAs

title: pi0.5: a Vision-Language-Action Model with Open-World Generalization
authors: Physical Intelligence, Kevin Black, Noah Brown, et al.
headline: Pi0.5 co-trains on heterogeneous tasks for first end-to-end VLA to clean kitchens in new homes
summary: Extends pi0 with co-training on heterogeneous tasks including multi-robot data, semantic prediction, and web data. Demonstrates for the first time that an end-to-end VLA can perform long-horizon dexterous manipulation like cleaning kitchens in entirely new homes.
abstract: We describe pi0.5, a new model based on pi0 that uses co-training on heterogeneous tasks to enable broad generalization. Our system uses a combination of co-training and hybrid multi-modal examples to enable broadly generalizable real-world robotic manipulation.
notes: High — landmark VLA with open-world generalization; major milestone in generalist policies

title: Fine-Tuning Vision-Language-Action Models: Optimizing Speed and Success
authors: Moo Jin Kim, Chelsea Finn, Percy Liang
headline: OpenVLA-OFT recipe: 97.1% LIBERO success with 26x throughput boost over baseline VLA
summary: Studies VLA fine-tuning design choices and proposes OFT recipe integrating parallel decoding, action chunking, continuous actions, and L1 objective. OpenVLA-OFT achieves 97.1% on LIBERO (from 76.5%) with 26x throughput boost, outperforming pi0 and RDT-1B in real-world experiments.
abstract: We study key VLA adaptation design choices and propose an Optimized Fine-Tuning recipe. OpenVLA-OFT sets a new state of the art on LIBERO, boosting success from 76.5% to 97.1% with 26x throughput.
notes: High — VLA fine-tuning best practices; important practical contribution to VLA deployment

title: Learned Perceptive Forward Dynamics Model for Safe and Platform-aware Robotic Navigation
authors: Pascal Roth, Jonas Frey, Cesar Cadena, Marco Hutter
headline: Learned forward dynamics model enables zero-shot safe MPPI navigation with 27% higher success
summary: Proposes a perceptive forward dynamics model predicting future states conditioned on geometry and proprioception. Integrated into MPPI planning with failure probability optimization. Achieves 41% better position estimation and 27% higher navigation success on ANYmal.
abstract: We propose a novel learned perceptive Forward Dynamics Model that predicts the robot future state conditioned on surrounding geometry and proprioception, integrated into zero-shot MPPI planning.
notes: High — learned dynamics for safe navigation; relevant to safe robot control

title: Safety Certification is Classification
authors: Oliver Schön, Licio Romao, Sadegh Soudjani
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: Novel kernel framework certifies safety without recursive DP compounding errors
summary: Proposes a kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data, directly estimating T-step safety probability without recursion. Subsumes barrier certificates and robust Markov models as special cases. Bypasses compounding error across horizons and enables certification for non-Markovian dynamics. Validated on a neural-controlled quadrotor.
abstract: The goal of this paper is certifying safety of dynamical systems subject to uncertainty. Existing approaches use trajectory data to estimate transition probabilities, and compute safety probabilities recursively via dynamic programming (DP). This recursion may lead to compounding errors in the certified safety probability, thus collapsing to a vacuous lower bound for growing horizons T. We propose a kernel embedding framework that treats safety certification as a classification problem on trajectory data, directly estimating the T-step safety probability without recursion. We show that the framework subsumes well-established approaches from the literature (e.g., barrier certificates, robust Markov models) as special cases, and allows us to go beyond their limitations. As the main consequence, it bypasses compounding error across the horizon and enables certification for systems with non-Markovian dynamics. We demonstrate that direct estimators remain stable independent of the certification horizon and in the non-Markovian setting, whilst DP-based certificates silently go unsound -- confirmed in simulation on a neural-controlled quadrotor.
notes: Core — data-driven safety certification via classification on trajectory data, directly applicable to neural-controlled systems [residual +1: directly addresses data-driven verification of neural controllers without requiring system model]

title: Set-Based Training of Neural Barrier Certificates for Safety Verification of Dynamical Systems
authors: Miriam Kranzlmüller, Lukas Koller, Tobias Ladner, Matthias Althoff
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: Integrates verification directly into neural barrier certificate training via set-based loss
summary: Proposes a set-based training approach that tightly integrates formal verification into neural barrier certificate training through a set-based loss function encoding all barrier certificate properties. Loss of zero formally proves the certificate's validity, collapsing iterative train-verify into one procedure. Scales well with system dimension and handles complex nonlinear dynamics.
abstract: Barrier certificates are scalar functions over the state space of dynamical systems that separate all unsafe states from all reachable states. The existence of a barrier certificate formally verifies the safety of the dynamical system. Recent approaches synthesize barrier certificates by iteratively training a neural network. In each iteration, the candidate is formally verified - if successful, the barrier certificate is found. Instead, we propose a set-based training approach that tightly integrates verification into training via a set-based loss function that soundly encodes all barrier certificate properties. A loss of zero formally proves the validity of the barrier certificate, collapsing the iterative training and verification into a single training procedure. Our experiments demonstrate that our set-based training approach scales well with the system dimension and naturally handles complex nonlinear dynamics.
notes: Core — set-based neural barrier certificate training fuses verification with learning

title: Feasibility-aware Hybrid Control for Motion Planning under Signal Temporal Logics
authors: Panagiotis Rousseas, Dimos V. Dimarogonas
matched_interests: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Hybrid control unifies STL-based planning with CBF-based safety in complex workspaces
summary: Proposes a hybrid modeling approach for planar task and motion planning that unifies planning with control design using a discrete variable for local constraint satisfaction. Designs CBFs on transformed disk versions of nonconvex robotic workspaces to address deadlock issues. Handles multiple overlapping spatio-temporal STL tasks under input saturation.
abstract: This paper proposes a novel method for planar task and motion planning based on hybrid modeling. By using a discrete variable which models local constraint satisfaction and enables local feasibility analysis, the proposed control architecture unifies planning with control design. Additionally, control barrier functions are designed on a transformed disk version of the original nonconvex and geometrically complex robotic workspace, helping to address deadlock issues. Simulations show that the method effectively handles multiple overlapping spatio-temporal tasks even under input saturation.
notes: High — combines STL planning with CBF safety; relevant to formal methods for robot control

title: Mask World Model: Predicting What Matters for Robust Robot Policy Learning
authors: Yunfan Lou, Xiaowei Chi, Xiaojie Zhang, Zezhong Qian, Chengxuan Li, Rongyu Zhang, Yaoxu Lyu, Guoyu Song, Chuyao Fu, Haoxuan Xu, Pengwei Wang, Shanghang Zhang
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Diffusion models|Generalist policies|Imitation learning
headline: Semantic mask prediction replaces pixel prediction for robust robot world models
summary: Introduces Mask World Model (MWM) that predicts semantic mask evolution instead of RGB pixels using video diffusion architectures. The geometric information bottleneck forces the model to capture physical dynamics while filtering visual noise. Significantly outperforms RGB-based world models on LIBERO and RLBench. Shows superior generalization and robustness in real-world experiments.
abstract: World models derived from large-scale video generative pre-training have emerged as a promising paradigm for generalist robot policy learning. However, standard approaches often focus on high-fidelity RGB video prediction, this can result in overfitting to irrelevant factors, such as dynamic backgrounds and illumination changes. These distractions reduce the model's ability to generalize, ultimately leading to unreliable and fragile control policies. To address this, we introduce the Mask World Model (MWM), which leverages video diffusion architectures to predict the evolution of semantic masks instead of pixels. This shift imposes a geometric information bottleneck, forcing the model to capture essential physical dynamics and contact relations while filtering out visual noise. We seamlessly integrate this mask dynamics backbone with a diffusion-based policy head to enable robust end-to-end control. Extensive evaluations demonstrate the superiority of MWM on the LIBERO and RLBench simulation benchmarks, significantly outperforming the state-of-the-art RGB-based world models. Furthermore, real-world experiments and robustness evaluation (via random token pruning) reveal that MWM exhibits superior generalization capabilities and robust resilience to texture information loss.
notes: High — novel world model architecture using mask prediction; combines diffusion models with world models for robust policy learning

title: MotuBrain: An Advanced World Action Model for Robot Control
authors: MotuBrain Team, Chendong Xiang, Fan Bao, Haitian Liu, Hengkai Tan, Hongzhe Bi, James Li, Jiabao Liu, Jingrui Pang, et al.
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Generalist policies|Imitation learning|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: Unified world-action model achieves 95% success across 50 manipulation tasks
summary: Presents a unified World Action Model combining video generation, action modeling, and language conditioning via a three-stream Mixture-of-Transformers. Supports five inference modes including VLA policy, world modeling, and inverse dynamics. Achieves 95% success on RoboTwin 2.0 and adapts to new humanoid embodiments with 50-100 trajectories.
abstract: MotuBrain presents a unified World Action Model (WAM) that jointly models video and action using a three-stream Mixture-of-Transformers architecture integrating video generation, action modeling, and language conditioning. The unified formulation supports inference over five distributions: VLA policy modeling, world modeling, video generation, inverse dynamics, and joint video-action prediction. MotuBrain introduces a unified multiview representation supporting arbitrary camera views, uses an independent text stream for tighter semantic-control coupling, and adopts a unified action representation across embodiments. On RoboTwin 2.0, MotuBrain achieves 95% average success rates and attains the strongest reported EWMScore on WorldArena.
notes: High — state-of-the-art unified world-action model; relevant to generalist policies and world model research

title: Uncertainty-aware Latent Safety Filters for Avoiding Out-of-Distribution Failures
authors: Junwon Seo, Kensuke Nakamura, Andrea Bajcsy
matched_interests: Latent-space safety analysis|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Conformal prediction|Data-driven verification|World models for robotics|Verification of visuomotor policies
headline: Conformal-calibrated latent safety filters detect OOD failures in world model-based control
summary: Introduces uncertainty-aware latent safety filters that use world model epistemic uncertainty to identify unseen hazards. Calibrates OOD detection thresholds via conformal prediction and performs HJ reachability analysis in an augmented latent+uncertainty state space. Reliably safeguards arbitrary policies from both known and novel safety hazards.
abstract: Recent advances in generative world models have enabled classical safe control methods such as Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability to generalize to complex robotic systems operating directly from high-dimensional sensor observations. However obtaining comprehensive coverage of all safety-critical scenarios during world model training is extremely challenging and as a result latent safety filters built on top of these models may miss novel hazards and even fail to prevent known ones overconfidently misclassifying risky out-of-distribution (OOD) situations as safe. To address this we introduce an uncertainty-aware latent safety filter that proactively steers robots away from both known and unseen failures. We use the world model epistemic uncertainty as a proxy for identifying unseen potential hazards. We propose a principled method to detect OOD world model predictions by calibrating an uncertainty threshold via conformal prediction and perform reachability analysis in an augmented state space spanning both the latent representation and the epistemic uncertainty to synthesize a latent safety filter that can reliably safeguard arbitrary policies from both known and unseen safety hazards.
notes: Core — combines latent-space HJ reachability + conformal prediction + world models for safety filtering [residual +1: directly addresses verification of black-box systems using latent representations with conformal guarantees]

title: Contact-Aware Safety in Soft Robots Using High-Order Control Barrier and Lyapunov Functions
authors: Kiwan Wong, Maximilian Stölzle, Wei Xiao, Cosimo Della Santina, Daniela Rus, Gioele Zardini
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: High-order CBFs enforce whole-body contact force limits on soft robots in real-time
summary: Introduces a HOCBF+HOCLF framework enforcing strict contact force limits across the entire soft robot body during environmental interactions. Combines a differentiable Piecewise Cosserat-Segment dynamics model with convex-polygon distance approximation for real-time whole-body safety. Validated on soft manipulators for human-robot interaction scenarios.
abstract: Robots operating alongside people particularly in sensitive scenarios such as aiding the elderly with daily tasks or collaborating with workers in manufacturing must guarantee safety and cultivate user trust. Continuum soft manipulators promise safety through material compliance but as designs evolve for greater precision payload capacity and speed and increasingly incorporate rigid elements their injury risk resurfaces. This paper introduces a comprehensive High-Order Control Barrier Function (HOCBF) and High-Order Control Lyapunov Function (HOCLF) framework that enforces strict contact force limits across the entire soft-robot body during environmental interactions. The approach combines a differentiable Piecewise Cosserat-Segment (PCS) dynamics model with a convex-polygon distance approximation metric (DCSAT) to enable real-time whole-body collision detection resolution and enforcement of safety constraints.
notes: High — novel CBF framework for soft robot safety; extends CBF methodology to continuum robots

title: Latent Representations for Control Design with Provable Stability and Safety Guarantees
authors: Paul Lutkus, Kaiyuan Wang, Lars Lindemann, Stephen Tu
matched_interests: Latent-space safety analysis|Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Formal guarantees transfer Lyapunov/barrier certificates from latent space to original system
summary: Initiates a formal study on using latent representations for verifiable control synthesis. Provides dynamics-aware approximate conjugacy conditions formalizing reconstruction error bounds. Transfers stability and invariance guarantees from latent certificate functions back to the original system. Enables Lyapunov/barrier function verification in low-dimensional spaces.
abstract: We initiate a formal study on the use of low-dimensional latent representations of dynamical systems for verifiable control synthesis. Our main goal is to enable the application of verification techniques such as Lyapunov or barrier functions that might otherwise be computationally prohibitive when applied directly to the full state representation. We provide dynamics-aware approximate conjugacy conditions which formalize the notion of reconstruction error necessary for systems analysis and then utilize our conjugacy conditions to transfer the stability and invariance guarantees of a latent certificate function for a latent space controller back to the original system.
notes: Core — formal framework for transferring safety guarantees from latent space to original system [residual +1: directly addresses provable safety guarantees for latent-space controllers foundational for data-driven verification of high-dimensional systems]

title: Safety Certification in the Latent Space using Control Barrier Functions and World Models
authors: Mehul Anand, Shishir Kolathaya
matched_interests: Latent-space safety analysis|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|World models for robotics|Verification of visuomotor policies
headline: Latent-space CBCs in world models enable safe visuomotor policy synthesis
summary: Introduces a semi-supervised framework using control barrier certificates learned in a world model's latent space to synthesize safe visuomotor policies. Integrates CBC learning with latent world model dynamics for safety-aware visual control. Demonstrates safety certification directly in learned latent representations.
abstract: This paper introduces a semi-supervised framework that leverages control barrier certificates (CBCs) learned in the latent space of a world model to synthesise safe visuomotor policies. The framework integrates Control Barrier Certificates with a latent world model through a semi-supervised approach to enable safe visuomotor control.
notes: Core — latent-space CBC synthesis via world models for visuomotor safety; directly relevant to verification of visuomotor policies

title: CPED-NCBFs: A Conformal Prediction for Expert Demonstration-based Neural Control Barrier Functions
authors: (from Stoch Lab / IISc)
headline: Conformal prediction verifies neural CBFs learned from expert demonstrations
summary: Proposes CPED-NCBFs a split-conformal prediction based verification strategy for neural control barrier functions learned from expert demonstrations. Overcomes conservative bounds from SMT solvers and MIP by providing probabilistic safety guarantees. Significantly outperforms existing approaches (FM-NCBF) with fewer samples. Validated on point mass and unicycle models.
abstract: Learning Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) from expert demonstrations has emerged as an effective strategy for enforcing safety in control systems. However a critical challenge remains: verifying that the learned CBFs truly enforce safety across the entire state space which is especially difficult when CBF is represented using neural networks (NCBFs). Existing verification techniques including SMT-based solvers mixed-integer programming and interval or bound-propagation methods often introduce loose conservative bounds. To overcome these limitations this work uses CPED-NCBFs a split-conformal prediction based verification strategy to verify the learned NCBF from expert demonstrations. Validated on benchmark dynamical systems including point mass and unicycle models.
notes: Core — conformal prediction for verifying neural CBFs from demonstrations [residual +1: directly combines conformal prediction with data-driven CBF verification core intersection of research interests]

title: SHIELD: Safety on Humanoids via CBFs In Expectation on Learned Dynamics
authors: Lizhi Yang, Blake Werner, Ryan K. Cosner, David Fridovich-Keil, Preston Culbertson, Aaron D. Ames
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Reinforcement learning
headline: Stochastic CBF safety layer protects learned humanoid locomotion controllers in real-world
summary: Proposes SHIELD a layered safety framework for humanoid robots combining a learned stochastic dynamics residual model with discrete-time CBF safety constraints in probability. Adds a safety filter on top of RL-trained locomotion controllers without retraining. Bridges the gap between RL-based controllers and formal CBF safety guarantees using real-world data.
abstract: Robot learning has produced remarkably effective controllers for complex tasks such as dynamic locomotion on humanoids yet ensuring dynamic safety remains challenging. SHIELD is a layered safety framework that bridges this gap by: (1) training a generative stochastic dynamics residual model using real-world data from hardware rollouts capturing system behavior and uncertainties; and (2) adding a safety layer on top of the nominal learned locomotion controller via a stochastic discrete-time CBF formulation enforcing safety constraints in probability.
notes: High — CBF safety layer for learned humanoid controllers; combines data-driven dynamics with formal safety

title: Data-driven Reachability Verification with Probabilistic Guarantees under Koopman Spectral Uncertainty
authors: (authors not specified)
headline: Probabilistic reachability guarantees via Koopman operator with spectral uncertainty bounds
summary: Addresses data-driven reachability verification using the Koopman operator with probabilistic guarantees accounting for spectral uncertainty in the learned operator. Lifts nonlinear dynamics into linear representations for tractable reachable set computation while quantifying uncertainty from finite data.
abstract: This paper addresses data-driven reachability verification with probabilistic guarantees under Koopman spectral uncertainty. The approach uses the Koopman operator to lift nonlinear dynamics into a linear representation and provides probabilistic guarantees on the reachable sets accounting for spectral uncertainty in the learned operator.
notes: High — data-driven reachability via Koopman with probabilistic guarantees; relevant to data-driven verification methods

title: Value Functions for Temporal Logic: Optimal Policies and Safety Filters
authors: Oswin So, William Sharpless, Sylvia Herbert, Chuchu Fan
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Safe & robust robot control|Data-driven verification
headline: Non-Markovian policies achieve TL value optimality and serve as Q-function safety filters.
summary: Constructs non-Markovian policies based on state history that fix a pathology where greedy Q-policies can defer task completion under reach-avoid (Until) specs even with optimal value functions. Builds on a recent decomposition of the TL value function into a graph of constituent value functions and proves optimality with respect to the quantitative robustness score for nested Until, Globally, and Globally-Until specifications. Also shows how the Q-function can serve as a safety filter for complex TL specifications, generalizing prior reach-avoid filters.
abstract: While Bellman equations for basic reach, avoid, and reach-avoid problems are well studied, the relationship between value optimality and policy optimality becomes subtle in the undiscounted infinite-horizon setting, particularly for more complicated tasks. Greedily maximizing the Q-function can produce policies that indefinitely defer task completion for reach-avoid problems, or equivalently, Until specifications, even when the value function is optimal. Building upon recent results decomposing the value function for temporal logic (TL) into a graph of constituent value functions, we construct non-Markovian policies based on state history that avoid this pathology and prove their optimality with respect to the quantitative robustness score for nested Until, Globally, and Globally-Until specifications. We further show how the Q function can serve as a safety filter for complex TL specifications, extending prior results beyond simple avoid or reach-avoid tasks.
notes: Alert: Fan new article, Lindemann related. Core — TL value functions + safety filters by Herbert/Fan, directly relevant to formal verification and HJ-style reach-avoid analysis

title: Neural Backward Reach-Avoid Tubes with MPC Supervision for High-Dimensional Systems: An Application to Safe Spacecraft Docking
authors: Santiago Thorup, Luca Castelletto, Zeyuan Feng, Somil Bansal
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Neural BRAT with MPC supervision scales HJ reach-avoid analysis to 13D spacecraft docking.
summary: Trains a neural approximation of the HJ value function using PDE-based losses augmented with curriculum-driven MPC supervision, providing informative value targets and stabilizing training where purely PDE-based methods fail. Online deployment uses two real-time controllers: a value-gradient controller and a value-augmented terminal MPC enforcing reachability at the horizon. Evaluated on a 6D planar docking against grid ground truth and scaled to a full 13D system, outperforming existing methods in success rate and computational efficiency.
abstract: Autonomous spacecraft docking requires control policies that simultaneously ensure collision avoidance and target reachability under coupled, high-dimensional translational-rotational dynamics. Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability provides formal reach-avoid guarantees, but classical solvers are limited to low-dimensional systems. Learning-based approaches have begun to scale HJ analysis, yet they struggle in reach-avoid settings, especially where goal and failure sets are tightly coupled, as in docking. We propose a learning-based Backward Reach-Avoid Tube (BRAT) framework that addresses this challenge by tightly integrating HJ structure with MPC-based supervision. In the offline phase, we train a neural approximation of the HJ value function using PDE-based losses augmented with curriculum-driven MPC supervision, which provides informative value targets and stabilizes training in regions where purely PDE-based methods fail. In the online phase, the learned value function is deployed through two real-time controllers: (i) a value gradient-driven controller, and (ii) a value-function-augmented terminal MPC that explicitly enforces reachability at the horizon. We evaluate the proposed method on a 6D planar docking problem against grid-based ground truth and then scale to the full 13D system. Across both settings, our approach outperforms existing methods in success rate and computational efficiency.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy/Bansal/Herbert related. Core — neural HJ reach-avoid scaled to 13D via MPC supervision, central to data-driven safety verification [residual +1: directly addresses scaling data-driven HJ verification to high-dim systems with learned value approximation]

title: Online Safety Filter for Deformable Object Manipulation with Horizon Agnostic Neural Operators
authors: Jiaxing Li, Hanjiang Hu, Zhuoyuan Wang, Yorie Nakahira, Changliu Liu
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Data-driven verification
headline: Boundary CBFs and neural operators give horizon-agnostic safety filters for deformable manipulation.
summary: Combines a horizon-agnostic neural operator that learns boundary input-output PDE dynamics (generalizing across rollout lengths without retraining) with a boundary control barrier function that certifies safety at the task-relevant output level via a lightweight QP. Constraint is affine in the boundary input rate, enabling real-time online filtering. On FluidLab fluid manipulation tasks, the filter improves safe trajectory rates by up to 22% over unfiltered base policies and reduces steps to reach the safe set.
abstract: Safety critical control of robotic manipulation tasks involving deformable media such as fluids, cloth, and soft objects remains challenging because existing learning based approaches encode safety indirectly through reward shaping, which provides no guarantee of constraint satisfaction at deployment. We present a constraint driven online safety filter for deformable object manipulation that enforces explicit task level safety constraints in real time by minimally modifying any nominal control policy. Our approach combines two key components: a horizon agnostic neural operator that learns the boundary input output mapping of the underlying PDE dynamics and generalizes across variable rollout lengths without retraining, and a boundary control barrier function that certifies safety at the task relevant output level via a lightweight quadratic program. The resulting safety constraint is affine in the boundary input rate, enabling real time online filtering. We evaluate the proposed method on fluid manipulation tasks in FluidLab, where the filter improves safe trajectory rates by up to 22% over unfiltered base policies while also reducing the number of steps required to reach the safe set, demonstrating that constraint driven safety enforcement is both more reliable and more efficient than reward shaping approaches.
notes: Alert: Herbert/Bansal related. High — neural-operator + boundary CBF for safe deformable manipulation, combines learned dynamics with formal safety filtering

title: TAIL-Safe: Task-Agnostic Safety Monitoring for Imitation Learning Policies
authors: Riad Ahmed, Momotaz Begum
matched_interests: Verification of visuomotor policies|Imitation learning|Safe & robust robot control|Adversarial robustness of robot policies|Runtime verification of learned policies|Flow matching
headline: Task-agnostic runtime safety monitor catches failures of flow-matching imitation policies online.
summary: Proposes a task-agnostic runtime safety monitor that wraps modern imitation learning algorithms (e.g., flow-matching policies) to detect and arbitrate against unsafe behavior at deployment. The framework is designed to be compatible across tasks and policy classes, providing a Simplex-style safety supervision layer for learned policies. Demonstrates monitoring effectiveness across imitation learning benchmarks where failure modes of learned policies can be caught online. Highlighted by Bajcsy citation alert.
abstract: Recent imitation learning (IL) algorithms such as flow-matching policies have made significant strides in learning complex robot manipulation behaviors from demonstrations, but providing safety guarantees for such black-box learned controllers at deployment remains an open challenge. TAIL-Safe introduces a task-agnostic runtime safety monitoring framework that operates around an arbitrary imitation policy and detects unsafe behavior online without requiring task-specific retraining. The framework formulates safety monitoring as a learned classifier over policy rollouts that flags potentially unsafe trajectories before execution and triggers a fallback controller in response. Empirical evaluation on imitation-learning manipulation benchmarks shows that TAIL-Safe substantially reduces unsafe outcomes for flow-matching and diffusion policies while maintaining task success on nominal trajectories, providing a Simplex-style safety supervision layer for modern black-box visuomotor policies.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy citation. Core — runtime safety monitoring for IL/flow-matching policies, exactly the verification-of-visuomotor-policies direction Sumanth tracks [residual +1: black-box safety monitor for learned visuomotor controllers — directly aligned with research focus]

title: Quality-Aware Exploration Budget Allocation for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
authors: Daniel Oh, Minkyo Yoon, H. Jin Kim
headline: Quality-aware exploration budget allocation improves cooperative multi-agent RL sample efficiency.
summary: Proposes a method for allocating exploration budget across agents in cooperative multi-agent RL based on per-agent estimated exploration quality. The approach concentrates exploratory actions on agents whose contributions are expected to most improve joint performance, improving sample efficiency over uniform exploration. Empirical results show gains on standard cooperative MARL benchmarks. Tangential to Sumanth’s core verification interests but tracked for the broader RL angle.
abstract: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) suffers from inefficient exploration, where naively spreading exploratory actions across all agents wastes interaction budget on agents whose updates contribute little to joint progress. We propose a quality-aware exploration budget allocation framework that estimates per-agent exploration quality and adaptively redistributes the global exploration budget toward agents whose actions are expected to most improve the team’s value. The framework integrates with standard cooperative MARL algorithms and is evaluated on common MARL benchmarks, where it consistently improves sample efficiency and final return relative to uniform exploration baselines.
notes: Alert: Tomlin citation. Low — multi-agent RL exploration, tangential to verification-focused interests but a citation-tracked direction

title: Distributed Control Lyapunov and Control Barrier Certificates for Reactive Formation
authors: D. Liu, M. Li, W. Fang, L. Zhu
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Distributed CLF/CBF certificates synthesize reactive multi-robot formation control with safety.
summary: Studies reactive formation control of multi-agent systems with distributed Lyapunov-barrier certificates that simultaneously guarantee formation convergence and inter-agent safety. The approach yields a distributed quadratic-program controller per agent and provides convergence and safety guarantees under mild communication assumptions. Demonstrates the framework on multi-robot formation tasks. Relevant to Sumanth as another instance of CBF-based safety in multi-agent settings.
abstract: In this article, we study reactive formation control of multi-agent systems and propose a distributed framework that combines control Lyapunov functions and control barrier functions to certify both formation convergence and inter-agent collision avoidance. Each agent solves a local quadratic program using only neighbor information, and the resulting controller is shown to be feasible and to enforce both the Lyapunov decrease and barrier non-negativity constraints under mild communication assumptions. We further analyze robustness of the certificates to bounded disturbances and provide simulation studies on multi-robot formation tasks demonstrating the practical advantages of the proposed approach over centralized baselines.
notes: Alert: Tomlin related. Moderate — distributed CLF/CBF for reactive multi-agent formation, aligned with safe robot control + CBF tracking

title: Lyapunov-based dropout deep neural network (Lb-DDNN) adaptive controller
authors: S. Akbari, E.J. Griffis, O.S. Patil, W.E. Dixon
headline: Lyapunov-stable dropout-DNN adaptive controller for nonlinear systems with stability proofs.
summary: Develops an adaptive controller that uses a deep neural network with dropout as the function approximator while preserving Lyapunov-style stability guarantees. Provides theoretical analysis showing convergence under dropout regularization and validates the design on nonlinear control benchmarks. The work strengthens the bridge between DNN-based control approximators and classical Lyapunov stability theory.
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) are well-established tools for function approximation in adaptive control, but their stochastic regularization techniques such as dropout complicate Lyapunov-based stability analysis. This paper develops a Lyapunov-based dropout deep neural network (Lb-DDNN) adaptive controller that incorporates dropout-trained DNN approximators while preserving formal stability guarantees. The proposed update laws are derived directly from a Lyapunov analysis that explicitly accounts for dropout-induced parameter variations, yielding ultimately bounded tracking errors for a class of nonlinear systems. Numerical studies illustrate the robustness benefits of dropout regularization in the closed-loop adaptive setting.
notes: Alert: Bansal/Tomlin citation. Low — DNN adaptive control with Lyapunov guarantees; theoretically interesting but mostly classical adaptive control [residual -1: pure adaptive control theory, no data-driven verification or robotics application focus]

title: Efficient Hierarchical Implicit Flow Q-learning for Offline Goal-conditioned Reinforcement Learning
authors: Zhiqiang Dong, Teng Pang, Rongjian Xu, Guoqiang Wu
headline: Goal-conditioned mean-flow policies replace Gaussians in hierarchical offline GCRL.
summary: Introduces a goal-conditioned mean-flow policy that adds an average velocity field to hierarchical policy modeling for offline goal-conditioned RL, capturing complex target distributions for both high-level and low-level policies and enabling efficient one-step action sampling. Adds a LeJEPA loss that repels goal-representation embeddings to encourage discriminative representations. Strong results on state-based and pixel-based OGBench tasks.
abstract: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) is a practical reinforcement learning paradigm that aims to learn goal-conditioned policies from reward-free offline data. Despite recent advances in hierarchical architectures such as HIQL, long-horizon control in offline GCRL remains challenging due to the limited expressiveness of Gaussian policies and the inability of high-level policies to generate effective subgoals. To address these limitations, we propose the goal-conditioned mean flow policy, which introduces an average velocity field into hierarchical policy modeling for offline GCRL. Specifically, the mean flow policy captures complex target distributions for both high-level and low-level policies through a learned average velocity field, enabling efficient action generation via one-step sampling. Furthermore, considering the insufficiency of goal representation, we introduce a LeJEPA loss that repels goal representation embeddings during training, thereby encouraging more discriminative representations and improving generalization.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy related. High — flow matching applied to hierarchical offline GCRL, directly relevant to flow/diffusion policy interest

title: Learning Human-Intention Priors from Large-Scale Human Demonstrations for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Y. Xie, YA Wang, G. Chen, J. Liu, Y. Sun, W. Ding
headline: Large-scale human-video pretraining yields transferable intention priors for robot manipulation.
summary: Pretrains an intention-prior representation on a large corpus of human manipulation videos and uses it to bootstrap policy learning for robotic manipulation. The intention prior captures task-level human intent that transfers across embodiments, accelerating downstream policy fine-tuning with limited robot data. Reports improved sample efficiency on standard manipulation benchmarks compared to baselines that train from robot data alone.
abstract: Human videos contain rich behavioral and semantic information about how humans interact with objects, but mapping this knowledge into robot policies remains difficult due to embodiment gaps. We propose to learn human-intention priors from a large-scale corpus of human manipulation demonstrations and use them as a transferable representation for downstream robotic manipulation. Our pipeline distills high-level intent (object-centric goals, contact events, action phases) from the human videos into a learned prior that conditions a robot policy during fine-tuning. Across simulated and real-world manipulation benchmarks, the resulting policies achieve substantially better sample efficiency and generalization than baselines trained only on robot demonstrations, illustrating the value of large-scale human data as an intent-level prior for embodied learning.
notes: Alert: Majumdar related. Moderate — human-video intention priors for manipulation, broadly relevant to imitation learning and generalist policy directions

title: Cooperative optimization of heterogeneous autonomous vehicles at intersections using graph neural networks and conformal prediction
authors: Y. Chen, J. Zhou, J. Zhang, J. Zhao, F. Yi, H. Liu, D. Lu
headline: GNN-based intersection planner uses conformal prediction for safe heterogeneous AV coordination.
summary: Combines graph neural networks for modeling heterogeneous vehicle interactions at intersections with conformal prediction to provide distribution-free uncertainty bounds on predicted trajectories. The cooperative planner uses these uncertainty intervals to compute risk-aware coordination decisions that respect inter-vehicle safety constraints. Validated on intersection coordination scenarios, showing improvements in safety metrics over deterministic GNN baselines.
abstract: Coordinating heterogeneous autonomous vehicles at unsignalized intersections is a challenging planning problem due to coupled multi-agent decision-making and the uncertainty of predicted intent. We propose a cooperative optimization framework that uses graph neural networks to model the interaction graph among vehicles at the intersection and conformal prediction to wrap the GNN-based intent predictor with distribution-free coverage guarantees on predicted future positions. The resulting risk-aware coordination problem is solved as a constrained optimization that respects safety margins implied by the conformal intervals. Simulation studies on diverse intersection scenarios show improved safety and throughput compared to deterministic GNN baselines and standard rule-based coordination strategies.
notes: Alert: Lindemann citation. Moderate — GNN + conformal prediction for cooperative driving, applies a Lindemann-favored method to multi-agent planning

title: Conformalized multi-step forecasting for high-rate dynamic systems
authors: Y.K. Chua, T. Li, S. Navidi, A. Razmarashooli, et al.
headline: Conformalized multi-step forecasting gives distribution-free intervals for high-rate dynamic systems.
summary: Extends conformal prediction to multi-step trajectory forecasting in high-rate dynamic systems where standard exchangeability assumptions break down. The method calibrates prediction intervals across forecasting horizons while maintaining valid coverage. Demonstrates the approach on engineering systems experiencing fast dynamic events, providing well-calibrated multi-step forecasts useful for monitoring.
abstract: Engineering systems that experience high-rate dynamic events require multi-step state and load forecasts with calibrated uncertainty. Standard split-conformal prediction provides finite-sample coverage guarantees for single-step prediction under exchangeability, but extending these guarantees to multi-step horizons in non-stationary, high-rate settings is non-trivial. We propose a conformalized multi-step forecasting framework that explicitly handles temporal dependence and forecast-horizon shift, producing per-step prediction intervals with empirically valid coverage on benchmark high-rate datasets. The method enables online uncertainty-aware monitoring of structural and dynamic systems and is shown to outperform naive multi-step conformal baselines in both interval width and coverage stability.
notes: Alert: Lindemann citation. Moderate — multi-step conformal prediction for high-rate dynamics, methodologically aligned with conformal-prediction-for-control work

title: Confidence-Gated Adapter Switching for Selective Specialization of Vision-Language-Action Policies
authors: N. Perera, S. Jayasinghe, C. Fernando, A. De Silva, et al.
headline: Confidence-gated adapters route VLA inference to task-specific experts only when needed.
summary: Augments a pretrained Vision-Language-Action policy with task-specific adapters that are switched in based on a confidence-gated routing signal. At inference, the gating mechanism selects the appropriate adapter only when the base policy is uncertain, preserving generalist behavior elsewhere. The approach reduces interference from specialization while preserving performance on diverse manipulation benchmarks, providing a lightweight specialization recipe for VLAs.
abstract: Specialization of pretrained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies to new tasks via adapter modules is appealing but risks degrading the generalist behavior of the underlying model. We propose Confidence-Gated Adapter Switching, a routing mechanism that activates task-specific adapters only when the base VLA is uncertain about the next action. Confidence is estimated from the action distribution of the base policy, and the gating threshold is calibrated per task. Across a suite of simulated and real manipulation benchmarks, this selective specialization preserves the generalist capabilities of the base VLA while delivering targeted improvements on specialized tasks, outperforming both always-on adapter baselines and naive task-conditioned routing.
notes: Alert: Tomlin related. Moderate — confidence-gated specialization for VLAs, default tier per VLA classification preference

title: PlayWorld: Learning Robot World Models from Autonomous Play
authors: Tenny Yin, Zhiting Mei, Zhonghe Zheng, Miyu Yamane, David Wang, Jade Sceats, Samuel M. Bateman, Lihan Zha, Apurva Badithela, Ola Shorinwa, Anirudha Majumdar
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Generalist policies|Reinforcement learning
headline: Autonomous play data scales high-fidelity action-conditioned world models for robot manipulation.
summary: Trains action-conditioned robot world models on Autonomous Play data, where a VLM proposes scene-grounded instructions and a generalist policy executes them with safety checks and automatic resets, yielding hours of unsupervised, contact-rich interaction data. PlayWorld produces physically consistent predictions for contact-rich tasks (up to 40% better than human-collected baselines) and enables in-world-model RL that improves real-world policy success by 65%.
abstract: Action-conditioned video models offer a promising substrate for robot policy evaluation and improvement, but training high-fidelity world models requires diverse contact-rich interaction data that human teleoperation alone cannot provide. We introduce PlayWorld, a scalable framework for training Action-Conditioned Robot World Models from Autonomous Play. Rather than relying on success-biased human demonstrations, PlayWorld continuously gathers diverse, contact-rich interactions and failure cases that better match downstream policy behaviors. A VLM proposes scene-grounded instructions, a generalist policy executes them, and safety checks plus automatic resets enable hours of unsupervised data collection with minimal human intervention, including overnight operation. Experiments across diverse manipulation tasks show that PlayWorld yields high-quality, physically consistent predictions for contact-rich interactions not captured by world models trained on human-collected data, with up to 40% improvements over human-collected baselines. PlayWorld also enables reinforcement learning in the world model, improving real-world policy success rates by 65%.
notes: Alert: Majumdar citation. High — Princeton (Majumdar lab) world model trained from autonomous play, matches world-models-for-robotics interest

title: Adaptive Finite-time Safe Tracking Control for Robotic Systems Based on High-order Finite-time Neural Control Barrier Functions
authors: H. Wang, J. Peng, W. He, H. Zhang, G. Li
venue: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: High-order finite-time neural CBFs deliver adaptive safe tracking for robot systems.
summary: Proposes adaptive finite-time safe tracking controllers using high-order finite-time neural control barrier functions for robotic systems. Combines neural approximators of barrier conditions with adaptive update laws to provide finite-time safety guarantees in the presence of model uncertainty. Validated on robot manipulation/tracking simulations.
abstract: This paper develops an adaptive finite-time safe tracking control framework for robotic systems based on high-order finite-time neural control barrier functions (HOFT-NCBFs). The proposed design integrates neural approximations of high-order CBF conditions with adaptive update laws so that safety constraints are enforced within a prescribed finite time, even in the presence of unknown system dynamics. The closed-loop system is shown to exhibit finite-time stability and finite-time forward invariance of the safe set under explicit conditions on the learning gains. Simulation studies on representative robotic tracking tasks demonstrate the practical advantages of the HOFT-NCBF design over conventional CBF and adaptive baselines.
notes: Alert: Tomlin related. Low — incremental CBF extension; useful CBF tracking but not central to data-driven verification [residual -1: tangential CBF theory variant, not central to the data-driven verification thread]

title: Efficient Continual Imitation Learning with Online Meta-Adapters
authors: R. Zhu, E. Sun, G. Huang, O. Celiktutan
headline: Online meta-adapters enable continual imitation learning across tasks without catastrophic forgetting.
summary: Proposes online meta-adapters for continual imitation learning that allow a robot to acquire new skills sequentially while preserving previously learned behavior. The meta-adapter formulation enables fast online adaptation with small parameter footprint and avoids catastrophic forgetting across task sequences. Empirical results on continual manipulation benchmarks show improved retention and faster acquisition than naive sequential fine-tuning.
abstract: Continual adaptation is essential for general-purpose robots that must acquire new behaviors over time without losing previously mastered skills. We propose Online Meta-Adapters, a lightweight continual imitation learning framework where each new task is encapsulated in a small adapter module trained with a meta-learning objective on a stream of demonstrations. The meta objective biases adapters toward fast online adaptation and parameter regions that minimize interference with prior tasks. Across continual manipulation benchmarks, our approach achieves higher final retention and faster acquisition of new tasks compared to naive sequential fine-tuning, replay-based, and standard adapter baselines, while keeping the per-task parameter overhead negligible.
notes: Alert: Majumdar related. Moderate — continual imitation learning with meta-adapters, aligned with imitation-learning interest tracking

title: Distributed Asymptotic Consensus Safety-Critical Control of Multi-Agent Systems with Vector Control Barrier Functions
authors: X. Huang, L. Long
venue: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Vector CBFs enable distributed safe consensus across multi-agent networks.
summary: Studies distributed safety-critical consensus control for multi-agent systems using vector control barrier functions that handle multiple coupled safety constraints simultaneously. Provides distributed update laws guaranteeing both asymptotic consensus and joint safety set invariance. Includes simulation studies on networked second-order multi-agent systems.
