abstract: This paper studies distributed safety-critical consensus control for networks of multi-agent systems where each agent must respect multiple coupled safety constraints. We extend the control barrier function framework to vector CBFs that compactly encode multiple safety constraints and develop distributed quadratic-program-based controllers that simultaneously achieve asymptotic consensus and forward invariance of the joint safe set. Sufficient conditions on the agent dynamics, communication graph, and barrier candidates are derived, and the framework is validated on numerical examples involving second-order multi-agent systems with multiple obstacle and inter-agent constraints.
notes: Alert: Herbert related. Low — vector CBFs for multi-agent consensus, classical multi-agent CBF extension [residual -1: pure CBF theory extension, no learning/data-driven component]

title: CauMon: A Tool for Online Monitoring against Signal Temporal Logic
authors: Z. Zhang, J. An, P. Arcaini, I. Hasuo
matched_interests: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Runtime verification of learned policies
headline: CauMon: an online STL monitor that exploits causality structure for fast trace checking.
summary: Presents CauMon, a tool for online monitoring of streaming traces against Signal Temporal Logic specifications that exploits causality structure to enable efficient incremental evaluation. The tool reports both Boolean satisfaction and quantitative robustness in real time and is positioned as a usable building block for runtime monitoring of cyber-physical and learned controllers.
abstract: CauMon is a tool for online monitoring against Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications. Building on a causality-aware semantics for STL, CauMon evaluates streaming traces incrementally and produces both Boolean verdicts and quantitative robustness scores in real time. The tool exposes an API and command-line interface compatible with common simulation and runtime environments, and is intended as an off-the-shelf monitor for cyber-physical systems and learned controllers. We describe the tool architecture, the online evaluation algorithm, and demonstrate its practical effectiveness on benchmarks ranging from automotive control traces to learned robot policy rollouts.
notes: Alert: Lindemann related. Moderate — STL online monitoring tool, useful for runtime verification of learned policies

title: PriPG-RL: Privileged Planner-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Partially Observable Systems with Anytime-Feasible MPC
authors: Mohsen Amiri, Ali Beikmohammadi, Sindri Magnusson, et al.
headline: Anytime-feasible MPC privileges teacher distills knowledge into a partial-observation RL student.
summary: Formalizes a POMDP framework where a planner agent with access to an approximate model and privileged state information guides an observation-only learning agent. Introduces an anytime-feasible MPC as the planner and Planner-to-Policy SAC (P2P-SAC) for the student, distilling planner knowledge to mitigate partial observability. Validated in NVIDIA Isaac Lab and deployed on a real Unitree Go2 quadruped navigating obstacle-rich environments.
abstract: We address training a reinforcement learning policy under partial observability by exploiting a privileged, anytime-feasible planner agent available exclusively during training. The problem is formalized as a POMDP in which a planner with access to an approximate dynamical model and privileged state information guides a learning agent that observes only a lossy projection of the true state. To realize this framework, we introduce an anytime-feasible Model Predictive Control algorithm that serves as the planner agent. For the learning agent, we propose Planner-to-Policy Soft Actor-Critic (P2P-SAC), a method that distills the planner agent’s privileged knowledge to mitigate partial observability and thereby improve both sample efficiency and final policy performance. The approach is validated in simulation using NVIDIA Isaac Lab and successfully deployed on a real-world Unitree Go2 quadruped navigating complex, obstacle-rich environments.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy related. Moderate — privileged-planner-guided RL with MPC for POMDPs, deployed on a real quadruped

title: How foundation models will revolutionize robot swarms
authors: V. Strobel, M. Dorigo, M. Fritz
headline: Perspective: foundation models will reshape how robot swarms are designed and deployed.
summary: A perspective in Science Robotics arguing that foundation models will fundamentally change how robot swarms are designed, programmed, and deployed. Surveys current limitations of swarm engineering, projects how language-conditioned and multi-modal foundation models could provide higher-level coordination primitives, and discusses open research challenges in scaling foundation models to many-robot systems.
abstract: Robot swarms are composed of many, typically simple, robots that accomplish complex tasks through local interactions. We argue in this perspective that foundation models will revolutionize how robot swarms are designed, programmed, and deployed. We survey the limitations of current swarm engineering pipelines, discuss how recent foundation models can provide higher-level coordination primitives, language-grounded mission specification, and shared world models for many-robot systems, and identify the most pressing research challenges in scaling foundation models to swarm robotics, including communication, sample efficiency, and verification.
notes: Alert: Fan citation. Low — Science Robotics perspective on foundation models for swarms, broad outlook piece

title: Thinking in Text and Images: Interleaved Vision-Language Reasoning Traces for Long-Horizon Robot Manipulation
authors: Jinkun Liu, Haohan Chi, Lingfeng Zhang, Yifan Xie, YuAn Wang, Long Chen, Hangjun Ye, Xiaoshuai Hao, Wenbo Ding
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|VLA reasoning and chain-of-thought|Generalist policies
headline: IVLR alternates textual subgoals and visual keyframes for long-horizon manipulation reasoning.
summary: Introduces Interleaved Vision-Language Reasoning (IVLR), a policy framework structured around an explicit intermediate representation that alternates textual subgoals with visual keyframes over the full task horizon. The interleaved trace acts as a chain-of-thought for long-horizon manipulation, conditioning low-level action prediction on both modalities. Reaches 95.5% average success on LIBERO (92.4% on LIBERO-Long) and 59.4% on SimplerEnv-WidowX.
abstract: Long-horizon robot manipulation requires policies that can reason at multiple levels of abstraction and recover when intermediate steps fail. We introduce Interleaved Vision-Language Reasoning (IVLR), a policy framework built around an explicit intermediate representation that alternates textual subgoals with visual keyframes over the full task horizon. The interleaved trace acts as a chain-of-thought for embodied long-horizon planning and conditions a low-level action policy on both modalities. IVLR achieves 95.5% average success on LIBERO, including 92.4% on the challenging LIBERO-Long subset, and reaches 59.4% overall success on SimplerEnv-WidowX, outperforming strong VLA baselines and ablations that use only one modality of the reasoning trace.
notes: Alert: Majumdar related. Moderate — interleaved text/image reasoning for long-horizon VLA manipulation, fits VLA reasoning + CoT interest

title: Divide and Conquer: Decoupled Representation Alignment for Multimodal World Models
authors: J. Xiao, D. Liang, X. Zhou, Y. Ye, T. Su, G. Yi, B. Xia, Q. Lyu, et al.
headline: M2-REPA aligns decoupled per-modality features with expert foundation models for multimodal world models.
summary: Introduces M2-REPA, the first representation-alignment method tailored for multi-modal video generation. Decouples modality-specific features from a diffusion model and aligns each with a corresponding expert foundation model via a multi-modal alignment loss (cosine similarity) and a modality-specific decoupling regularization (CKA similarity). Produces high-fidelity, temporally stable video sequences over long 150-frame horizons by exploiting complementary semantic cues across modalities.
abstract: Emerging multi-modal world models for embodied agents must produce videos that remain visually faithful and temporally consistent over long horizons across multiple modalities. We propose M2-REPA, the first representation-alignment method tailored for multi-modal video generation in world models. Our key insight is that foundation models trained on different modality spaces naturally capture distinct domain-specific priors and can be treated as complementary experts. M2-REPA first decouples modality-specific features from the diffusion model’s intermediate representations, then aligns each with its corresponding expert foundation model via two synergistic objectives: a multi-modal representation alignment loss using cosine similarity, and a modality-specific decoupling regularization that encourages complementarity across modalities via Centered Kernel Alignment. M2-REPA produces high-fidelity RGB frames that maintain stable scene coherence and temporal consistency throughout 150-frame horizons, illustrating that decoupled per-modality alignment is a strong recipe for long-horizon multi-modal world modeling.
notes: Alert: Majumdar citation. Moderate — multimodal world model with decoupled representation alignment, matches world-models-for-robotics interest

title: Predictive Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs for Semi-Static Scenes
authors: M. Saavedra-Ruiz, C. Gauthier, K. Gupta, S. Shahfar, et al.
headline: Predictive spatio-temporal scene graphs model object persistence in semi-static robot environments.
summary: Introduces a predictive scene-graph representation for semi-static scenes, where most objects are persistent but a subset move on slow timescales. The model maintains node-level predictions for object positions and existence over time, enabling robots to anticipate environment changes. Supports downstream planning and SLAM-style tasks in environments that drift between robot visits.
abstract: Robots that operate over long deployments encounter semi-static scenes where most objects are persistent but a subset shift, appear, or disappear over time. We propose Predictive Spatio-Temporal Scene Graphs, a structured representation that maintains node-level predictions for object positions and existence across visits, capturing both the spatial layout and the temporal persistence of objects. The model is trained on long-horizon robot deployment data and supports downstream tasks such as planning under uncertain world state and persistent SLAM. Experiments in simulated and real semi-static environments show that the predictive scene graph anticipates environment changes between visits more accurately than non-predictive scene-graph baselines and improves the success rate of long-horizon navigation tasks.
notes: Alert: Fan citation. Low — predictive scene graphs for semi-static robot scenes, world-modeling-adjacent but mostly perception/SLAM

title: Safety-Constrained Reinforcement Learning with Post-Training Reachability Verification for Robot Navigation
authors: Qisong He, Xinmiao Huang, Jinwei Hu, Zhuoyun Li, Yi Dong, Changshun Wu, Xiaowei Huang
matched_interests: Safe & robust robot control|Reinforcement learning|Data-driven verification
headline: CVaR-constrained RL with post-training reachability verification ensures safe robot navigation
summary: Proposes a framework combining CVaR-constrained policy optimization on TD3 with post-training neural network reachability verification for mobile robot navigation. Achieves 98.3% success rate and highest safety verification rate among compared methods across ten navigation scenarios.
abstract: Safe navigation for mobile robots demands policies that remain reliable under the high-consequence perception uncertainty of cluttered environments. Yet most existing safe reinforcement learning (RL) methods assess safety through average cumulative cost. Such metrics can mask dangerous tail-risk behaviors. To address this, we propose a framework that trains risk-sensitive policies through Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) constrained optimization on an off-policy TD3 backbone and evaluates their safety margins post-training through neural network reachability verification.
notes: High — combines safe RL with reachability verification post-training; relevant to data-driven verification pipeline

title: Reachability Barrier Networks: Learning Hamilton-Jacobi Solutions for Smooth and Flexible Control Barrier Functions
authors: Matthew Kim, William Sharpless, Hyun Joe Jeong, Sander Tonkens, Somil Bansal, Sylvia Herbert
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: PINNs generate smooth CBFs from HJ reachability, enabling flexible safety-critical control
summary: Uses physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) to generate smooth approximations of CBFs by computing Hamilton-Jacobi optimal control solutions. Avoids traditional dimensionality constraints and supports tuning of conservativeness post-training through a parameterized discount term.
abstract: Recent developments in autonomous driving and robotics underscore the necessity of safety-critical controllers. Control barrier functions (CBFs) are a popular method for appending safety guarantees to a general control framework, but they are notoriously difficult to generate beyond low dimensions. Existing methods often yield non-differentiable or inaccurate approximations that lack integrity, and thus fail to ensure safety. In this work, we use physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) to generate smooth approximations of CBFs by computing Hamilton-Jacobi optimal control solutions.
notes: High — bridges HJ reachability and CBF synthesis via PINNs; from Herbert group (key researchers in this space)

title: SafeFlow: Safe Robot Motion Planning with Flow Matching via Control Barrier Functions
authors: Xiaobing Dai, Zewen Yang, Dian Yu, Fangzhou Liu, Hamid Sadeghian, Sami Haddadin, Sandra Hirche
matched_interests: Flow matching|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Flow matching barrier functions ensure safe trajectory generation without retraining
summary: Proposes SafeFlow, integrating flow matching with safety guarantees via flow matching barrier functions (FMBF). Ensures planned trajectories remain in safe regions across the entire planning horizon. Enables training-free, real-time safety enforcement at test time on planar navigation and 7-DoF manipulation.
abstract: Recent advances in generative modeling have led to promising results in robot motion planning, particularly through diffusion and flow matching (FM)-based models that capture complex, multimodal trajectory distributions. However, these methods are typically trained offline and remain limited when faced with new environments with constraints, often lacking explicit mechanisms to ensure safety during deployment. In this work, safe flow matching (SafeFlow), a motion planning framework, is proposed that integrates flow matching with safety guarantees.
notes: Core — directly combines flow matching policies with CBF safety guarantees; central to verification of generative policies

title: SafeFlowMatcher: Safe and Fast Planning using Flow Matching with Control Barrier Functions
authors: Jeongyong Yang, Seunghwan Jang, SooJean Han
matched_interests: Flow matching|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Sampling-based motion planning
headline: Two-phase prediction-correction integrator couples flow matching with CBFs for certified safe planning
summary: Presents SafeFlowMatcher, coupling flow matching with CBFs through a prediction-correction integrator. Prediction phase integrates learned FM, correction phase refines with vanishing time-scaled vector field and CBF-based QP. Achieves faster, smoother, safer paths on maze navigation and locomotion benchmarks.
abstract: Generative planners based on flow matching (FM) produce high-quality paths in a single or a few ODE steps, but their sampling dynamics offer no formal safety guarantees and can yield incomplete paths near constraints. We present SafeFlowMatcher, a planning framework that couples FM with control barrier functions (CBFs) to achieve both real-time efficiency and certified safety.
notes: High — complementary to SafeFlow; adds CBF-based correction to flow matching planning

title: Conformal Signal Temporal Logic for Robust Reinforcement Learning Control: A Case Study
authors: Hani Beirami, M M Manjurul Islam
matched_interests: Conformal prediction|Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Conformal STL monitoring with runtime shield ensures safe RL-based F-16 flight control
summary: Proposes a safe RL framework for F-16 flight control integrating conformal STL monitoring with a runtime shield. PPO policy trained in AeroBench simulator; short-horizon predictor calibrated via robust CP forecasts critical states. Shield overrides RL action when worst-case STL robustness becomes non-positive.
abstract: We investigate how formal temporal logic specifications can enhance the safety and robustness of reinforcement learning (RL) control in aerospace applications. Using the open source AeroBench F-16 simulation benchmark, we train a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agent to regulate engine throttle and track commanded airspeed. The control objective is encoded as a Signal Temporal Logic (STL) requirement to maintain airspeed within a prescribed band during the final seconds of each maneuver.
notes: High — novel combination of conformal prediction + STL for runtime RL safety monitoring

title: Scalable Data-Driven Reachability Analysis and Control via Koopman Operators with Conformal Coverage Guarantees
authors: Devesh Nath, Haoran Yin, Glen Chou
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Conformal prediction|Koopman operator methods|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Koopman + conformal prediction enables scalable data-driven reachability on high-dimensional MuJoCo tasks
summary: Uses Koopman theory with NN lifting to learn approximate linear dynamics and designs controllers in lifted space. Computes closed-loop reachable sets efficiently, mapped back via NN verification. Conformal prediction provides statistically-valid error bounds on 11D Hopper, 28D Swimmer, 12D quadcopter.
abstract: We propose a scalable reachability-based framework for probabilistic, data-driven safety verification of unknown nonlinear dynamics. We use Koopman theory with a neural network (NN) lifting function to learn an approximate linear representation of the dynamics and design linear controllers in this space to enable closed-loop tracking of a reference trajectory distribution.
notes: Core — data-driven verification + conformal prediction + Koopman; directly addresses black-box systems [residual +1: directly combines data-driven verification with conformal guarantees on high-dimensional robotic systems]

title: Scaling Verification Can Be More Effective than Scaling Policy Learning for Vision-Language-Action Alignment
authors: Jacky Kwok, Xilun Zhang, Mengdi Xu, Yuejiang Liu, Azalia Mirhoseini, Chelsea Finn, Marco Pavone
matched_interests: Verification of visuomotor policies|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Runtime verification of learned policies
headline: Test-time verification scaling outperforms policy scaling for VLA instruction-action alignment
summary: Investigates test-time verification to close the VLA intention-action gap. Characterizes test-time scaling laws for embodied instruction following. Precomputes diverse rephrased instructions, generates action candidates, and uses a verifier to select optimal prompt and action chunks. From Finn/Pavone groups.
abstract: The long-standing vision of general-purpose robots hinges on their ability to understand and act upon natural language instructions. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have made remarkable progress toward this goal, yet their generated actions can still misalign with the given instructions. In this paper, we investigate test-time verification as a means to shrink the intention-action gap.
notes: Core — directly about verification of VLA policies at test time; from Finn/Pavone groups [residual +1: directly addresses verification of black-box VLA policies, core research direction]

title: Do What You Say: Steering Vision-Language-Action Models via Runtime Reasoning-Action Alignment Verification
authors: Yilin Wu, Anqi Li, Tucker Hermans, Fabio Ramos, Andrea Bajcsy, Claudia Pérez-D'Arpino
matched_interests: Verification of visuomotor policies|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Runtime verification of learned policies
headline: Training-free runtime verification steers reasoning VLAs toward faithful instruction execution
summary: Formalizes embodied CoT faithfulness for reasoning VLAs and introduces a training-free runtime policy steering method. Samples multiple candidate action sequences, predicts outcomes via simulation, and uses VLM to select sequence whose outcome best aligns with the VLA's own textual plan.
abstract: Reasoning Vision Language Action (VLA) models improve robotic instruction-following by generating step-by-step textual plans before low-level actions, an approach inspired by Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in language models. Yet even with a correct textual plan, the generated actions can still miss the intended outcomes in the plan, especially in out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios.
notes: Core — runtime verification of VLA reasoning-action alignment; from Bajcsy group (relevant researcher)

title: SafeVLA: Towards Safety Alignment of Vision-Language-Action Model via Constrained Learning
authors: Borong Zhang, Yuhao Zhang, Jiaming Ji, Yingshan Lei, Yishuai Cai, Josef Dai, Yuanpei Chen, Yaodong Yang
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Safe & robust robot control|Adversarial robustness of robot policies
headline: Integrated safety approach constrains VLA policies via safe RL and targeted evaluation
summary: Explores an integrated safety approach (ISA) for VLAs: systematically models safety requirements, elicits diverse unsafe behaviors, constrains VLA policies via safe RL using CMDP paradigm, and assures safety through targeted evaluations. First work to explicitly integrate safety constraints into VLA training.
abstract: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) show potential as generalist robot policies. However, these models pose extreme safety challenges during real-world deployment, including the risk of harm to the environment, the robot itself, and humans.
notes: High — safety alignment for VLAs via constrained learning; relevant to VLA verification

title: dWorldEval: Scalable Robotic Policy Evaluation via Discrete Diffusion World Model
authors: Yaxuan Li, Zhongyi Zhou, Yefei Chen, Yaokai Xue, Yichen Zhu
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Diffusion models|Verification of visuomotor policies
headline: Discrete diffusion world model enables scalable proxy evaluation of thousands of robot policies
summary: Proposes dWorldEval using a discrete diffusion world model as scalable evaluation proxy. Maps vision, language, and robotic actions into unified token space via single transformer-based denoising network. Outperforms WorldEval, Ctrl-World, and WorldGym on LIBERO, RoboTwin, and real-robot environments.
abstract: Evaluating robotics policies across thousands of environments and thousands of tasks is infeasible with existing approaches. This motivates the need for a new methodology for scalable robotics policy evaluation.
notes: High — world model for policy evaluation could enable scalable verification of robot policies

title: A Closed-Form Dual-Barrier CBF Safety Filter for Holonomic Robots on Incrementally Built Occupancy Grid Maps
authors: Himanshu Paudel, Basanta Joshi, Dhirendra Raj Madai, Alina Bartaula, Biman Rimal, Sanjay Neupane
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Dual-barrier CBF filter handles unknown regions and obstacles in incrementally built maps
summary: Presents a dual-barrier CBF safety filter for real-time velocity control on incrementally built occupancy grid maps. Enforces two constraints: avoiding mapped obstacles and preventing entry into unknown regions. Uses signed distance field for analytical CBF derivation.
abstract: We present a dual-barrier control barrier function (CBF) safety filter for real-time, safety-critical velocity control of holonomic robots operating in incrementally built occupancy grid maps.
notes: Moderate — CBF extension for occupancy grids; no data-driven or learning component [residual -1: pure CBF engineering, no data-driven/learning element]

title: CoLA-Flow Policy: Temporally Coherent Imitation Learning via Continuous Latent Action Flow Matching for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Wu Songwei, Jiang Zhiduo, Sun Wandong, Xie Guanghu, Zhao Rui, Liu Hong, Liu Yang
headline: Latent-space flow matching achieves near-single-step inference with stable long-horizon manipulation
summary: Proposes CoLA-Flow Policy performing flow matching in a coherent latent action space. Maps action sequences into temporally coherent latent trajectory manifold and learns explicit latent-space flow. Enables near-single-step inference while improving execution smoothness and stability.
abstract: Learning long-horizon robotic manipulation requires jointly achieving expressive behavior modeling, real-time inference, and stable execution, which remains challenging for existing generative policies.
notes: High — flow matching in latent space for imitation learning; relevant to understanding policies that need verification

title: Atomic-Probe Governance for Skill Updates in Compositional Robot Policies
authors: Xue Qin, Simin Luan, John See, Zeyd Boukhers, Cong Yang, Zhijun Li
headline: Cross-version swap protocol reveals how skill updates affect compositional robot policy outcomes
summary: Introduces a paired-sampling cross-version swap protocol to characterize how composition outcomes change when skills are updated. Studies BLADE, SymSkill, and Generative Skill Chaining. Proposes a diagnostic framework for skill library maintenance in deployed robotic systems.
abstract: Skill libraries in deployed robotic systems are continually updated through fine-tuning, fresh demonstrations, or domain adaptation, yet existing typed-composition methods treat the library as frozen at test time and do not analyze how composition outcomes change when a skill is replaced.
notes: High — directly about compositional policy updates and their effects; relevant to controller composition verification

title: ORN-CBF: Learning Observation-conditioned Residual Neural Control Barrier Functions via Hypernetworks
authors: Bojan Derajić, Sebastian Bernhard, Wolfgang Hönig
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Hypernetwork-based observation-conditioned neural CBFs recover maximal safe sets from HJ reachability
summary: Proposes observation-conditioned neural CBFs based on HJ reachability analysis. Uses a hypernetwork architecture to parametrize the main CBF network conditioned on partial observations. Approximately recovers maximal safe sets while handling partial observability.
abstract: Control barrier functions (CBFs) have been demonstrated as an effective method for safety-critical control of autonomous systems. Although CBFs are simple to deploy, their design remains challenging, motivating the development of learning-based approaches.
notes: High — neural CBFs from HJ reachability with partial observability; relevant to verification of learned safety certificates

title: Neural Network-assisted Interval Reachability for Systems with Control Barrier Function-Based Safe Controllers
authors: Damola Ajeyemi, Saber Jafarpour, Emiliano Dall'Anese
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: NN-assisted interval reachability reveals spurious equilibria and unbounded behaviors in CBF controllers
summary: Addresses reachability analysis of systems with CBF-based controllers, showing CBFs can create undesirable behaviors like spurious equilibria and unbounded trajectories. Uses neural network-assisted interval reachability to efficiently compute reachable sets, revealing performance-safety tradeoffs.
abstract: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) have been widely utilized in the design of optimization-based controllers and filters for dynamical systems to ensure forward invariance of a given set of safe states.
notes: Core — reachability analysis of CBF-controlled systems; directly relevant to data-driven verification of safe controllers

title: Reinforcement Learning for Flow-Matching Policies
authors: Samuel Pfrommer, Yixiao Huang, Somayeh Sojoudi
headline: RL fine-tuning enables flow-matching robot policies to surpass demonstration performance
summary: Explores training flow-matching policies via reinforcement learning to exceed original demonstration quality. Notes minimum-time control as key application. Presents approaches for RL optimization of flow-matching generalist robotics policies conditioned on sensor observations and textual instructions.
abstract: Flow-matching policies have emerged as a powerful paradigm for generalist robotics. These models are trained to imitate an action chunk, conditioned on sensor observations and textual instructions.
notes: High — RL for flow matching policies; understanding these policies is important for verification

title: Refining Almost-Safe Value Functions on the Fly
authors: Sander Tonkens, Sosuke Kojima, Chenhao Liu, Judy Masri, Sylvia Herbert
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Data-driven verification
headline: Online refinement bridges offline HJ synthesis and real-time CBF adaptation for dynamic safety
summary: Introduces refineCBF for online refinement of approximate CBFs. Bridges offline HJ reachability synthesis with online adaptation. Addresses the gap where offline synthesis scales poorly and limits applicability in dynamic environments. From Herbert group.
abstract: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) are a powerful tool for ensuring robotic safety, but designing or learning valid CBFs for complex systems is a significant challenge. While Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability provides a formal method for synthesizing safe value functions, it scales poorly and is typically performed offline, limiting its applicability in dynamic environments.
notes: Core — online refinement of HJ-based CBFs; from Herbert group; directly relevant to data-driven verification in dynamic settings

title: End-to-End Humanoid Robot Safe and Comfortable Locomotion Policy
authors: Zifan Wang, Xun Yang, Jianzhuang Zhao, Jiaming Zhou, Teli Ma, Ziyao Gao, Arash Ajoudani, Junwei Liang
matched_interests: Safe & robust robot control|Reinforcement learning|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: LiDAR-to-action humanoid locomotion uses CBF-based CMDP for provable safety in 3D environments
summary: Presents end-to-end locomotion policy mapping raw LiDAR point clouds to motor commands. Formulates as CMDP with CBF-based costs. Uses Penalized PPO. Demonstrates safe, socially-aware navigation in unstructured human-centric environments.
abstract: The deployment of humanoid robots in unstructured, human-centric environments requires navigation capabilities that extend beyond simple locomotion to include robust perception, provable safety, and socially aware behavior.
notes: Moderate — humanoid locomotion with CBFs; interesting but tangential to core verification work [residual -1: locomotion application, no data-driven verification component]

title: Formation-Aware Adaptive Conformalized Perception for Safe Leader-Follower Multi-Robot Systems
authors: Richie R. Suganda, Bin Hu
headline: Formation-conditioned conformal prediction ensures perception safety in multi-robot leader-follower systems
summary: Proposes distributed, formation-aware adaptive conformal prediction using Risk-Aware Mondrian CP for leader-follower multi-robot formations. Produces formation-conditioned uncertainty quantiles that tighten in high-confidence configurations and expand when formation is degraded.
abstract: This paper considers the perception safety problem in distributed vision-based leader-follower formations, where each robot uses onboard perception to estimate relative states, track desired setpoints, and keep the leader within its camera field of view (FOV).
notes: High — conformal prediction for multi-robot safety; novel application of CP to formation control

title: Verifiable Model-Free Safety Filters via Reinforcement Learning
authors: Bihui Yin, Yiwen Lu, Yuchen Jiang, Yilin Mo
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Reinforcement learning
headline: RL-trained safety filter with formal certificate, no system model required.
summary: Yin et al. propose a model-free safety filter that mimics model-based Predictive Safety Filters (PSFs) by representing the filter as an unrolled QP solver network, but learns the QP parameters through Deep Reinforcement Learning rather than from system identification. The QP structure preserves a formal certificate for persistent safety of the learned filter. Numerical experiments show the method beats both conventional PSFs and MLP-based RL baselines on safety guarantees, intervention minimality, and per-step compute.
abstract: This paper presents a reinforcement learning approach of a model-free safety filter, drawing inspiration from the framework of model-based Predictive Safety Filters (PSFs). Similar to conventional PSFs, our method adopts a Quadratic Programming (QP) formulation by representing the filter as an unrolled QP solver network. However, unlike existing PSFs that derive QP parameters explicitly from system models, we learn these parameters directly through Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL), thereby eliminating the dependency on accurate system identification. Furthermore, compared to traditional neural network-based methods, this QP structure allows us to furnish a formal certificate for the persistent safety of the learned filter. Numerical results demonstrate that our method outperforms both conventional model-based PSFs and RL-trained Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) baselines in terms of safety guarantees, minimal intervention, and per-step computational load.
notes: Alert: Tomlin/Herbert/Bajcsy. Core - model-free safety filter trained via RL with formal certificate; directly relevant to data-driven verification of learned controllers

title: Escaping the Diversity Trap in Robotic Manipulation via Anchor-Centric Adaptation
authors: Yanzhe Chen, Kevin Yuchen Ma, Qi Lv, Yiqi Lin, Zechen Bai, Chen Gao, Mike Zheng Shou
headline: Anchor-centric demonstrations beat maximum-coverage diversity when fine-tuning VLAs on a budget.
summary: Chen et al. identify a diversity trap in VLA fine-tuning: collecting many diverse single-shot demonstrations is self-defeating due to non-vanishing estimation noise, formalized as a coverage-density trade-off. They propose Anchor-Centric Adaptation (ACA), which first stabilizes a policy skeleton via repeated demos at core anchors, then expands coverage to high-risk boundaries via teacher-forced error mining and constrained residual updates. Real-robot experiments show ACA substantially improves task reliability over standard diverse-sampling under the same data budget.
abstract: While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer broad general capabilities, deploying them on specific hardware requires real-world adaptation to bridge the embodiment gap. Since robot demonstrations are costly, this adaptation must often occur under a strict data budget. In this work, we identify a critical diversity trap: the standard heuristic of "maximizing coverage" by collecting diverse, single-shot demonstrations can be self-defeating due to non-vanishing estimation noise. We formalize this phenomenon as a Coverage--Density Trade-off. By decomposing the policy error into estimation (density) and extrapolation (coverage) terms, we characterize an interior optimal allocation of unique conditions for a fixed budget. Guided by this analysis, we propose Anchor-Centric Adaptation (ACA), a two-stage framework that first stabilizes a policy skeleton through repeated demonstrations at core anchors, then selectively expands coverage to high-risk boundaries via teacher-forced error mining and constrained residual updates. Real-robot experiments validate our trade-off framework and demonstrate that ACA significantly improves task reliability and success rates over standard diverse sampling strategies under the same budget.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High - VLA data-collection strategy connecting active learning to imitation learning under budget constraints

title: Learned Lyapunov Shielding for Adaptive Control
authors: Giansalvo Cirrincione, Adriano Fagiolini
headline: Closed-form Lyapunov safety filter shields a residual SAC policy on Euler-Lagrange robots.
summary: Cirrincione and Fagiolini augment the Slotine-Li adaptive controller with three learned components: a Cholesky-parameterized Lyapunov function, a residual SAC policy adding bounded torque corrections, and a physics-informed network for unmodeled dynamics. A closed-form safety filter projects every policy output onto the safe set without an online QP. They prove global feasibility, exponential stability, KKT convergence, and a PAC generalization bound, with experiments showing 41% tracking-error reduction on a 2-DOF manipulator and clean scaling to a 7-DOF Franka.
abstract: We augment the Slotine--Li adaptive controller for Euler--Lagrange systems with three learned components: a structured-quadratic Lyapunov function V_psi whose positive-definiteness follows from a Cholesky parameterization, a residual Soft Actor--Critic policy that adds bounded torque corrections to the analytic baseline, and a physics-informed neural network that estimates unmodeled dynamics. A closed-form safety filter, derived from the single affine constraint, projects every policy output onto the safe set without requiring an online QP solver. We prove: global feasibility of the filter under a drift-decay condition on the control-degeneracy set; exponential stability under exact shielding, with a robust extension whose margin depends on the PINN approximation error; almost-sure convergence of the three-timescale policy--certificate--multiplier updates to a KKT point; and a PAC generalization bound for the certificate over compacts. On a 2-DOF manipulator with nonlinear friction and variable payload, the learned certificate accounts for most of the empirical gain: tracking error drops by 41% on nominal friction and 24% on aggressive friction at the centroid of the training distribution. A 7-DOF scalability study on a Franka Emika Panda confirms clean convergence of the full pipeline at industrial scale, identifies the conditions under which gains over exact model-based baselines should and should not be expected, and documents a warm-start pathology of the learned certificate that has practical implications for deployment.
notes: Alert: Chuchu Fan citation. High - closed-form Lyapunov shielding combining learned certificates with residual RL, relevant to safe robot control

title: Learning When to Stop: Selective Imitation Learning Under Arbitrary Dynamics Shift
authors: Surbhi Goel, Jonathan Pei, James Wang
matched_interests: Imitation learning|Active learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Selective imitation lets a BC learner stop when dynamics shift makes its demos unreliable.
summary: Goel et al. introduce a selective imitation framework where the learner may choose to stop acting when test-time dynamics differ from training. Their algorithm SeqRejectron builds a stopping rule from a small set of validator policies whose size is horizon- and class-independent. They achieve horizon-free O-tilde(log |Pi|/eps^2) sample complexity for deterministic policies, with analogous guarantees for stochastic policies via a cumulative Hellinger stopping time, and extend gracefully to misspecified or shifted expert policies.
abstract: Behavior cloning provides strong imitation learning guarantees when training and test environments share the same dynamics. However, in many deployment settings the test environment's transitions differ from training, and classical offline IL offers no recourse: the learner must commit to an action at every state, even when its demonstrations are uninformative and could lead to arbitrary degradation of performance. This motivates the study of selective imitation, where the learner may choose to stop when it cannot act reliably. We introduce a model for selective imitation under arbitrary dynamics shift: given labeled expert demonstrations from a training environment and unlabeled state trajectories from the same expert in a test environment, the learner outputs a selective policy that is complete (rarely stops in training) and sound (incurs low regret before stopping in test). Our algorithm, SeqRejectron, constructs a stopping rule using a small set of validator policies whose size is independent of the horizon or policy class. For deterministic policies, this yields horizon-free O-tilde(log|Pi|/eps^2) sample complexity, assuming sparse costs. For stochastic policies, we obtain analogous horizon-free guarantees using a cumulative Hellinger stopping time. We extend the framework to misspecified experts and different expert policies across train and test and obtain results that gracefully degrade with the amount of misspecification.
notes: Alert: Bansal/Herbert. High - selective imitation provides runtime stopping rule for learned policies under dynamics shift; connects IL with safety-aware abstention

title: Risk-Controlled Post-Processing of Decision Policies
authors: Sunay Joshi, Tao Wang, Hamed Hassani, Edgar Dobriban
headline: Threshold-based post-processing wraps a baseline policy with a fallback under a risk budget.
summary: Joshi et al. study risk-controlled post-processing: given a deterministic baseline policy, choose a new policy that maximizes agreement with the baseline subject to a chance constraint on a user-specified loss. The optimal policy has a threshold structure following the baseline except where switching to a fallback reduces conditional violation risk substantially. A calibration-based algorithm achieves O(log n/n) expected excess risk and is evaluated on a COVID radiograph, LLM routing, and synthetic multiclass tasks.
abstract: Predictive models are often deployed through existing decision policies that stakeholders are reluctant to change unless a risk constraint requires intervention. We study risk-controlled post-processing: given a deterministic baseline policy, choose a new policy that maximizes agreement with the baseline subject to a chance constraint on a user-specified loss. At the population level, we show that the optimal policy has a threshold structure: it follows the baseline except on contexts where switching to the oracle fallback policy yields a large reduction in conditional violation risk. At the finite-sample level, given a fitted fallback policy and score, we develop a post-processing algorithm that uses calibration data to select a threshold. Leveraging tools from algorithmic stability and stochastic processes, we show that under regularity conditions, in the i.i.d. setting, the expected excess risk of the post-processed policy is O(log n/n). In the special case when an exact-safe fallback policy is available, the algorithm achieves precise expected risk control under exchangeability. In this setting, we also give high-probability near-optimality guarantees on the post-processed policy. Experiments on a COVID-19 radiograph diagnosis task, an LLM routing problem, and a synthetic multiclass decision task show that targeted post-processing can meet or nearly meet risk budgets while preserving substantially more agreement with the baseline than score-blind random mixing.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy citation. Low - distribution-free risk control framework for decision policies; methods overlap with conformal prediction but not applied to robotics [residual -1: medical/LLM applications, no robotics or learned-controller component]

title: Safety by Invariance, Liveness through Refinement: Heterogeneous Contract Framework for Co-Design of Layered Control
authors: Yoshinari Takayama, Alessio Iovine, Bart Besselink, Guillaume Sandou, Adnane Saoud
headline: Layered control architecture decouples discrete-time liveness from continuous-time safety via contracts.
summary: Takayama et al. propose a heterogeneous assume-guarantee contract framework for layered control architectures (LCAs): safety is enforced by invariance at the continuous-time layer while liveness is achieved through refinement at the discrete-time layer, with inter-layer coordination via vertical refinement and timing-compatibility conditions. They instantiate the contract with an MPC planner, an ISS low-level controller, and a reference-governor bridge, validated on a hybrid energy storage system.
abstract: Real-world control systems must achieve long-horizon objectives (liveness) while respecting continuous-time safety constraints, a combination that motivates hierarchical layered control architectures (LCAs). Existing LCA research, however, lacks (i) a uniform specification language across discrete planning and continuous execution, (ii) formal guarantees that specifications are preserved when interconnecting subsystems at heterogeneous time scales, and (iii) compositional separation between layers, owing to reliance on naive input-filtering laws. This paper addresses all three gaps by importing the safety--liveness decomposition into a heterogeneous assume--guarantee framework: safety is enforced by invariance at the continuous-time layer, while liveness is achieved through refinement at the discrete-time layer, with inter-layer coordination formalized via vertical refinement and timing-compatibility conditions. We instantiate this contract with a novel LCA combining an MPC planner, an input-to-state stabilizing (ISS) low-level controller, and a reference-governor bridge, and validate it on a Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) comprising a battery and a supercapacitor.
notes: Alert: Lindemann citation. High - formal contract framework for layered/composed controllers connecting controller composition and safety guarantees

title: Unifying Goal-Conditioned RL and Unsupervised Skill Learning via Control-Maximization
authors: Alireza Modirshanechi, Benjamin Eysenbach, Peter Dayan, Eric Schulz
headline: Goal-conditioned RL and skill learning unified as instances of control-maximization.
summary: Modirshanechi et al. unify goal-conditioned RL (GCRL) and mutual information skill learning (MISL) as instances of control maximization. They identify three canonical GCRL formulations that are fundamentally inequivalent, then show all share a sensitivity interpretation: a good policy's future trajectory is sensitive to the commanded goal. They prove MISL objectives are bounded by formulation-specific downstream goal sensitivities, giving a precise theoretical correspondence between pretraining objectives and downstream GCRL tasks.
abstract: Unsupervised pretraining has driven empirical advances in goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL), but its theoretical foundations remain poorly understood. In particular, an influential class of methods, mutual information skill learning (MISL), discovers behaviorally diverse skills that can later be used for downstream goal-reaching. However, it remains a theoretical mystery why skills learned through MISL should support goal-reaching. A subtle challenge is that both GCRL and MISL are umbrella terms: different GCRL tasks use distinct criteria for measuring goal-reaching performance, while different MISL methods optimize distinct notions of behavioral diversity. We address this challenge and unify GCRL and MISL as instances of control maximization. We identify three canonical GCRL formulations and prove that they are fundamentally inequivalent: they can induce incompatible optimal policies even in the same environment. Nevertheless, they all share a common interpretation: a well-performing goal-conditioned policy is one whose future trajectory is highly sensitive to the commanded goal, with the precise notion of sensitivity determined by the GCRL formulation. Noting that MISL objectives can be understood as measures of skill-sensitivity akin to goal-sensitivity, we show that MISL objectives are bounded by formulation-specific downstream goal-sensitivities. These bounds establish a precise correspondence between MISL methods and downstream GCRL tasks: for every GCRL formulation, there exists a matching MISL objective for which more diverse skills afford greater downstream goal sensitivity. Our results thus lay a theoretical foundation for RL pretraining and have important practical implications, such as suggesting which pretraining objectives to use when a user cares about a specific class of downstream tasks.
notes: Alert: Bansal. High - theoretical unification of GCRL and unsupervised skill learning, relevant to choosing pretraining objectives for downstream robot tasks

title: On Surprising Effects of Risk-Aware Domain Randomization for Contact-Rich Sampling-based Predictive Control
authors: Sergio A. Esteban, Junheng Li, Vince Kurtz, Aaron D. Ames
headline: Risk-aware domain randomization reshapes the cost landscape in contact-rich predictive sampling.
summary: Esteban et al. (Ames lab) study risk-aware domain randomization in sampling-based predictive control on a Push-T contact task, comparing average, optimistic, and pessimistic rollout aggregations under randomized model instances. They observe that DR not only improves robustness to model error but also reshapes the effective cost landscape seen by the sampling-based optimizer, expanding the basin of attraction around contact-producing actions.
abstract: Domain randomization (DR) is widely used in policy learning to improve robustness to modeling error, but remains underexplored in contact-rich sampling-based predictive control (SPC), where rollout quality is highly sensitive to uncertainty. In this work, we take the first step by studying risk-aware DR in predictive sampling on a simple yet representative Push-T task, comparing average, optimistic, and pessimistic rollout aggregations under randomized model instances. Our initial results suggest that DR affects not only robustness to model error, but also the effective cost landscape seen by the sampling-based optimizer, by reshaping the basin of attraction around contact-producing actions. This opens up potential for exploring better grounded risk-aware contact-rich SPC under model uncertainty. Video: https://youtu.be/f1F0ALXxhSM
notes: Alert: Chuchu Fan citation. High - risk-aware sampling-based MPC for contact-rich manipulation; surprising effect on cost landscape relevant to predictive control safety

title: Time-To-Reach Separation and Safety Filtering for Safe, Fair, and Efficient Multi-Agent Coordination
authors: M Low, JJ Aloor, VM Tuck, P Nuzzo, JJ Choi
headline: Time-to-reach metric unifies priority, separation, and safety for UAV traffic management
summary: Proposes a multi-agent coordination framework that uses minimum time-to-reach (TTR) as a unifying metric for priority assignment, temporal separation, and safety filtering in Advanced Air Mobility. The approach handles multiple aerial vehicles merging in urban airspace, ensuring collision-free operations in congested environments. Combines reachability-style certificates with practical traffic management.
abstract: Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) operations are expected to significantly increase aerial traffic in urban airspace, requiring autonomous traffic management systems to ensure collision-free operations in highly congested environments. In this paper, we propose a multi-agent coordination framework that uses minimum time-to-reach (TTR) as a unifying metric for priority assignment, temporal separation, and safety filtering. We focus on the problem of coordinating multiple aerial vehicles merging into a common airspace, where each agent must respect minimum separation requirements while maintaining throughput and fairness.
notes: Alert: Fan/Tomlin (+1). High — Safe & robust robot control + Hamilton-Jacobi reachability

title: Safety-Critical Multi-Agent Flocking via Motion-Aware Control Barrier Functions
authors: YH Su, F Arvin, J Hu
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Motion-aware CBFs scale heterogeneous multi-agent flocking via local QPs
summary: Presents a safe and scalable control framework for heterogeneous multi-agent flocking using motion-aware control barrier functions. Inter-agent separation and collision avoidance are enforced through pairwise safety constraints in local quadratic programs. Explicitly handles agent heterogeneity in physical radii and actuation limits to reduce conservatism.
abstract: This paper presents a safe and scalable control framework for heterogeneous multi-agent flocking using motion-aware control barrier functions. A nominal flocking controller is designed for cohesion and velocity alignment, while inter-agent separation and collision avoidance are enforced through pairwise safety constraints in local quadratic programs (QPs). Agent heterogeneity is explicitly considered, allowing for different physical radii and actuation limits.
notes: Alert: Fan. Moderate — Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) + Safe & robust robot control

title: Safe Fuzzy-CBF: A Monitoring Approach for Deep Reinforcement Learning Navigation Agents
authors: M Yang, G Liu, Z Zhou, G Liu
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Fuzzy-rule CBFs learned from data monitor DRL navigation agent risk
summary: Introduces Safe Fuzzy Control Barrier Functions, a monitoring framework that continuously evaluates DRL agent risk using a smooth control barrier function learned from data via Takagi-Sugeno fuzzy rules. Avoids the need for a predefined dynamics model, enabling runtime safety monitoring of learned navigation policies.
abstract: In safety-critical reinforcement learning tasks such as robot navigation, autonomous driving, and industrial control, real-time monitoring of agent behavior is paramount to prevent unsafe actions from compromising system reliability. We introduce Safe Fuzzy Control Barrier Functions (Safe Fuzzy-CBF), a monitoring framework that continuously evaluates agent risk using a smooth control barrier function learned from data via Takagi-Sugeno fuzzy rules, rather than relying on a predefined model.
notes: Alert: Fan/Herbert. Moderate — Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) + Reinforcement learning

title: SENTIL: A Runtime Verification Tool for Probabilistic Temporal Logic
authors: PK Quansah, E Bonnah
headline: SENTIL: runtime verification tool with statistical guarantees for probabilistic temporal logic
summary: Presents SENTIL, a runtime verification tool for probabilistic temporal logic on stochastic cyber-physical systems. Provides provable statistical guarantees for monitoring requirements that capture probabilistic dynamics, filling a gap between theoretical PTL foundations and practical tooling.
abstract: Stochastic cyber-physical systems (CPS) permeate critical infrastructure, from autonomous vehicles to medical devices. Yet, tools for runtime verification of such systems capturing the probabilistic dynamics in stochastic systems remain generally absent despite theoretical foundations established nearly a decade ago. In this paper, we present SENTIL, a novel runtime verification tool with provable statistical guarantees for the runtime monitoring of requirements expressed as Probabilistic Signal Temporal Logic.
notes: Alert: Fan/Lindemann. High — Signal Temporal Logic (STL) + Data-driven verification

title: Factored Diffusion Policies: Compositionally Generalized Robot Control with a Single Score Network
authors: S Mitra, E Yuceel, N Giles, A Pai
headline: Single diffusion network compositionally generalizes via per-factor null-token dropout
summary: Proposes Factored Diffusion Policies: a single shared diffusion network trained with per-factor null-token dropout, whose score decomposes additively across factors at inference. Under approximate conditional independence between factors given state, this enables compositional generalization to unseen combinations of factor values without requiring demonstrations for every combination.
abstract: Robotic tasks are typically specified by a tuple of factors, such as the object to be grasped, the obstacles to be avoided, the color of the target, and so on. Collecting expert demonstrations for every combination of factor values grows combinatorially. We present factored diffusion policies: a single shared diffusion network trained with per-factor null-token dropout, whose score decomposes additively across factors at inference. Under approximate conditional independence between factors given the state, the policy generalizes compositionally to unseen factor combinations.
notes: Alert: Fan. Core — Diffusion models + Imitation learning [residual +1: Core intersection — diffusion + compositional generalization + controller composition]

title: Actors Guided by Implicit Critic Stackelberg for Convergent Neural Synthesis of Robot Safety
authors: JS Wang, H Hu, DP Nguyen, JF Fisac
matched_interests: Safe & robust robot control|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Reinforcement learning
headline: MAGICS: Stackelberg-style adversarial training for convergent neural safety synthesis
summary: Introduces MAGICS (Minimax Actors Guided by Implicit Critic Stackelberg), a novel adversarial RL method for neural synthesis of robot safety that provides convergence guarantees. Addresses the lack of interpretability and convergence in existing neural safety synthesis methods. Aimed at scaling robust optimal control to high-dimensional problems.
abstract: While robust optimal control theory provides a rigorous framework to compute robot control policies that are provably safe, it struggles to scale to high-dimensional problems, leading to increased use of deep learning for tractable synthesis of robot safety. Unfortunately, existing neural safety synthesis methods often lack convergence guarantees and solution interpretability. In this paper, we present Minimax Actors Guided by Implicit Critic Stackelberg (MAGICS), a novel adversarial RL framework that achieves provable local convergence to a Stackelberg equilibrium and produces interpretable neural safety controllers.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy/Lindemann (+2). High — Safe & robust robot control + Hamilton-Jacobi reachability

title: Branch-Stochastic Model Predictive Control for Motion Planning under Multi-Modal Uncertainty with Scenario Clustering
authors: Z Xing, R Chaudhari, M Leibold, D Wollherr, M Buss
headline: Branch-stochastic MPC handles multi-modal uncertainty with scenario clustering
summary: Proposes a Branch-Stochastic MPC framework for motion planning under multi-modal intention and trajectory uncertainty in autonomous driving. Uses scenario clustering to reduce conservatism that arises when chance constraints must hold across all intentions simultaneously. Targets a middle ground between fully robust and per-trajectory stochastic MPC.
abstract: Motion planning for autonomous driving must account for multi-modal uncertainty in both the intentions and trajectories of surrounding vehicles. Handling uncertainty in a worst-case manner guarantees robustness but often leads to excessive conservatism. Stochastic Model Predictive Control (SMPC) reduces trajectory-level conservatism through chance constraints, yet remains conservative with respect to intention uncertainty since constraints must hold across all intentions. We present a branch-stochastic MPC framework that uses scenario clustering to balance robustness with efficiency.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy. Moderate — Safe & robust robot control

title: Proximal State Nudging: Reducing Skill Atrophy from AI Assistance
authors: M Srivastava, J Ouyang, E Zhou, A Silva, E Sumner
headline: Shared-autonomy algorithm nudges users toward learnable states to prevent skill atrophy
summary: Proposes Proximal State Nudging (PSN), a shared autonomy algorithm that jointly optimizes for human skill development and task performance. Addresses safety risks in semi-autonomous systems where operators may lose ability to distinguish their own inputs from autonomous corrections. Nudges users toward states estimated to be most learnable.
abstract: Skill atrophy, the gradual decline of human capability under AI assistance, poses a safety risk in shared-control of semi-autonomous systems, where operators may be unable to distinguish their own inputs from autonomous corrections. We propose Proximal State Nudging (PSN), a shared autonomy algorithm that jointly optimizes for skill development and task performance by nudging users toward states estimated to be most learnable.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy. Low — Safe & robust robot control [residual -1: Shared autonomy/skill atrophy — adjacent to but not in core interest set]

title: Implicit Safety Alignment from Crowd Preferences
authors: Q Lin, DS Brown
headline: Learn shared safety criteria from crowd preferences and transfer to RL tasks
summary: Extracts implicit safety criteria from RLHF crowd preference datasets to discover shared safety principles across users. Transfers these criteria to downstream RL tasks as regularizers. Aims to capture safety considerations that go beyond simple task completion in preference-based learning.
abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) can reveal implicit objectives such as safety considerations that go beyond task completion. In this work, we focus on the common safety criteria embedded in crowd preference datasets, where different users may express distinct preferences or objectives, yet follow similar safety principles. Our aim is to discover shared safety criteria from crowd preferences and then transfer them to downstream RL tasks to regularize agent behavior.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy. Low — Reinforcement learning + Safe & robust robot control [residual -1: RLHF for shared safety principles — adjacent to robot policy safety but more alignment-focused]

title: Reinforcement Learning for Risk Adaptation via Differentiable CVaR Barrier Functions
authors: X Wang, T Kim, B Hoxha, G Fainekos, D Panagou
matched_interests: Reinforcement learning|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: End-to-end RL with differentiable CVaR-CBF safety layer for crowd navigation
summary: Proposes an end-to-end risk adaptation framework combining RL with a differentiable quadratic-program safety layer based on CVaR control barrier functions. Targets crowd navigation under obstacle-motion uncertainty modeled by Gaussian mixture models. Adapts conservatism via the risk parameter learned end-to-end.
abstract: Planning through crowded environments under uncertain obstacle motions remains difficult, as stochastic interactions often induce overly conservative behavior or reduced efficiency. To address this challenge, we propose an end-to-end risk adaptation framework for crowd navigation under obstacle-motion uncertainty modeled by a Gaussian mixture model. The framework combines reinforcement learning (RL) with a differentiable quadratic-program safety layer based on CVaR control barrier functions.
notes: Alert: Lindemann/Tomlin (+2). High — Reinforcement learning + Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)

title: Disturbance Rejection Control under Nested Signal Temporal Logic Specifications: A Recursive Design Approach
authors: Y Peng, J Yan, W Wang
matched_interests: Signal Temporal Logic (STL)|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Recursive CBF design encodes nested STL formulas under disturbances
summary: Investigates control synthesis for continuous-time uncertain systems under nested STL specifications. Uses CBFs to encode STL formulas as system constraints. Addresses the gap that traditional CBF designs fail to encode nested STL formulas while reachability-based methods are inapplicable to uncertain systems.
abstract: This paper investigates the control synthesis for continuous-time uncertain systems under nested Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications containing nested temporal operators. Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) are utilized herein to encode STL formulas into system constraints. However, traditional CBF designs fail to encode nested STL formulas, whereas recent reachability analysis-based methods capable of handling such formulas are inapplicable to uncertain systems and suffer from a computational burden.
notes: Alert: Lindemann. Moderate — Signal Temporal Logic (STL) + Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)

title: Retrieve-then-Steer: Online Success Memory for Test-Time Adaptation of Generative VLAs
authors: J Zhao, H Yang, H Yusong, Y Gao, Q Ou, C Wan
headline: Online retrieval of past successes steers generative VLAs at test time
summary: Proposes Retrieve-then-Steer, a test-time adaptation method for generative VLAs that maintains an online memory of successful rollouts and retrieves them to steer the policy at inference. Aims to address distribution shift and rare failure modes without retraining the foundation policy.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer broad generalization but remain brittle under deployment-time distribution shifts. We introduce Retrieve-then-Steer, an online test-time adaptation approach that maintains a memory of successful rollouts and retrieves nearby successes to steer the generative VLA's action distribution. By conditioning on retrieved successes, the method adapts to new scenes and rare modes without weight updates.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy. Moderate — Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) + Generalist policies

title: GaussianDream: A Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian World Model for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Z Zhang, Y Jiang, Q Cheng, S Liu, D Zhao, P Luo
headline: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian world model augments VLA policy training
summary: Introduces GaussianDream, a feed-forward 3D Gaussian world model that augments standard VLA training with stronger semantic priors. Targets the limitation that action-imitation training alone provides limited spatial grounding when transferring from pretrained vision-language models to action generation.
abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies have advanced language-conditioned robotic manipulation by transferring semantic priors from pretrained vision-language models to action generation. Yet, standard action-imitation training often provides limited spatial grounding. We introduce GaussianDream, a feed-forward 3D Gaussian world model that augments VLA training with explicit 3D scene representations to improve generalization.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — World models for robotics + Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)

title: Action Agent: Agentic Video Generation Meets Flow-Constrained Diffusion
authors: J Sam, N Khang, Y Mahmoud, MA Cabrera
headline: LLM-orchestrated video generation pipes into flow-constrained diffusion controller
summary: Proposes Action Agent, a two-stage framework unifying agentic navigation video generation with flow-constrained diffusion control for multi-embodiment robot navigation. An LLM acts as orchestrator in Stage I; a flow-constrained diffusion policy executes in Stage II.
abstract: We present Action Agent, a two-stage framework that unifies agentic navigation video generation with flow-constrained diffusion control for multi-embodiment robot navigation. In Stage I, a large language model (LLM) acts as an orchestration agent that decomposes language goals into structured video plans. In Stage II, a flow-constrained diffusion policy translates these video plans into robot actions across multiple embodiments.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Moderate — Diffusion models + Flow matching

title: VLA-REPLICA: A Low-Cost, Reproducible Benchmark for Real-World Evaluation of Vision-Language-Action Models
authors: AS Huang, J Zhang, S Tang, Y Xiang
headline: Low-cost reproducible real-world benchmark for evaluating VLA models
summary: Proposes VLA-REPLICA, a reproducible benchmark for real-world evaluation of vision-language-action models. Addresses the gap that existing real-world VLA evaluations are inaccessible or inconsistent, while simulation benchmarks fail to capture deployment-relevant phenomena.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong promise for general-purpose robotic manipulation, but their real-world evaluation remains limited by a lack of accessible, reproducible, and consistent benchmarks. Simulation benchmarks fail to capture deployment-relevant phenomena. We present VLA-REPLICA, a low-cost benchmark designed for reproducible real-world VLA evaluation, with standardized tasks, hardware specifications, and protocols.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) + Verification of visuomotor policies

title: STARRY: Spatial-Temporal Action-Centric World Modeling for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Y Tian, Y Jin, B Yu, Y Shi, H Wu, CH Liu, K Chen
headline: STARRY models action-relevant spatial-temporal interactions for manipulation policies
summary: Proposes STARRY, a spatial-temporal action-centric world model addressing the limitation that existing VLA policies and world-model-enhanced policies do not fully model action-relevant interactions. Targets long-horizon manipulation tasks requiring reasoning about future spatial-temporal dynamics.
abstract: Robotic manipulation critically requires reasoning about future spatial-temporal interactions, yet existing VLA policies and world-model-enhanced policies do not fully model action-relevant spatial-temporal interaction structure. We propose STARRY, a spatial-temporal action-centric world model that explicitly captures interactions between actions and scene dynamics for improved long-horizon manipulation.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Moderate — World models for robotics + Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)

title: Beyond Action Residuals: Real-World Robot Policy Steering via Bottleneck Latent Reinforcement Learning
authors: D Yu, K Lei, Z Jiang, J Pan, H Xu
headline: Bottleneck latent RL steers pretrained imitation policies more cleanly than action residuals
summary: Proposes Bottleneck Latent RL for fine-tuning pretrained imitation policies. Argues that existing lightweight methods applying residual corrections in action space yield noisy, poorly structured updates, and instead steers policies through a bottleneck latent space. Targets online improvement of pretrained robot policies.
abstract: Pretrained imitation policies have become a strong foundation for robot manipulation, but they often require online improvement to overcome execution errors, limited dataset coverage, and deployment mismatch. A central question is therefore how reinforcement learning (RL) should adapt policies after offline pretraining. Existing lightweight methods commonly apply residual corrections directly in action space, but this often leads to noisy and poorly structured updates. We propose Bottleneck Latent RL, which steers the policy through a learned latent bottleneck.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Moderate — Reinforcement learning + Imitation learning

title: X-Imitator: Spatial-Aware Imitation Learning via Bidirectional Action-Pose Interaction
authors: K Xiong, H Fang, L Yang, C Lu
headline: X-Imitator: bidirectional action-pose interaction for spatial-aware imitation
summary: Addresses the bottleneck between spatial perception and action generation in robotic manipulation, which existing methods treat as decoupled or unidirectional. Proposes X-Imitator, a bidirectional action-pose interaction framework for spatial-aware imitation learning.
abstract: Effectively handling the interplay between spatial perception and action generation remains a critical bottleneck in robotic manipulation. Existing methods typically treat spatial perception and action execution as decoupled or strictly unidirectional. We present X-Imitator, a bidirectional action-pose interaction framework for spatial-aware imitation learning, enabling perception and action to mutually inform one another during inference.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Moderate — Imitation learning

title: Learning while Deploying: Fleet-Scale Reinforcement Learning for Generalist Robot Policies
authors: Y Wang, X Li, P Xie, P Yang, B Nie, Y Cai, Q Zhang
headline: Fleet-scale RL closes the loop between deployment and policy improvement
summary: Proposes fleet-scale RL to continuously improve generalist robot policies as they encounter distribution shifts, long-tail failures, task variations, and human corrections in deployment. Argues that offline data alone is insufficient for robust real-world deployment.
abstract: Generalist robot policies increasingly benefit from large-scale pretraining, but offline data alone is insufficient for robust real-world deployment. Deployed robots encounter distribution shifts, long-tail failures, task variations, and human corrections that pretraining cannot anticipate. We propose Learning while Deploying, a fleet-scale RL framework that aggregates deployment-time signals across many robots to continually improve a shared generalist policy.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — Reinforcement learning + Generalist policies

title: Zero-Shot Sim-to-Real Robot Learning: A Dexterous Manipulation Study on Reactive Catching
authors: K Ren, G Wang, AS Morgan, K Hang
headline: Domain-randomized RL achieves zero-shot sim-to-real reactive catching
summary: Studies sim-to-real transfer for dexterous manipulation on reactive catching tasks. Combines domain randomization with policy structuring choices to overcome modeling errors and perception noise.
abstract: Dexterous manipulation is physics-intensive and highly sensitive to modeling errors and perception noise, making sim-to-real transfer prohibitively challenging. Domain randomization (DR) is commonly used to improve the robustness of learned policies, but DR alone often produces overly conservative behaviors. We present a study on zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for reactive catching with a dexterous hand, identifying which design choices matter for closing the sim-to-real gap.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Low — Reinforcement learning [residual -1: Sim-to-real specific case study, no verification/safety component]

title: 3D Generation for Embodied AI and Robotic Simulation: A Survey
authors: T Ye, Y Mao, M Liao, J Liu, C Guo, D Du, Q Shou, F Zhu
headline: Survey of 3D generative modeling for embodied AI and robotic simulation
summary: A comprehensive survey of 3D generative modeling for embodied AI and robotic simulation. Reviews methods for generating scalable, diverse, physically grounded 3D content used in simulation-based training and real-world deployment.
abstract: Embodied AI and robotic systems increasingly depend on scalable, diverse, and physically grounded 3D content for simulation-based training and real-world deployment. While 3D generative modeling has advanced rapidly, embodied applications impose unique requirements around physical realism, asset diversity, and task-relevance. We survey recent work on 3D generation for embodied AI and robotic simulation, organizing methods by representation and discussing open challenges.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Low — World models for robotics [residual -1: Survey on 3D content generation, tangential to world models for control]

title: Online Conformal Prediction with Corrupted Feedback
authors: B Wang, M Zecchin, O Simeone
headline: Online conformal prediction with provable guarantees under corrupted miscoverage feedback
summary: Extends online conformal prediction (OCP) to settings where the observed miscoverage indicator may be corrupted. Standard OCP relies on perfect feedback about past coverage; this work develops algorithms with deterministic long-run miscoverage guarantees under corrupted feedback.
abstract: Modern artificial intelligence systems require calibrated uncertainty estimates that remain reliable in sequential and non-stationary environments. Online conformal prediction (OCP) addresses this challenge through adaptively updated prediction sets that provide deterministic long-run miscoverage guarantees. These guarantees, however, hinge on the assumption of perfect feedback about the coverage of past prediction sets. In practice, the observed miscoverage indicator may be corrupted by sensor noise, delayed reporting, or adversarial perturbations. We extend OCP to handle such corrupted feedback.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — Conformal prediction

title: Do Vision-Language Models Understand 3D Scenes or Just Catalogue Objects?
authors: A Maheshwari, D Sahu, N Verma
headline: Benchmark probes whether VLMs represent 3D scene layout or only enumerate objects
summary: Introduces a 3,034-sample human-curated benchmark probing VLM spatial understanding across depth-ordered occlusion, optical-geometry inference, and volumetric rearrangement planning. Tests six frontier and open-weight VLMs to assess whether they represent 3D layout beyond surface object recognition.
abstract: Vision-language models reliably name objects in a scene, but do they represent the 3D layout those objects inhabit? We introduce a 3,034-sample human-curated benchmark targeting three components of spatial understanding: depth-ordered occlusion (probed via three independent counterfactual operationalisations), optical-geometry inference over visible reflections, and volumetric rearrangement planning. Six frontier and open-weight VLMs, scored by trained annotators on 18,204 responses, reveal substantial gaps in 3D understanding.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Low — Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) [residual -1: VLM probing benchmark, only tangentially related to VLAs for robot control]

title: Mechanisms of Misgeneralization in Physical Sequence Modeling
authors: K Nishi, R Tang, K Kumar, CF Park, H Tanaka
headline: How dataset curation choices cause generative sequence models to misgeneralize in physical domains
summary: Studies failure modes of generative sequence models trained for motion planning in physical domains. Shows that engineering choices about how trajectories are distributed in the dataset (e.g., uniform over travel distance) lead to specific, predictable misgeneralization patterns. Relevant to imitation learning and behavior cloning policies.
abstract: Generative sequence models are often trained to plan motion in physical domains, from robotics to mechanical simulations. When constructing a dataset to train such a model, engineers may curate demonstrations to specify how trajectories should be distributed over a physical quantity like travel distance or mechanical energy. For example, a roboticist building a maze navigation agent might choose demonstrations whose travel distances cover a fixed range uniformly, hoping to constrain the agent's behavior. We show that such curation choices induce specific failure modes when the agent encounters out-of-distribution physical contexts.
