notes: Alert: Bansal. Moderate — Imitation learning + Generalist policies

title: HJ-Gauss: A Monte-Carlo HJ Reachability Scheme
authors: L Molu, V Renganathan, N Cho
headline: Monte-Carlo HJ reachability scales to high-dimensional systems via local PDE linearization
summary: Addresses the curse-of-dimensionality bottleneck in classical grid-based HJ reachability via a Monte-Carlo scheme based on local PDE linearization. Targets safety certificates for learned controllers and planning algorithms in trustworthy ML where grid-based solvers are intractable.
abstract: Backward reachable tubes (BRTs), computed via viscous Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) partial differential equations, provide principled safety certificates for learned controllers and planning algorithms in trustworthy machine learning. However, classical grid-based HJ solvers require O(M^n) memory footprint for M grid points per n state dimension. This renders them impractical for high-dimensional systems. We address this bottleneck with a local PDE linearization that enables a Monte-Carlo HJ reachability scheme with significantly reduced memory footprint.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy/Herbert (+1). High — Hamilton-Jacobi reachability + Safe & robust robot control

title: Equalized Coverage in Motion Control Performance Prediction for Self-Adaptive Road Vehicles
authors: O Reuter, R Schubert, M Loba, M Maurer
headline: Equalized-coverage conformal prediction monitors degraded automated-driving controllers
summary: Proposes a lightweight capability monitoring framework for automated road vehicles that uses equalized-coverage conformal prediction to bound motion control performance under actuator degradations and failures. Targets over-actuated vehicles where capability monitoring is critical.
abstract: Automated driving systems require monitoring mechanisms to ensure operation as intended, especially when system elements degrade and/or fail. Hence, capability monitoring is crucial in order to evaluate the system's remaining performance and implement capability-based behavior. In this paper, we investigate the dynamics of a highly over-actuated automated vehicle under actuator degradations and failures, affecting the vehicle's motion control capabilities. We propose a lightweight equalized-coverage conformal prediction framework for predicting motion control performance.
notes: Alert: Tomlin. Moderate — Conformal prediction + Safe & robust robot control

title: Sampling-Based Safe Reinforcement Learning
authors: L Vignola, BD Lee, M Prajapat, M Wendl, M Zeilinger
matched_interests: Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control|Sampling-based motion planning
headline: Model-based RL approximates worst-case safety via constraints across sampled dynamics
summary: Proposes SBSRL, a model-based RL algorithm that maintains safety throughout learning by enforcing constraints jointly across a finite set of sampled dynamics models. Approximates intractable worst-case optimization over uncertain dynamics, enabling practical safety guarantees during exploration.
abstract: Safe exploration remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning (RL), limiting the deployment of RL agents in the real world. We propose Sampling-Based Safe Reinforcement Learning (SBSRL), a model-based RL algorithm that maintains safety throughout the learning process by enforcing constraints jointly across a finite set of dynamics samples. This formulation approximates an intractable worst-case optimization over uncertain dynamics and enables practical safety guarantees in learning-based control.
notes: Alert: Tomlin. High — Reinforcement learning + Safe & robust robot control

title: Adversarial Stress Testing of SPARK Humanoid Safety Filters
authors: S Ghosh, A Sow, L Zhang
matched_interests: Adversarial robustness of robot policies|Safe & robust robot control|Data-driven verification
headline: Adversarial replication and stress testing reveal brittleness in SPARK humanoid safety filters
summary: Replicates and stress-tests SPARK humanoid safety filters to reveal robustness gaps not visible in nominal benchmarks. Targets the regime where standard safety filters modify control actions that may violate collision constraints, but where their behavior under harder environments has not been systematically evaluated.
abstract: Humanoid robots are difficult to deploy safely because they have high-dimensional bodies, many collision constraints, and must operate near people and obstacles. Safety filters help by modifying a nominal control action when it may violate collision-avoidance constraints. Still, nominal benchmark scores do not fully show how these filters behave in harder environments. In this work, we study the robustness of SPARK humanoid safety filters through replication and stress testing.
notes: Alert: Tomlin. Core — Adversarial robustness of robot policies + Safe & robust robot control [residual +1: Directly addresses data-driven adversarial stress-testing of safety filters on learned humanoid systems]

title: Safe Continual Reinforcement Learning under Nonstationarity via Adaptive Safety Constraints
authors: T Tomashevskiy
headline: LILAC+ adapts safety constraints for safe continual RL under nonstationarity
summary: Proposes LILAC+, a framework for safe continual RL under nonstationary environments. Combines three adaptive safety mechanisms (context-based, others) since standard safe RL assumes fixed constraints or stable environments, which fail under distribution shift.
abstract: Safe reinforcement learning in nonstationary environments requires safety mechanisms that adapt as environmental conditions change. Standard safe reinforcement learning methods often assume fixed constraints or stable environmental conditions, which can become inadequate under distribution shift. We propose LILAC+, a framework for safe continual reinforcement learning under nonstationarity that combines three adaptive safety mechanisms: context-based safety reasoning, online constraint adaptation, and risk-aware policy updates.
notes: Alert: Tomlin. Moderate — Reinforcement learning + Safe & robust robot control

title: Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability for Spacecraft Collision Avoidance
authors: L Hui, J Kam, W Su, J Zhou
headline: HJ reachability framework for two-satellite collision avoidance in same orbit
summary: Applies Hamilton-Jacobi reachability to two-satellite collision avoidance in circular orbits using planar Hill-Clohessy-Wiltshire dynamics. Targets minimum-separation requirements consistent with FCC regulations. Domain-specific application of HJ reachability.
abstract: This article presents a Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability framework for a two-satellite collision avoidance problem operating in the same circular orbit, where relative motion is modeled in the radial-tangential-normal (RTN) frame using planar Hill-Clohessy-Wiltshire (HCW) dynamics. We define the target state space as unsafe relative configurations in the orbit plane corresponding to minimum separation requirements consistent with Federal Communications Commission guidance.
notes: Alert: Tomlin. Low — Hamilton-Jacobi reachability [residual -1: Application of HJ reachability to spacecraft — narrow domain, no robotic learning component]

title: Bridging fixed-time stability and safety reachability: A critic-only hierarchical framework for faulty euler-lagrange systems
authors: M Mazare, H Ramezani
headline: Critic-only RL combines fixed-time stability with HJ reachability for faulty Euler-Lagrange systems
summary: Presents a hierarchical framework combining fixed-time stability with real-time HJ reachability learning for safety in Euler-Lagrange systems under actuator faults. Uses critic-only RL architecture to reduce computational cost compared to standard actor-critic schemes.
abstract: Ensuring safety in Euler-Lagrange (EL) systems under actuator faults and state constraints is challenging when computational limitations hinder standard reachability and reinforcement learning (RL) methods. This paper presents a hierarchical framework combining fixed-time stability with real-time Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability learning. To overcome the high computational cost of actor-critic schemes, we develop an efficient critic-only RL architecture to compute optimal safe control under actuator faults.
notes: Alert: Tomlin/Bansal. Low — Hamilton-Jacobi reachability + Reinforcement learning [residual -1: Faulty Euler-Lagrange specific, fixed-time stability control — narrow application]

title: Constrained stabilization control via safe reinforcement learning and control Lyapunov value function
authors: H Wang, S Bo, SM Fazeli, A Yavari, B Liu, Q Zhao, J Liu
headline: SCPO-S with control Lyapunov value function jointly handles stabilization and constraints
summary: Presents SCPO-S, a state-wise constrained policy optimization controller for stabilization, enhanced with a Control Lyapunov Value Function. Jointly optimizes stabilization and constraint satisfaction. Targets safe RL controllers that enforce instantaneous safety constraints.
abstract: This paper presents a general design method for safe reinforcement learning (RL) controllers that enforce instantaneous safety constraints. Applied to stabilization, we propose a dual-objective controller, namely, the State-wise Constrained Policy Optimization based Stabilization controller (SCPO-S), where stabilization and constraint satisfaction are optimized jointly. We further enhance this controller with the Control Lyapunov Value Function (CLVF) to improve stabilization performance under safety constraints.
notes: Alert: Herbert. Moderate — Reinforcement learning + Safe & robust robot control

title: Event-Grounded Sparse Autoencoders for Vision-Language-Action Policies
authors: X Jin, A Chatterjee, P Kumar, R Paleja
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Verification of visuomotor policies|Latent-space safety analysis
headline: Event-grounded sparse autoencoders enable interpretability and steering of VLA policies
summary: Proposes event-grounded sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability of VLA policies. Addresses the difficulty that interventions on VLAs can only be tested via expensive closed-loop rollouts and outputs are robot actions rather than human-readable tokens. Discovers and edits behaviorally meaningful features in VLA hidden states.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies translate language and visual inputs into robot actions, where their hidden representations directly shape closed-loop behavior. However, mechanistic interpretability tools from language and vision-language models do not transfer cleanly to VLAs: outputs are robot actions rather than human-readable tokens, and interventions can only be tested via expensive closed-loop rollouts. We propose an event-grounded interpretability pipeline that learns sparse autoencoder features tied to behaviorally meaningful events in rollouts, enabling intervention and steering.
notes: Alert: Tomlin. Core — Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) + Verification of visuomotor policies [residual +1: Mechanistic interpretability and steering for VLAs — directly relevant to verification/analysis of visuomotor policies]

title: Risk-managed safety screening via stability-constrained reachability tubes for vehicle handling
authors: J Yang, J Liu, K Tian, Y Lin, J Zhang
headline: Stability-constrained reachability tubes screen near-limit emergency maneuvers in vehicles
summary: Formulates a stability-constrained finite-time reachability tube for a planar single-track vehicle with Magic-Formula tires. Near-limit emergency maneuvers are dominated by nonlinear tire saturation and stability constraints; the tube enables online safety screening across discretized primitive libraries.
abstract: Near-limit emergency maneuvers are dominated by nonlinear tire saturation and stability constraints, calling for a short-horizon handling envelope that can be queried online. We formulate a stability-constrained finite-time reachability tube for a planar single-track vehicle with Magic-Formula tires. For a discretized primitive library and an environment channel, the tube aggregates all states that remain dynamically feasible and safe.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy/Bansal. Low — Hamilton-Jacobi reachability + Safe & robust robot control [residual -1: Vehicle-handling specific reachability, no learning component]

title: Learning One-Step Inverse for Performance Improvement of Nonlinear Control Systems: Application to Quadrotor Control
authors: H Chang, J Lane, NP Hyun
headline: Kernel-based one-step inverse learning enlarges region of attraction for quadrotor control
summary: Proposes a learning-based control framework that enlarges the region of attraction for nonlinear controllers under model uncertainties. Constructs a controller learning a one-step inverse of the dynamics via kernel methods. Application to quadrotor control.
abstract: This letter proposes a learning-based control framework to enlarge the region of attraction of controllers for nonlinear systems subject to model uncertainties. When a nominal controller designed for an assumed nominal model is applied to a physical system, discrepancies between the model and the actual dynamics act as perturbations and degrade stability and performance. To mitigate this issue, we construct a controller that learns a one-step inverse of the dynamics via kernel methods.
notes: Alert: Tomlin. Low — Safe & robust robot control [residual -1: Kernel-based inverse learning, narrow control application]

title: NavRL++: A System-Level Framework for Improving Sim-to-Real Transfer in Reinforcement Learning-Based Robot Navigation
authors: Z Xu, H Jin, K Shimada
headline: System-level framework analyzes sim-to-real transfer for RL-based robot navigation
summary: Introduces NavRL++, an RL framework for autonomous navigation, with analysis of how training strategies affect sim-to-real deployment. Argues that prior work overemphasized framework design (input/action/reward) while underanalyzing real-world transfer.
abstract: Recent years have witnessed significant progress in autonomous navigation using reinforcement learning. However, existing approaches largely emphasize reinforcement learning framework design, such as input representations, action spaces, and reward functions, while providing limited analysis of sim-to-real transfer and insufficient insight into how training strategies affect real-world deployment performance. To bridge this gap, we not only introduce an effective RL framework but also dissect the design choices that drive successful sim-to-real transfer.
notes: Alert: Tomlin. Low — Reinforcement learning [residual -1: Systems-level sim-to-real navigation work, no verification/safety connection]

title: Policy Library CBF: Finite-Horizon Safety at Runtime via Parallel Rollouts
authors: T Kim, H Okamoto, B Hoxha, G Fainekos, D Panagou
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Controller composition|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Policy Library CBF picks the least invasive safe fallback via parallel finite-horizon rollouts
summary: Proposes PL-CBF, a runtime safety filter that evaluates a library of fallback policies via parallel finite-horizon rollouts and selects the least invasive safe mode. Solves a QP to minimally modify a nominal policy, with a finite-horizon language analysis providing theoretical guarantees. Strong fit with controller composition.
abstract: Safety-critical autonomy in unstructured environments poses significant challenges for online safety certification under evolving constraints. We propose Policy Library Control Barrier Function (PL-CBF), a runtime safety filter that evaluates a library of fallback policies via parallel finite-horizon rollouts, selects the least invasive safe mode, and enforces safety by solving a quadratic program that minimally modifies a nominal policy. We provide a theoretical analysis based on a finite-horizon language of safe behaviors.
notes: Alert: Tomlin/Fan. High — Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) + Controller composition

title: Reconstruction or Semantics? What Makes a Latent Space Useful for Robotic World Models
authors: S Jha, A Zholus, S Chandar
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Latent-space safety analysis|Verification of visuomotor policies
headline: Investigates what latent-space properties make world models useful for robot policy evaluation
summary: Studies whether reconstruction-based or semantic latents produce more useful world models for robotic policy evaluation. World model-based policy evaluation requires latent spaces that preserve task-relevant information; this paper systematically tests both classes. Directly relevant to latent-space safety analysis of robot policies.
abstract: World model-based policy evaluation is a promising approach for predicting how learned robot policies will behave without expensive real-world rollouts. The choice of latent space, however, profoundly affects how well a world model serves this purpose. We systematically compare reconstruction-based and semantically-trained latents, and study what properties make a latent space useful for policy evaluation in robotic world models.
notes: Alert: Bajcsy. Core — World models for robotics + Latent-space safety analysis [residual +1: Directly investigates latent-space properties for robot world models — a core question for latent-space safety analysis]

title: Active Perception for Deformable Linear Objects Stiffness Estimation
authors: C Dong, A Caporali, H Lan, G Palli
headline: Active perception strategy estimates stiffness of deformable linear objects for manipulation
summary: Proposes an active perception strategy for estimating the stiffness of deformable linear objects (DLOs), crucial for robust DLO manipulation. The robot actively probes the DLO to reduce uncertainty in stiffness estimates.
abstract: Estimating the stiffness of Deformable Linear Objects (DLOs) is crucial for robust manipulation. We propose an active perception strategy where the robot actively probes the DLO to reduce uncertainty in stiffness estimates, enabling downstream manipulation tasks that require knowledge of material properties.
notes: Alert: Herbert. Low — Active learning [residual -1: Active perception for DLO stiffness — narrow manipulation application]

title: A Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability-Guided Search Framework for Efficient and Safe Indoor Planar Robot Navigation
authors: H Hu, C Siu, M Chen
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Sampling-based motion planning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: HJ reachability-guided search efficiently plans safe paths for indoor planar robots
summary: Combines HJ reachability with search-based planning for efficient, safe indoor planar robot navigation. Uses reachability information to guide the search and prune unsafe branches, balancing planning efficiency with safety guarantees.
abstract: Autonomous navigation in cluttered indoor environments requires planning that is both efficient and safe. We propose a Hamilton-Jacobi reachability-guided search framework that uses reachability information to guide a search-based planner, enabling efficient planning while maintaining safety guarantees for planar robot navigation.
notes: Alert: Tomlin/Bajcsy (+1). Moderate — Hamilton-Jacobi reachability + Sampling-based motion planning

title: Time Robustness for Point-Based Semantics of Metric Interval Temporal Logic
authors: S Silvetti, I Compagnucci, F Cairoli, C Trubiani, L Nenzi
headline: Time-robustness semantics for point-based Metric Interval Temporal Logic monitoring
summary: Develops time-robustness semantics for point-based monitoring of Metric Interval Temporal Logic. Extends robustness notions (typically defined for STL) to MITL, supporting time-critical CPS that must satisfy temporal constraints whose timing can be quantitatively assessed.
abstract: Time-critical systems must satisfy temporal constraints whose timing can be quantitatively assessed. We develop time-robustness semantics for point-based monitoring of Metric Interval Temporal Logic (MITL), extending robustness notions typically defined for STL to MITL specifications.
notes: Alert: Lindemann. Moderate — Signal Temporal Logic (STL)

title: CALOS: Control-Affine Lyapunov On-manifold Safety Layer for Safe Deep Reinforcement Learning for Quadrotors
authors: F Cesareo, S Mengozzi, N Mimmo, A Acquaviva
matched_interests: Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: On-manifold Lyapunov safety layer enables safe DRL for quadrotor control
summary: Proposes CALOS, a control-affine Lyapunov-based on-manifold safety layer for safe deep RL on quadrotors. Adds safety constraints that respect the manifold structure of quadrotor dynamics during exploration and deployment.
abstract: Safe Deep Reinforcement Learning has gained traction for high-dimensional robotic control, but enforcing safety during exploration on aerial systems like quadrotors remains challenging. We propose CALOS, a Control-Affine Lyapunov On-manifold Safety layer that respects the manifold structure of quadrotor dynamics while providing safety guarantees during training and deployment of DRL policies.
notes: Alert: Bansal. Moderate — Reinforcement learning + Safe & robust robot control

title: Beyond Safety Filtering: Control Barrier Function-Informed Reinforcement Learning for Connected and Automated Vehicles
authors: J Xu, B Alrifaee
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: CBF-informed RL goes beyond safety filtering for connected and automated vehicles
summary: Argues that conventional safety filtering with CBFs limits expressiveness and proposes a CBF-informed RL framework for connected automated vehicles. The CBF shapes the learning objective rather than being applied only at deployment time.
abstract: Reinforcement learning offers great potential for connected and automated vehicle control, but ensuring safety while preserving performance remains challenging. Conventional approaches use control barrier functions as post-hoc safety filters, which can be overly conservative. We propose a CBF-informed reinforcement learning framework that bakes the CBF into the learning objective, enabling smoother integration of safety and performance.
notes: Alert: Herbert/Tomlin. Moderate — Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) + Reinforcement learning

title: Differentiable Optimization Layered Safety-Critical Control for Risk-Aware Navigation via Conformal Prediction
authors: J Dong, S Wu, Y Fang
matched_interests: Conformal prediction|Safe & robust robot control|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: Differentiable safety-critical control layer with conformal prediction for risk-aware navigation
summary: Combines a differentiable safety-critical control layer with conformal prediction for risk-aware navigation. Treats safety constraints as differentiable optimization layers that can be backpropagated through, with conformal prediction providing the risk quantification.
abstract: Risk-aware navigation requires safety constraints that can be jointly optimized with task objectives. We propose a differentiable optimization-layered safety-critical control framework where conformal prediction provides calibrated risk estimates, and a differentiable QP layer enforces safety constraints. The combination supports end-to-end training of navigation policies with provable risk guarantees.
notes: Alert: Herbert. High — Conformal prediction + Safe & robust robot control

title: GUARDIAN: Safety Filtering for Systems with Perception Models Under Adversarial Attack
authors: N Rober, A Rose, JP How
matched_interests: Adversarial robustness of robot policies|Safe & robust robot control|Verification of visuomotor policies
headline: GUARDIAN: safety filtering for perception-in-the-loop systems under adversarial attack
summary: Proposes GUARDIAN, a safety filtering method for systems with perception models that are subject to adversarial attack. Targets the key failure mode where adversarial perturbations to perception inputs cause learned controllers to take unsafe actions. Directly relevant to black-box verification of perception-based robot policies.
abstract: Safety filtering is an effective method for enforcing safety constraints on learned controllers, but most existing filters assume access to accurate state observations. In real-world deployments, perception modules are vulnerable to adversarial attacks that can cause unsafe behavior. We propose GUARDIAN, a safety filtering framework that maintains safety guarantees for systems with perception models under adversarial attack by robustly accounting for perception uncertainty in the safety-critical loop.
notes: Alert: Bansal. Core — Adversarial robustness of robot policies + Safe & robust robot control [residual +1: Directly addresses safety filtering of perception-in-the-loop systems under adversarial attack — core to verification of black-box visuomotor controllers]

title: Online Conformal Prediction for Non-Exchangeable Panel Data
authors: D Tu, K Giesecke
headline: Online conformal prediction for non-exchangeable panel data with valid coverage
summary: Extends online conformal prediction to panel data settings where multiple units are repeatedly observed over time, breaking standard exchangeability. Develops methods with valid coverage in this setting.
abstract: Panel data, in which multiple units are repeatedly observed over time, arise in many domains but violate standard exchangeability assumptions of conformal prediction. We develop online conformal prediction methods that maintain valid coverage under panel data structure, exploiting cross-unit information without assuming exchangeability across time.
notes: Alert: Lindemann/Bajcsy. Low — Conformal prediction [residual -1: Panel data, finance/statistics motivated — not robotics]

title: RoboFlow4D: A Lightweight Flow World Model Toward Real-Time Flow-Guided Robotic Manipulation
authors: S Lin, J Chen, H Xu, Z Li, G Wang, Y Jing, S Xu, R Zhao
matched_interests: Flow matching|World models for robotics|Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)
headline: Lightweight flow world model enables real-time flow-guided robotic manipulation
summary: Introduces RoboFlow4D, a lightweight flow-based world model for real-time flow-guided robotic manipulation. Combines flow matching for action generation with a 4D (3D + time) world model for planning, targeting fast inference suitable for closed-loop manipulation.
abstract: Planning and manipulation in cluttered 3D environments benefit from world models that can predict future states. Existing 3D world models are often too slow for real-time use. We introduce RoboFlow4D, a lightweight flow-based world model that supports real-time flow-guided robotic manipulation by combining flow matching with efficient 4D (3D + time) scene representations.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — Flow matching + World models for robotics

title: Video Models Can Reason with Verifiable Rewards
authors: T Zhu, S Zhang, JY Huang, S Song, X Wen, Y Li
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Diffusion models|Reinforcement learning
headline: Video diffusion models gain reasoning ability via training with verifiable rewards
summary: Shows that video diffusion models can be trained to reason via verifiable rewards (RLVR-style training). Extends the perception-realism advances of video models to higher-level reasoning and planning over visual content. Has implications for video-as-world-model approaches.
abstract: Video diffusion models have made rapid progress in perceptual realism. We show that video models can additionally acquire reasoning ability when trained with verifiable rewards, akin to RLVR for language models. Trained models exhibit emergent abilities on tasks requiring multi-step visual reasoning and planning over video content.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — World models for robotics + Diffusion models

title: PAPO-VLA: Planning-Aware Policy Optimization for Vision-Language-Action Models
authors: P Guo, J Wang, C Zheng, W Qiang
headline: PAPO-VLA: planning-aware policy optimization recognizes VLAs as planners and executors
summary: Argues VLA policies act simultaneously as planners (making decisions that change execution direction) and executors. Proposes PAPO-VLA, a planning-aware policy optimization framework that explicitly accounts for this duality during closed-loop interaction.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models show promising ability in language-guided robotic tasks. However, making VLA policies reliable remains challenging, because a manipulation task is completed through closed-loop interaction, where each action affects subsequent execution. We revisit VLA policy during execution and argue that a VLA policy acts both as a planner, which makes task-oriented decisions that change the direction of execution, and as an executor. We propose PAPO-VLA, a planning-aware policy optimization that explicitly disentangles the two roles.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. High — Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) + Reinforcement learning

title: RT-K: A key point-aware transformer framework for efficient vision-language robot control
authors: L Li, S Zhao, H Jing, Q Zhang, H Ju, K Sun, J Zhao
headline: Key-point-aware transformer cuts VLM latency for real-time vision-language robot control
summary: Addresses the latency-frequency mismatch between VLMs (1-10 Hz) and robot control (50-200 Hz). Proposes RT-K, a key-point-aware transformer framework that improves VLM inference efficiency for real-time vision-language robot control.
abstract: While Vision Language Models (VLMs) offer transformative potential for adaptive robot decision-making, their practical deployment is severely hindered by a critical mismatch between high computational latency and the strict real-time requirements of robotic control. Conventional VLMs typically operate at low inference frequencies (e.g., 1-10 Hz), which fails to meet the high-frequency (e.g., 50-200 Hz) demands necessary for smooth, dynamic manipulation. We present RT-K, a key-point-aware transformer framework that improves VLM efficiency for real-time vision-language robot control.
notes: Alert: Majumdar. Low — Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) [residual -1: VLA efficiency-focused, marginal relevance beyond practical deployment]

title: Learning Verifiable Control Policies Using Relaxed Verification
authors: Puja Chaudhury, Alexander Estornell, Michael Everett
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability
headline: Training neural controllers with differentiable reachability to guarantee reach-avoid specs at runtime
summary: Proposes integrating verification into the training loop via differentiable reachability analysis, producing policies that can be verified at runtime with lightweight algorithms. Demonstrates on quadrotor and unicycle models that the approach yields learned control policies satisfying desired reach-avoid and invariance specifications, unlike post-hoc verification which may fail if the trained policy is non-compliant.
abstract: To provide safety guarantees for learning-based control systems, recent work has developed formal verification methods to apply after training ends. However, if the trained policy does not meet the specifications, or there is conservatism in the verification algorithm, establishing these guarantees may not be possible. Instead, this work proposes to perform verification throughout training to ultimately aim for policies whose properties can be evaluated throughout runtime with lightweight, relaxed verification algorithms. The approach is to use differentiable reachability analysis and incorporate new components into the loss function. Numerical experiments on a quadrotor model and unicycle model highlight the ability of this approach to lead to learned control policies that satisfy desired reach-avoid and invariance specifications.
notes: Core — verification-in-the-loop for learned controllers, directly relevant to data-driven verification pipeline

title: Beyond Safety Filtering: Control Barrier Function-Informed Reinforcement Learning for Connected and Automated Vehicles
authors: Jianye Xu, Bassam Alrifaee
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Reinforcement learning
headline: CBF constraint values as reward signals for multi-agent safe RL at intersections
summary: Introduces a CBF-informed reward design for multi-agent RL that converts barrier function constraint values into reward signals, eliminating hand-crafted reward heuristics. Evaluated on a four-way intersection with connected autonomous vehicles, showing highest task performance and reduced sensitivity to reward hyperparameters compared to heuristic baselines.
abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) uses rewards to guide learning, yet reward design is typically hand-crafted using heuristics that can be difficult to tune. We propose a Control Barrier Function (CBF)-informed reward design for Multi-Agent RL (MARL) that converts CBF constraint values under joint MARL actions into a reward signal that explicitly guides safe learning. We compare against two heuristic reward baselines in a four-way multi-lane intersection with connected and automated vehicles. Results show that our method achieves the highest task performance and is less sensitive to reward hyperparameters, yielding consistently strong performance across the tested hyperparameter range.
notes: Moderate — CBF+RL integration but focused on autonomous vehicles, not robotics manipulation [residual -1: autonomous driving domain, no robotics/manipulation component]

title: ForceFlow: Learning to Feel and Act via Contact-Driven Flow Matching
authors: Shuoheng Zhang, Yifu Yuan, Hongyao Tang, Yan Zheng, Qiaojun Yu, Pengyi Li, Guowei Huang, Helong Huang, Xingyue Quan, Jianye Hao
headline: Force-aware flow matching policy achieves 37% gains on contact-rich manipulation tasks
summary: Proposes ForceFlow, a force-aware reactive framework built on flow matching for contact-rich manipulation. Uses asymmetric multimodal fusion treating force as a global regulatory signal and a hierarchical Vision-to-Force handover mechanism. Demonstrates 37% success rate improvement over ForceVLA across six real-world contact-rich tasks with lower cost.
abstract: Existing imitation learning methods enable robots to interact autonomously with the physical environment. However, contact-rich manipulation tasks remain a significant challenge due to complex contact dynamics that demand high-precision force feedback and control. Although recent efforts have attempted to integrate force/torque sensing into policies, how to build a simple yet effective framework that achieves robust generalization under multimodal observations remains an open question. In this paper, we propose ForceFlow, a force-aware reactive framework built upon flow matching. For contact-stage policy design, we investigate force signal fusion mechanisms and adopt an asymmetric multimodal fusion architecture that treats force as a global regulatory signal, combined with a joint prediction paradigm that enhances the policy's understanding of instantaneous force and historical information, thereby achieving deep coupling between force and motion. For task-level hierarchical decomposition, we divide manipulation into a vision-dominant approach stage and a touch-dominant interaction stage, with a Vision-to-Force handover mechanism that explicitly decouples spatial generalization from contact regulation. Experimental results across six real-world contact-rich tasks demonstrate that ForceFlow achieves a 37% success rate improvement over the strong baseline ForceVLA while maintaining significantly lower cost.
notes: High — flow matching for contact-rich manipulation, extends flow policy paradigm with tactile sensing

title: Bridging Model Predictive Control and Deep Learning for Scalable Reachability Analysis
authors: Zeyuan Feng, Le Qiu, Somil Bansal
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Safe & robust robot control
headline: MPC-guided neural network training scales HJ reachability to 40D systems
summary: Bridges model predictive control with deep learning for scalable Hamilton-Jacobi reachability analysis. Uses MPC to generate approximate reachability solutions at collocation points that guide neural network training, iteratively refining both. Demonstrates on systems up to 40 dimensions (2D drone, 13D quadrotor, 7D car, 40D publisher-subscriber), significantly improving accuracy and robustness of reachable sets.
abstract: Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability analysis is a widely used method for ensuring the safety of robotic systems. Traditional approaches compute reachable sets by numerically solving an HJ Partial Differential Equation (PDE) over a grid, which is computationally prohibitive due to the curse of dimensionality. Recent learning-based methods have sought to address this challenge by approximating reachability solutions using neural networks trained with PDE residual error. However, these approaches often suffer from unstable training dynamics and suboptimal solutions due to the weak learning signal provided by the residual loss. In this work, we propose a novel approach that leverages model predictive control (MPC) techniques to guide and accelerate the reachability learning process. Observing that HJ reachability is inherently rooted in optimal control, we utilize MPC to generate approximate reachability solutions at key collocation points, which are then used to tactically guide the neural network training by ensuring compliance with these approximations. Moreover, we iteratively refine the MPC generated solutions using the learned reachability solution, mitigating convergence to local optima. Case studies on a 2D vertical drone, a 13D quadrotor, a 7D F1Tenth car, and a 40D publisher-subscriber system demonstrate that bridging MPC with deep learning yields significant improvements in the robustness and accuracy of reachable sets, as well as corresponding safety assurances, compared to existing methods.
notes: Core — scalable HJ reachability via learned approximation, directly relevant to data-driven verification

title: pi_0.7: a Steerable Generalist Robotic Foundation Model with Emergent Capabilities
authors: Physical Intelligence et al.
headline: Physical Intelligence's steerable foundation model enables zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization
summary: Presents pi_0.7, a steerable generalist robotic foundation model using diverse context conditioning (language, subgoal images, performance metadata) during training. Achieves zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization, out-of-the-box performance matching specialized RL models on tasks like espresso machine operation, and compositional task generalization across multiple robot platforms.
abstract: We present a new robotic foundation model, called pi_0.7, that can enable strong out-of-the-box performance in a wide range of scenarios. pi_0.7 can follow diverse language instructions in unseen environments, including multi-stage tasks with various kitchen appliances, provide zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization, for example enabling a robot to fold laundry without seeing the task before, and perform challenging tasks such as operating an espresso machine out of the box at a level of performance that matches much more specialized RL-finetuned models. The main idea behind pi_0.7 is to use diverse context conditioning during training. This conditioning information, contained in the prompt, makes it possible to steer the model precisely to perform many tasks with different strategies. It is conditioned not just on a language command that describes what it should do, but on additional multimodal information that also describes the manner or strategy in which it should do it, including metadata about task performance and subgoal images. This enables pi_0.7 to use very diverse data, including demonstrations, potentially suboptimal (autonomous) data including failures, and data from non-robot sources.
notes: High — major VLA/foundation model release from Physical Intelligence, relevant as policy class for verification

title: ForeDiffusion: Foresight-Conditioned Diffusion Policy via Future View Construction for Robot Manipulation
authors: Weize Xie, Yi Ding, Ying He, Leilei Wang, Binwen Bai, Zheyi Zhao, Chenyang Wang, F. Richard Yu
headline: Foresight-conditioned diffusion policy outperforms baselines by 23% on complex manipulation
summary: Introduces ForeDiffusion, which injects predicted future view representations into the diffusion process to make policies forward-looking and able to correct trajectory deviations. Uses a dual loss combining denoising and future observation consistency. Achieves 80% average success rate on Adroit/MetaWorld, outperforming existing diffusion methods by 23% on complex tasks.
abstract: Diffusion strategies have advanced visual motor control by progressively denoising high-dimensional action sequences, providing a promising method for robot manipulation. However, as task complexity increases, the success rate of existing baseline models decreases considerably. Analysis indicates that current diffusion strategies are confronted with two limitations. First, these strategies only rely on short-term observations as conditions. Second, the training objective remains limited to a single denoising loss, which leads to error accumulation and causes grasping deviations. To address these limitations, this paper proposes Foresight-Conditioned Diffusion (ForeDiffusion), by injecting the predicted future view representation into the diffusion process. As a result, the policy is guided to be forward-looking, enabling it to correct trajectory deviations. Following this design, ForeDiffusion employs a dual loss mechanism, combining the traditional denoising loss and the consistency loss of future observations, to achieve the unified optimization. Extensive evaluation on the Adroit suite and the MetaWorld benchmark demonstrates that ForeDiffusion achieves an average success rate of 80% for the overall task, significantly outperforming the existing mainstream diffusion methods by 23% in complex tasks.
notes: High — novel diffusion policy architecture with foresight conditioning, advances diffusion policy paradigm

title: Compose Your Policies! Improving Diffusion-based or Flow-based Robot Policies via Test-time Distribution-level Composition
authors: Jiahang Cao, Yize Huang, Hanzhong Guo, Rui Zhang, Mu Nan, Weijian Mai, Jiaxu Wang, Hao Cheng, Jingkai Sun, Gang Han, Wen Zhao, Qiang Zhang, Yijie Guo, Qihao Zheng, Chunfeng Song, Xiao Li, Ping Luo, Andrew F. Luo
headline: Training-free composition of diffusion/flow policies provably exceeds individual policy performance
summary: Establishes theoretical foundations showing convex composition of distributional scores from multiple diffusion/flow models yields superior objectives. Proposes General Policy Composition (GPC), a training-free method for plug-and-play composition of heterogeneous policies (VA, VLA, diffusion, flow-matching). Validated on Robomimic, PushT, RoboTwin benchmarks and real-world tasks.
abstract: Diffusion-based models for robotic control, including vision-language-action (VLA) and vision-action (VA) policies, have demonstrated significant capabilities. Yet their advancement is constrained by the high cost of acquiring large-scale interaction datasets. This work introduces an alternative paradigm for enhancing policy performance without additional model training. Perhaps surprisingly, we demonstrate that the composed policies can exceed the performance of either parent policy. Our contribution is threefold. First, we establish a theoretical foundation showing that the convex composition of distributional scores from multiple diffusion models can yield a superior one-step functional objective compared to any individual score. Second, we propose General Policy Composition (GPC), a training-free method that enhances performance by combining the distributional scores of multiple pre-trained policies via a convex combination and test-time search. Third, we provide extensive empirical validation on Robomimic, PushT, and RoboTwin benchmarks, alongside real-world robotic evaluations.
notes: High — directly addresses controller composition for diffusion/flow policies with theoretical guarantees

title: From Demonstrations to Safe Deployment: Path-Consistent Safety Filtering for Diffusion Policies
authors: Ralf Romer, Julian Balletshofer, Jakob Thumm, Marco Pavone, Angela P. Schoellig, Matthias Althoff
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control|Diffusion models
headline: Path-consistent safety filtering gives diffusion policies formal guarantees without killing performance
summary: Proposes PACS, a safety filtering approach for diffusion policies that performs path-consistent braking using set-based reachability analysis. Keeps filtered actions consistent with the training distribution to maintain task performance while providing formal safety guarantees. Outperforms CBF-based reactive safety by up to 68% in task success on three real-world human-robot interaction tasks.
abstract: Diffusion policies (DPs) achieve state-of-the-art performance on complex manipulation tasks by learning from large-scale demonstration datasets, often spanning multiple embodiments and environments. However, they cannot guarantee safe behavior, requiring external safety mechanisms. These, however, alter actions in ways unseen during training, causing unpredictable behavior and performance degradation. To address these problems, we propose path-consistent safety filtering (PACS) for DPs. Our approach performs path-consistent braking on a trajectory computed from the sequence of generated actions. In this way, we keep the execution consistent with the training distribution of the policy, maintaining the learned, task-completing behavior. To enable real-time deployment and handle uncertainties, we verify safety using set-based reachability analysis. Our experimental evaluation in simulation and on three challenging real-world human-robot interaction tasks shows that PACS provides formal safety guarantees in dynamic environments, preserves task success rates, and outperforms reactive safety approaches such as control barrier functions by up to 68% in terms of task success.
notes: Core — formal safety guarantees for diffusion policies via reachability, directly bridges verification and learned policies [residual +1: directly addresses verification/safety of learned diffusion policies with formal guarantees]

title: PC-Diffuser: Path-Consistent Capsule CBF Safety Filtering for Diffusion-Based Trajectory Planner
authors: Eugene Ku, Yiwei Lyu
matched_interests: Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Safe & robust robot control|Diffusion models
headline: Embedding CBF safety filters inside diffusion denoising loops for certified trajectory planning
summary: Presents PC-Diffuser, which embeds certifiable barrier-function structures directly into the denoising loop of diffusion-based trajectory planners. Uses capsule-distance barrier functions for collision risk, converts waypoints to feasible motion under bicycle kinematics, and applies path-consistent filtering to eliminate constraint violations without geometric distortion. Targets autonomous driving scenarios.
abstract: Autonomous driving in complex traffic requires planners that generalize beyond hand-crafted rules, motivating data-driven approaches that learn behavior from expert demonstrations. Diffusion-based trajectory planners have recently shown strong closed-loop performance by iteratively denoising a full-horizon plan, but they remain difficult to certify and can fail catastrophically in rare or out-of-distribution scenarios. To address this challenge, we present PC-Diffuser, a safety augmentation framework that embeds a certifiable, path-consistent barrier-function structure directly into the denoising loop of diffusion planning. The key idea is to make safety an intrinsic part of trajectory generation rather than a post-hoc fix: we enforce forward invariance along the rollout while preserving the diffusion model's intended path geometry.
notes: Moderate — CBF safety for diffusion planners, relevant concept but autonomous driving domain [residual -1: autonomous driving focus, not robotics manipulation]

title: Efficiently Learning Robust Torque-based Locomotion Through Reinforcement with Model-Based Supervision
authors: Yashuai Yan, Tobias Egle, Christian Ott, Dongheui Lee
headline: Model-based oracle supervision enables efficient residual RL for robust bipedal walking
summary: Combines model-based bipedal locomotion (DCM planner + whole-body controller) with residual RL, using a model-based oracle with privileged ground-truth dynamics access to supervise the residual policy. Eliminates need for extensive reward shaping while achieving robust and adaptive walking under real-world uncertainties.
abstract: We propose a control framework that integrates model-based bipedal locomotion with residual reinforcement learning (RL) to achieve robust and adaptive walking in the presence of real-world uncertainties. Our approach leverages a model-based controller, comprising a Divergent Component of Motion (DCM) trajectory planner and a whole-body controller, as a reliable base policy. To address the uncertainties of inaccurate dynamics modeling and sensor noise, we introduce a residual policy trained through RL with domain randomization. Crucially, we employ a model-based oracle policy, which has privileged access to ground-truth dynamics during training, to supervise the residual policy via a novel supervised loss. This supervision enables the policy to efficiently learn corrective behaviors that compensate for unmodeled effects without extensive reward shaping.
notes: Moderate — residual RL for robust locomotion, relevant to safe robot control but not verification-focused

title: Is VLA Reasoning Faithful? Probing Safety of Chain-of-Causation in Autonomous Driving Models
authors: Nicanor Mayumu, Xiaoheng Deng, Patrick Mukala
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Adversarial robustness of robot policies|VLA reasoning and chain-of-thought
headline: VLA driving models show only 42.5% reasoning fidelity and 97.7% trajectory fragility
summary: First systematic study of faithfulness in VLA driving models, analyzing 300 inferences on 100 scenarios. Finds reasoning fidelity of only 42.5%, 94 missed pedestrians in pedestrian-relevant scenes, 97.7% trajectory fragility under mild perturbations, and 48.3% reasoning-action consistency. Formalizes faithfulness information-theoretically and proposes a four-component safety architecture.
abstract: We present the first systematic study of faithfulness in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) driving models, analyzing 300 Alpamayo-R1-10B inferences across 100 diverse PhysicalAI-AV scenarios. Our main finding is that output natural-language rationales with trajectories may be significantly unfaithful: overall reasoning fidelity is only 42.5%, with Chain-of-Causation matching scene reality less than half the time; 94 missed pedestrians in one-third of pedestrian-relevant scenes; 97.7% trajectory fragility under mild visual perturbations; and only 48.3% mean reasoning-action consistency.
notes: High — VLA reasoning faithfulness and safety evaluation, directly relevant to verification of VLA policies

title: DexImit: Learning Bimanual Dexterous Manipulation from Monocular Human Videos
authors: Juncheng Mu, Sizhe Yang, Yiming Bao, Hojin Bae, Tianming Wei, Linning Xu, Boyi Li, Huazhe Xu, Jiangmiao Pang
headline: Automated pipeline converts monocular human videos to bimanual dexterous robot demonstrations
summary: Proposes DexImit, an automated framework converting monocular human manipulation videos into physically plausible robot training data. Uses a four-stage pipeline: hand-object reconstruction, subtask decomposition, robot trajectory synthesis, and data augmentation for zero-shot deployment. Handles diverse tasks including tool use, long-horizon manipulation, and fine-grained bimanual tasks.
abstract: Data scarcity fundamentally limits the generalization of bimanual dexterous manipulation, as real-world data collection for dexterous hands is expensive and labor-intensive. Human manipulation videos, as a direct carrier of manipulation knowledge, offer significant potential for scaling up robot learning. However, the substantial embodiment gap between human hands and robotic dexterous hands makes direct pretraining from human videos extremely challenging. To bridge this gap, we propose DexImit, an automated framework that converts monocular human manipulation videos into physically plausible robot data, without any additional information.
notes: Moderate — imitation learning from human videos for dexterous manipulation, tangentially relevant

title: Hand-in-the-Loop: Improving VLA Policies for Dexterous Manipulation via Seamless Hand-Arm Intervention
authors: Zhuohang Li, Liqun Huang, Wei Xu, Zhengming Zhu, Nie Lin, Xiao Ma, Xinjun Sheng, Ruoshi Wen
headline: Seamless human intervention for VLA dexterous policies cuts jitter by 99.8%
summary: Introduces HandITL, a seamless human-in-the-loop intervention method for VLA policies in dexterous manipulation. Blends human corrective intent with autonomous policy execution to avoid gesture jumps. Reduces intervention jitter by 99.8%, grasp failures by 87.5%, and completion time by 19.1% on bimanual dexterous tasks.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are prone to compounding errors in dexterous manipulation, where high-dimensional action spaces and contact-rich dynamics amplify small policy deviations over long horizons. While Interactive Imitation Learning (IIL) can refine policies through human correction data, applying it to high-degree-of-freedom robotic hands remains challenging due to a command mismatch between human teleoperation and policy execution at the intervention moment. We present Hand-in-the-Loop (HandITL), a seamless human-in-the-loop intervention method that blends human corrective intent with autonomous policy execution to avoid gesture jumps during bimanual dexterous manipulation.
notes: Moderate — VLA policy refinement via human intervention, relevant to VLA improvement but not verification

title: FP3: A 3D Foundation Policy for Robotic Manipulation
authors: Rujia Yang, Geng Chen, Chuan Wen, Yang Gao
headline: First large-scale 3D foundation policy achieves 90%+ success with just 80 demos
summary: Introduces FP3, the first large-scale 3D foundation policy for robotic manipulation using a scalable diffusion transformer architecture pre-trained on 60k trajectories with point cloud observations. Achieves over 90% success on new tasks with only 80 demonstrations in novel environments with unseen objects.
abstract: Following its success in natural language processing and computer vision, foundation models that are pre-trained on large-scale multi-task datasets have also shown great potential in robotics. However, most existing robot foundation models rely solely on 2D image observations, ignoring 3D geometric information. In this paper, we introduce FP3, a first large-scale 3D foundation policy model for robotic manipulation. FP3 builds on a scalable diffusion transformer architecture and is pre-trained on 60k trajectories with point cloud observations. Experiments on real robots demonstrate that with only 80 demonstrations, FP3 can learn a new task with over 90% success rates in novel environments with unseen objects.
notes: High — 3D foundation policy with diffusion transformer, relevant as new policy architecture for manipulation

title: Policy Contrastive Decoding for Robotic Foundation Models
authors: Shihan Wu, Xu Luo, Ji Zhang, Junlin Xie, Jingkuan Song, Heng Tao Shen, Lianli Gao
headline: Training-free contrastive decoding boosts pi_0 real-world performance by 108%
summary: Proposes Policy Contrastive Decoding (PCD), a training-free plugin improving robotic foundation models by contrasting action distributions from original and object-masked visual inputs. Works across policy types (OpenVLA, Octo, pi_0) without finetuning. Enhances pi_0 by 8.9% in simulation and 108% in real-world environments.
abstract: Robotic foundation models hold immense potential to enable flexible, general-purpose and dexterous robotic systems. Despite their advancements, our empirical experiments reveal that existing robot policies are prone to learning spurious correlations from pre-training trajectories. To tackle this, we propose Policy Contrastive Decoding (PCD), which redirects the robot policy's focus toward object-relevant visual clues by contrasting action probability distributions derived from original and object-masked visual inputs. As a training-free method, PCD can improve different types of robot policies without finetuning. PCD enhances pi_0 by 8.9% in simulation and by 108% in real-world.
notes: Moderate — VLA improvement method, relevant as analysis of generalist policy failure modes

title: Unifying Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Prashant Solanki, Isabelle El-Hajj, Jasper van Beers, Erik-Jan van Kampen, Coen de Visser
matched_interests: Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Data-driven verification|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Formal proof unifying HJ reachability and RL value iteration preserves safety semantics
summary: Proves HJ reachability and RL can be unified through a running cost formulation, where the travel-cost value function is the unique bounded viscosity solution of a time-dependent HJB PDE whose sublevel set equals the backward-reachable tube. Shows RL value iteration fixed points converge to the HJB viscosity solution. Validates that the framework preserves reachability-based safety semantics while being compatible with deep RL.
abstract: We unify Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) reachability and Reinforcement Learning (RL) through a proposed running cost formulation. We prove that the resultant travel-cost value function is the unique bounded viscosity solution of a time-dependent Hamilton-Jacobi Bellman (HJB) Partial Differential Equation (PDE) with zero terminal data, whose negative sublevel set equals the strict backward-reachable tube. Using a forward reparameterization and a contraction inducing Bellman update, we show that fixed points of small-step RL value iteration converge to the viscosity solution of the forward discounted HJB. Experiments on a classical benchmark validate this connection.
notes: Core — theoretical unification of HJ reachability and RL, directly relevant to data-driven safety verification

title: Autonomous Integration and Improvement of Robotic Assembly using Skill Graph Representations
authors: Peiqi Yu, Philip Huang, Chaitanya Chawla, Guanya Shi, Jiaoyang Li, Changliu Liu
headline: Skill Graph representations enable autonomous integration and improvement of robotic assembly
summary: Presents a framework using Skill Graph representations for autonomous integration of robotic assembly systems. Organizes capabilities as verb-based skills with explicit semantic descriptions, executable policies, pre/post-conditions, and evaluators. Enables semantic-level planning, grounded execution, and closed-loop performance improvement.
abstract: Robotic assembly systems traditionally require substantial manual engineering effort to integrate new tasks, adapt to new environments, and improve performance over time. This paper presents a framework for autonomous integration and continuous improvement of robotic assembly systems based on Skill Graph representations. A Skill Graph organizes robot capabilities as verb-based skills, explicitly linking semantic descriptions with executable policies, pre-conditions, post-conditions, and evaluators.
notes: Moderate — skill composition framework for assembly, relevant to controller composition interest

title: Uncertainty-Aware Robotic World Model Makes Offline Model-Based Reinforcement Learning Work on Real Robots
authors: Chenhao Li, Andreas Krause, Marco Hutter
headline: Uncertainty-aware world model enables offline MBRL to work on real quadruped and humanoid
summary: Presents RWM-U, an uncertainty-aware autoregressive world model with epistemic uncertainty estimation for temporally consistent multi-step rollouts. Combined with MOPO-PPO for uncertainty-penalized policy optimization. Evaluated on real quadruped and humanoid robots from offline datasets, consistently outperforming model-free and uncertainty-unaware baselines.
abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has achieved impressive results in robotics, yet high-performing pipelines remain highly task-specific, with little reuse of prior data. Offline Model-based RL (MBRL) offers greater data efficiency by training policies entirely from existing datasets, but suffers from compounding errors and distribution shift in long-horizon rollouts. We present a principled pipeline for making offline MBRL effective on physical robots. Our RWM-U extends autoregressive world models with epistemic uncertainty estimation, enabling temporally consistent multi-step rollouts with uncertainty effectively propagated over long horizons.
notes: High — world model with uncertainty for real robot offline RL, relevant to world models interest

title: World Model for Robot Learning: A Comprehensive Survey
authors: Bohan Hou, Gen Li, Jindou Jia, Tuo An, Xinying Guo, Sicong Leng, Haoran Geng, Yanjie Ze, Tatsuya Harada, Philip Torr, Oier Mees, Marc Pollefeys, Zhuang Liu, Jiajun Wu, Pieter Abbeel, Jitendra Malik, Yilun Du, Jianfei Yang
matched_interests: World models for robotics|Reinforcement learning|Generalist policies
headline: Comprehensive survey unifies fragmented world model literature for robot learning
summary: Comprehensive review of world models from a robot-learning perspective covering how they couple with policies, serve as learned simulators for RL and evaluation, and have progressed to foundation-scale formulations. Covers datasets, benchmarks, and evaluation protocols across architectures and domains.
abstract: World models, which are predictive representations of how environments evolve under actions, have become a central component of robot learning. They support policy learning, planning, simulation, evaluation, data generation, and have advanced rapidly with the rise of foundation models and large-scale video generation. However, the literature remains fragmented across architectures, functional roles, and embodied application domains. To address this gap, we present a comprehensive review of world models from a robot-learning perspective.
notes: Moderate — comprehensive survey of world models for robotics, useful reference but not direct research contribution

title: Not All Features Are Created Equal: A Mechanistic Study of Vision-Language-Action Models
authors: Bryce Grant, Xijia Zhao, Peng Wang
matched_interests: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs)|Adversarial robustness of robot policies
headline: Mechanistic VLA study reveals visual pathway dominates action generation across all architectures
summary: Applies activation injection, sparse autoencoders, and linear probes to six VLA models (80M-7B parameters, 394K+ episodes). Finds visual pathway dominates action generation with spatially bound motor programs. Language sensitivity depends on task structure, not model design. Multi-pathway architectures encode motor programs vs. goal semantics in separable activation subspaces.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models combine perception, language, and motor control in a single architecture, yet how they translate multimodal inputs into actions remains poorly understood. We apply activation injection, sparse autoencoders, and linear probes to six models spanning 80M-7B parameters across 394,000+ rollout episodes on four benchmarks. The visual pathway dominates action generation across all architectures.
notes: Moderate — mechanistic understanding of VLA internals, relevant to verification/interpretability of VLA policies

title: Parallel Differentiable Reachability for Learning and Planning with Certified Neural Dynamics and Controllers
authors: Keyi Shen, Glen Chou
matched_interests: Data-driven verification|Hamilton-Jacobi reachability|Safe & robust robot control
headline: GPU-parallelized differentiable reachability enables certified online planning with neural controllers
summary: Presents a JAX-based differentiable reachability framework combining Taylor-model flowpipes with CROWN-style bound propagation for neural dynamics. Enables certified training and reachability-aware MPC for NN controllers. Demonstrated on manipulation and quadrotor tasks up to 72D with hardware validation.
abstract: Neural network (NN) dynamics models and control policies achieve strong performance in robotics, but providing sound guarantees under uncertainty remains difficult, especially for closed-loop NN systems. Existing reachability tools provide formal over-approximations, yet are often non-differentiable, overly conservative, or too slow for modern learning and online planning pipelines. To address this, we present a parallelizable, differentiable reachability framework in JAX for continuous- and discrete-time systems with analytical and NN-based dynamics and controllers. Our framework combines Taylor-model flowpipe construction with CROWN-style linear bound propagation through a unified representation that preserves affine dependencies while supporting GPU-batched computation and automatic differentiation. Building on this reachability primitive, we develop (i) a certified training method that encourages reachability-friendly dynamics models and controllers, and (ii) a reachability-aware sampling-based MPC scheme with gradient-based refinement. Experiments on non-prehensile manipulation and quadrotor tasks, including hardware and higher-dimensional evaluations (up to 72D), demonstrate practical online planning while maintaining certified reachable-set over-approximations under bounded uncertainty.
notes: Core — differentiable reachability for certified neural controllers, directly relevant to data-driven verification [residual +1: directly addresses certified verification of neural dynamics and controllers with practical online planning]

title: Robust Koopman Control Barrier Filters for Safe Actor-Critic Reinforcement Learning
authors: Dhruv S. Kushwaha, Zoleikha A. Biron
matched_interests: Koopman operator methods|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)|Reinforcement learning|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Koopman-CBF safety filter achieves zero constraint violations in safe actor-critic RL
summary: Proposes Robust Koopman-CBF SAC, combining data-driven Koopman predictors with CBF safety filters for safe RL. Achieves zero violations on CartPole tasks while matching unconstrained returns. Exposes limitations of first-order velocity barriers on high-dimensional locomotion tasks.
abstract: Safe reinforcement learning (RL) for robotic systems requires policies that improve task performance while satisfying state and input constraints during both training and deployment. Control barrier functions (CBFs) provide a principled mechanism for enforcing forward invariance through minimally invasive safety filters, but their use in model-free RL is limited by the need for accurate dynamics and hand-designed barrier certificates. We propose Robust Koopman-CBF SAC, a safety-filtered actor-critic framework that learns a finite-dimensional Koopman predictor from data, constructs affine CBF constraints in the lifted space, and enforces them through a quadratic-program safety layer. To account for finite-dimensional Koopman approximation error, the CBF condition is tightened using a projected residual margin estimated from held-out rollout data. The critic is trained on the executed safe action, while the actor is regularized toward the Koopman-CBF feasible set, reducing dependence on the filter over training. Across safe-control benchmarks, the method achieves zero constraint violations on CartPole stabilization and tracking while matching or exceeding unconstrained SAC returns. On high-dimensional Safety Gymnasium locomotion tasks, the method reduces violations in some settings but also exposes important limitations of first-order velocity barriers and linear EDMD models, motivating high-order and multi-step Koopman-CBF extensions. These results suggest that robust Koopman-CBF filters are a promising bridge between model-free RL and certifiable safety, while clarifying the structural conditions under which such filters remain effective.
notes: High — Koopman-CBF fusion for safe RL, combines data-driven dynamics with CBF safety filtering

title: Safe Data-Driven Control and Dynamical Learning via Constrained Neural Architectures and Koopman Operators
authors: Lin Feng, Xin He
matched_interests: Koopman operator methods|Data-driven verification|Safe & robust robot control
headline: Certified Koopman-neural ODE framework achieves provable stability for data-driven control
summary: Integrates Neural ODEs with Koopman theory and ISS verification via convex LMIs for certifiably stable data-driven control. Demonstrates 61% tracking RMSE reduction and 54% state estimation improvement over uncertified baselines on vehicle and oscillator benchmarks.
abstract: The deployment of learning-based models in safety-critical control systems demands mathematical guarantees that standard regression architectures cannot provide. This paper presents an integrated framework that bridges Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs), measurement-induced geometric structures, and Koopman operator theory, with the explicit aim of producing data-driven models whose stability certificates are computable, not merely conjectured. Three complementary components are developed and analyzed. First, ControlSynth Neural ODEs enforce global convergence through tractable linear matrix inequalities (LMIs), enabling complex nonlinear dynamics to be captured without sacrificing boundedness guarantees. Second, the ICODE formulation incorporates extrinsic environmental inputs into the learned vector field, while measurement-induced bundle structures confine state trajectories to physically admissible manifolds. Third, a systematic ISS verification pipeline certifies the input-to-state stability of Koopman-identified models via a convex L_2-gain LMI, converting an otherwise intractable robustness question into a solvable semidefinite program. The certified model is embedded in an ICODE-MPPI controller, which uses continuous-time residual learning inside a stochastic sampling loop to deliver robust path tracking under parametric uncertainty and persistent disturbances. Numerical experiments on a vehicle path-tracking benchmark and a nonlinear mechanical oscillator demonstrate up to a 61% reduction in tracking RMSE and a 54% reduction in state estimation error relative to uncertified baselines, with near-zero LMI violation rates across all evaluated disturbance levels.
notes: High — certified Koopman-based control with provable stability guarantees, relevant to data-driven verification

title: Hierarchical End-to-End Taylor Bounds for Complete Neural Network Verification
authors: Taha Entesari, Mahyar Fazlyab
headline: HiTaB exploits curvature Lipschitz continuity for tighter neural network safety certificates
summary: Introduces HiTaB, a verification framework using higher-order Taylor bounds with compositional Hessian Lipschitz propagation for smooth neural networks. Provides tighter reachable set bounds than existing first/second-order methods for both L2 and Linf input sets.
abstract: Reachability analysis of neural networks, which seeks to compute or bound the set of outputs attainable over a given input domain, is central to certifying safety and robustness in learning-enabled physical systems. Since exact reachable set computation is generally intractable, existing methods typically rely on tractable overapproximations. Examining the state of the art for smooth, twice-differentiable networks, we observe that existing approaches exploit at most second-order information and do not systematically leverage higher-order information. In this work, we introduce HiTaB, a novel verification framework that exploits second-order smoothness through both the Hessian, nabla^2 f, and its Lipschitz constant, L_{nabla^2 f}. We further develop a unified hierarchy of zeroth-, first-, and second-order bounds, together with precise conditions under which higher-order approximations yield provable improvements. Our main technical contribution is a compositional procedure for efficiently bounding L_{nabla^2 f} in deep neural networks via layerwise propagation of curvature bounds. We extend the framework to both l_2- and l_inf-constrained input sets and show how it can be integrated into branch-and-bound verification pipelines. To our knowledge, this is the first practical reachability analysis framework for smooth neural networks that systematically exploits Lipschitz continuity of curvature, leading to tighter and more informative safety certificates.
notes: Moderate — NN verification method, relevant to verification pipeline but pure theory without robotics application [residual -1: pure verification theory, no robotics or data-driven component]

title: Who Moved My Distribution? Conformal Prediction for Interactive Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Allen Emmanuel Binny, Anushri Dixit
headline: Iterative conformal prediction handles endogenous distribution shift in multi-agent planning
summary: Introduces an iterative conformal prediction framework addressing endogenous distribution shift when uncertainty-aware agents change behavior in interactive settings. Provides probabilistic safety guarantees while avoiding over-conservative planning, improving success rates by 9.6% over baselines.
abstract: Uncertainty-aware prediction is essential for safe motion planning, especially when using learned models to forecast the behavior of surrounding agents. Conformal prediction is a statistical tool often used to produce uncertainty-aware prediction regions for machine learning models. Most existing frameworks utilizing conformal prediction-based uncertainty predictions assume that the surrounding agents are non-interactive. This is because in closed-loop, as uncertainty-aware agents change their behavior to account for prediction uncertainty, the surrounding agents respond to this change, leading to a distribution shift which we call endogenous distribution shift. To address this challenge, we introduce an iterative conformal prediction framework that systematically adapts the uncertainty-aware ego-agent controller to the endogenous distribution shift. The proposed method provides probabilistic safety guarantees while adapting to the evolving behavior of reactive, non-ego agents. We establish a model for the endogenous distribution shift and provide the conditions for the iterative conformal prediction pipeline to converge under such a distribution shift. We validate our framework in simulation for 2- and 3- agent interaction scenarios, demonstrating collision avoidance without resulting in overly conservative behavior and an overall improvement in success rates of up to 9.6% compared to other conformal prediction-based baselines.
notes: High — novel conformal prediction for interactive multi-agent systems with endogenous distribution shift

title: Contractive Diffusion Policies: Robust Action Diffusion via Contractive Score-Based Sampling with Differential Equations
authors: Amin Abyaneh, Charlotte Morissette, Mohamad H. Danesh, Anas El Houssaini, David Meger, Gregory Dudek, Hsiu-Chin Lin
headline: Contractive diffusion sampling reduces action variance and improves robustness in policy learning
summary: Introduces Contractive Diffusion Policies that induce contraction in diffusion sampling dynamics to pull nearby flows closer, reducing solver/score-matching errors and action variance. Provides theoretical analysis and practical implementation with pronounced benefits under data scarcity across simulation and real-world settings.
abstract: Diffusion policies have emerged as powerful generative models for offline policy learning, whose sampling process can be rigorously characterized by a score function guiding a stochastic differential equation (SDE). However, the same score-based SDE modeling that grants diffusion policies the flexibility to learn diverse behavior also incurs solver and score-matching errors, large data requirements, and inconsistencies in action generation. While less critical in image generation, these inaccuracies compound and lead to failure in continuous control settings. We introduce Contractive Diffusion Policies (CDPs) to induce contractive behavior in the diffusion sampling dynamics. Contraction pulls nearby flows closer to enhance robustness against solver and score-matching errors while reducing unwanted action variance. We develop an in-depth theoretical analysis along with a practical implementation recipe to incorporate CDPs into existing diffusion policy architectures with minimal modification and computational cost. We evaluate CDPs for offline learning by conducting extensive experiments in simulation and real world settings. Across benchmarks, CDPs often outperform baseline policies, with pronounced benefits under data scarcity.
notes: High — theoretical improvement to diffusion policy robustness, relevant to understanding and improving diffusion-based robot policies

title: Latent Policy Barrier: Learning Robust Visuomotor Policies by Staying In-Distribution
authors: Zhanyi Sun, Shuran Song
matched_interests: Verification of visuomotor policies|Latent-space safety analysis|Diffusion models|Control Barrier Functions (CBFs)
headline: CBF-inspired latent barriers keep diffusion visuomotor policies in-distribution for robust manipulation
summary: Proposes Latent Policy Barrier inspired by CBFs, treating expert latent embeddings as implicit safety barriers. Decouples expert imitation (diffusion policy) from OOD recovery (dynamics model) and optimizes future latent states to stay within the expert distribution at inference time.
abstract: Visuomotor policies trained via behavior cloning are vulnerable to covariate shift, where small deviations from expert trajectories can compound into failure. Common strategies to mitigate this issue involve expanding the training distribution through human-in-the-loop corrections or synthetic data augmentation. However, these approaches are often labor-intensive, rely on strong task assumptions, or compromise the quality of imitation. We introduce Latent Policy Barrier, a framework for robust visuomotor policy learning. Inspired by Control Barrier Functions, LPB treats the latent embeddings of expert demonstrations as an implicit barrier separating safe, in-distribution states from unsafe, out-of-distribution (OOD) ones. Our approach decouples the role of precise expert imitation and OOD recovery into two separate modules: a base diffusion policy solely on expert data, and a dynamics model trained on both expert and suboptimal policy rollout data. At inference time, the dynamics model predicts future latent states and optimizes them to stay within the expert distribution.
